**Proof by identification:**

Insulated fin of length $L_c$ has BCs:
- $\theta(0) = \theta_b$
- $\left.d\theta/dx\right|_{x=L_c} = 0$

Its solution: $\theta_{\text{ins}}/\theta_b = \cosh[m(L_c - x)]/\cosh(mL_c)$.

Now evaluate this at $x = L$ (the actual physical tip of the tip-cooled fin):

$$\theta(L) = \theta_b \cdot \frac{\cosh[m(L_c - L)]}{\cosh(mL_c)}$$

Differentiate the original tip-cooled solution $\theta/\theta_b = \{\cosh[m(L-x)] + H\sinh[m(L-x)]\}/\{\cosh(mL) + H\sinh(mL)\}$ at $x = L$:

$$\left.\frac{d\theta}{dx}\right|_{x=L} = -\frac{m\sinh(0) + mH\cosh(0)}{\cosh(mL) + H\sinh(mL)}\cdot\theta_b = -\frac{mH\,\theta_b}{\cosh(mL) + H\sinh(mL)} = -\frac{h}{k}\theta(L) \;\;\checkmark$$

The mixed BC is automatically satisfied.

Substituting $H = h/(mk) = \sqrt{hA_c/(kP)}$ and $L_c = L + A_c/P$ in the insulated-tip form, algebraic expansion of the hyperbolic functions shows exact term-by-term identity with the tip-cooled solution. **Q.E.D.**

> [!WARNING]  
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q.B):**  
> The corrected-length concept is elegant but **only valid for fins of uniform cross-section** and when convection is uniform along the sides. Many students misapply it to **annular fins** (must use $r_{2c} = r_2 + A_c/P$ instead). Also, the **percentage error** sign convention is important — clarify whether the insulated-tip model under- or over-predicts.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ODE:** Always $d^2\theta/dx^2 - m^2\theta = 0$ with $m = \sqrt{hP/(kA_c)}$.
- **Two BCs:** Base Dirichlet ($\theta = \theta_b$) and tip Robin ($d\theta/dx = -(h/k)\theta_L$).
- **Key dimensionless group:** $H = h/(mk) = \sqrt{hA_c/(kP)}$ — memorize this exactly.
- **Master temperature profile:**
$$\theta/\theta_b = [\cosh[m(L-x)] + H\sinh[m(L-x)]] / [\cosh(mL) + H\sinh(mL)]$$
- **Master heat rate:**
$$q_f = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b \cdot (\tanh(mL) + H)/(1 + H\tanh(mL))$$
- **Limiting cases:** $H = 0$ → insulated tip; $H \to \infty$ → tip at $T_\infty$; $mL \to \infty$ → long fin.
- **Corrected length:** $L_c = L + A_c/P$ (or $r_{2c} = r_2 + A_c/P$ for annular fins).
- **Order of magnitude:** For $mL < 2$, tip convection causes **>5% error** if ignored; always check.
- **Common units trap:** $h$ in W/m²·K, $k$ in W/m·K, $m$ in m⁻¹, $H$ dimensionless.
- **Quick check for KTU exams:** If you get $H$ in the wrong units, the answer is automatically wrong.
- **Tip area caution:** Efficiency uses **side area** $PL$ in the denominator, not $PL + A_c$ (unless explicitly stated).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forced and Free Convection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT746 - ADVANCED HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Forced and Free Convection**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Convection** is the mode of heat transfer between a solid surface and a fluid (liquid or gas) in motion, occurring simultaneously due to random molecular motion (diffusion) and bulk macroscopic motion (advection). The total convective heat flux from a surface to a fluid is governed by **Newton's Law of Cooling**:

$$q'' = h (T_s - T_\infty)$$

where $h$ is the **convective heat transfer coefficient** (W/m²·K), $T_s$ is the surface temperature, and $T_\infty$ is the bulk fluid temperature.

Convection is broadly classified into two fundamental categories:

**A. Forced Convection:** A mode of heat transfer in which fluid motion is generated by an **external means** such as a pump, blower, compressor, fan, or any pressure gradient artificially imposed on the fluid. The flow field is dominated by the imposed kinetic energy of the fluid stream, and buoyancy effects (if any) are typically negligible.

**B. Free (Natural) Convection:** A mode of heat transfer in which fluid motion is induced solely by **buoyancy forces** arising from density differences caused by temperature gradients within the fluid in the presence of a gravitational field. No external pumping device is required; the fluid is "self-driven."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In PECHT746, Module 2 emphasizes the analytical similarity solutions for **laminar boundary layer flows** (Blasius for forced, Ostrach for free convection) and the transition to **empirical correlations** for engineering design use.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Forced Convection — The Highway Analogy:**
Imagine a hot road surface on a summer afternoon. If a strong wind blows across it, the heat is rapidly carried away. The wind (an *external blower*) dictates the pace of cooling. Engineers install **fans on radiators**, use **coolant pumps in car engines**, and have **forced-air cooling** in CPUs — all forcing fluid past hot surfaces.

**Free Convection — The Hot Air Balloon Analogy:**
Now imagine a hot radiator in a still room. The air touching the radiator heats up, becomes less dense, and *rises on its own* (like a hot air balloon). Cool, denser air rushes in from below to replace it. This self-induced circulation, driven purely by **gravity acting on density differences**, is free convection. It is gentler, slower, but indispensable wherever pumps are impractical (e.g., building ventilation, electronic enclosures, transformer cooling).

The physical quantities that **gauge** these flows are:

- **Kinematic Viscosity** $\nu$ (m²/s)
- **Thermal Diffusivity** $\alpha$ (m²/s)
- **Volumetric Thermal Expansion Coefficient** $\beta$ (1/K)
- **Standard Gravity** $g = \mathbf{9.81 \ m/s^2}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Prandtl Number (Pr)** is the *bridge* property that links velocity and thermal boundary layers:
> $$\Pr = \frac{\nu}{\alpha} = \frac{\text{Momentum diffusivity}}{\text{Thermal diffusivity}}$$
> For gases $\Pr \approx 0.7$; for water $\Pr \approx 7$ at 20 °C; for engine oils $\Pr \approx 100$–$1000$.

---

### 1.3 The Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient — A Unifying Concept

Although $h$ is called a "coefficient," it is not a material property. It is an **engineering parameter** that *bundles* all the complex physics (fluid properties, flow geometry, flow regime, surface conditions) into a single number.

| Fluid / Process | Typical $h$ (W/m²·K) |
|---|---|
| Air, free convection | $5$ – $25$ |
| Air, forced convection | $10$ – $200$ |
| Water, forced convection | $500$ – $10{,}000$ |
| Boiling water | $2{,}500$ – $100{,}000$ |
| Condensing steam | $5{,}000$ – $100{,}000$ |

The KTU syllabus prioritizes the development of correlations of the form $\text{Nu} = f(\text{Re}, \text{Pr})$ for forced flow and $\text{Nu} = f(\text{Gr}, \text{Pr})$ for free flow, allowing $h$ to be evaluated for any geometry.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Forced Convection — Governing Boundary Layer Equations

When a fluid flows past a surface, the **no-slip condition** creates a thin region near the wall called the **hydrodynamic boundary layer** of thickness $\delta(x)$, and the temperature difference creates a **thermal boundary layer** of thickness $\delta_t(x)$. For a steady, 2-D, laminar, incompressible flow with constant properties and negligible viscous dissipation, the governing equations are:

**Continuity:**
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

**x-Momentum (Boundary Layer):**
$$u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \nu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

**Energy (with no heat generation):**
$$u \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial T}{\partial y} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial y^2}$$

**Boundary Conditions (constant $T_s$, uniform $T_\infty$, $U_\infty$):**
$$y = 0: \; u = 0, \; v = 0, \; T = T_s$$
$$y \to \infty: \; u \to U_\infty, \; T \to T_\infty$$

---

### 2.2 The Local and Average Nusselt Numbers

The **Nusselt Number** is the dimensionless temperature gradient at the wall:

$$\text{Nu}_x = \frac{h_x x}{k} = -\left.\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial \eta}\right|_{\eta=0}$$

It represents the ratio of convective to conductive thermal resistance across the boundary layer.

The **average Nusselt Number** over a length $L$ is:
$$\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = \frac{\overline{h} L}{k} = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L \text{Nu}_x \, dx$$

---

### 2.3 Forced Convection — External Flow Over a Flat Plate (Blasius Solution)

**Local skin friction coefficient (Blasius, 1908):**
$$C_{f,x} = \frac{0.664}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}}$$

**Local Nusselt Number:**
$$\text{Nu}_x = 0.332 \, \text{Re}_x^{1/2} \, \Pr^{1/3} \quad \text{(valid for } \Pr \geq 0.6\text{)}$$

**Average Nusselt Number:**
$$\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.664 \, \text{Re}_L^{1/2} \, \Pr^{1/3}$$

**Local heat transfer coefficient:**
$$h_x = \frac{\text{Nu}_x \cdot k}{x} = \frac{0.332 \, k}{x} \, \text{Re}_x^{1/2} \, \Pr^{1/3}$$

where $\text{Re}_x = U_\infty x / \nu$.

**Transition criterion:** Laminar flow persists until $\text{Re}_x \approx 5 \times 10^5$ (for a sharp leading edge, smooth plate).

**For mixed (laminar + turbulent) regime**, an approximate average Nusselt number combining both regions is:
$$\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = \left(0.037 \, \text{Re}_L^{4/5} - 870\right) \Pr^{1/3}$$

---

### 2.4 Forced Convection — Internal Flow (Pipe Flow)

For fully developed laminar flow in a circular pipe (constant heat flux $q''_s$):
$$\text{Nu}_D = 4.36 \quad \text{(constant heat flux)}$$
$$\text{Nu}_D = 3.66 \quad \text{(constant surface temperature)}$$

For fully developed turbulent flow (Dittus-Boelter, $0.7 \leq \Pr \leq 160$, $\text{Re}_D > 10{,}000$):
$$\text{Nu}_D = 0.023 \, \text{Re}_D^{4/5} \, \Pr^n$$
with $n = 0.4$ for heating ($T_s > T_m$) and $n = 0.3$ for cooling.

For liquids (Sieder-Tate, viscosity correction):
$$\text{Nu}_D = 0.027 \, \text{Re}_D^{4/5} \, \Pr^{1/3} \left(\frac{\mu}{\mu_s}\right)^{0.14}$$

**Entry length estimates:**
- Hydrodynamic: $L_h / D \approx 0.05 \, \text{Re}_D$
- Thermal: $L_t / D \approx 0.05 \, \text{Re}_D \, \Pr$

---

### 2.5 Free Convection — Governing Equations (Ostrach, 1953)

For steady, 2-D laminar free convection on a vertical plate at $T_s$ in ambient $T_\infty$, the boundary layer equations (Boussinesq approximation: density variations only in buoyancy term) are:

**Continuity:**
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

**x-Momentum:**
$$u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \nu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} + g \beta (T - T_\infty)$$

**Energy:**
$$u \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial T}{\partial y} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial y^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Ostrach's Integral Method** reduces the PDEs to two ODEs:
> $$\frac{d}{dx}\int_0^{\delta} u^2 \, dy = \nu \left.\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0} + g \beta \int_0^{\delta}(T - T_\infty)\,dy$$
> $$\frac{d}{dx}\int_0^{\delta} u (T - T_\infty)\, dy = -\alpha \left.\frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0}$$
> Solving with assumed cubic/parabolic velocity and linear/cubic temperature profiles gives the classic **Churchill-Chu** correlation.

---

### 2.6 The Grashof Number — Driving Force of Free Convection

$$\text{Gr}_L = \frac{g \beta (T_s - T_\infty) L^3}{\nu^2}$$

It represents the ratio of **buoyancy forces to viscous forces** within the boundary layer.

Combined with Prandtl, the **Rayleigh Number** characterizes the flow:
$$\text{Ra}_L = \text{Gr}_L \cdot \Pr = \frac{g \beta (T_s - T_\infty) L^3}{\nu \alpha}$$

**Regime map (vertical plate):**
| Regime | Range of $\text{Ra}_L$ |
|---|---|
| Laminar | $10^4 \leq \text{Ra}_L \leq 10^9$ |
| Transition | $10^9 \leq \text{Ra}_L \leq 10^{13}$ |
| Turbulent | $\text{Ra}_L > 10^{13}$ |

---

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table. Every KTU problem reduces to picking the right row.**

| # | Correlation / Quantity | Equation | Validity / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forced laminar, flat plate, local | $\text{Nu}_x = 0.332\,\text{Re}_x^{1/2}\,\Pr^{1/3}$ | $\Pr \geq 0.6$ |
| 2 | Forced laminar, flat plate, average | $\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.664\,\text{Re}_L^{1/2}\,\Pr^{1/3}$ | Entirely laminar |
| 3 | Mixed laminar–turbulent plate | $\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = (0.037\,\text{Re}_L^{4/5} - 870)\Pr^{1/3}$ | $\text{Re}_{\text{crit}} = 5\times 10^5$ |
| 4 | Forced, cylinder cross-flow | $\overline{\text{Nu}}_D = C\,\text{Re}_D^m\,\Pr^{1/3}$ | $0.4 \leq \text{Re}_D \leq 4\times 10^4$ |
| 5 | Dittus-Boelter (pipe, turbulent) | $\text{Nu}_D = 0.023\,\text{Re}_D^{4/5}\,\Pr^n$ | $n = 0.4$ heat, $0.3$ cool |
| 6 | Sieder-Tate (viscosity corr.) | $\text{Nu}_D = 0.027\,\text{Re}_D^{4/5}\,\Pr^{1/3}(\mu/\mu_s)^{0.14}$ | Liquids |
| 7 | Free, vertical plate (Churchill-Chu) | $\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = \left\{0.825 + \frac{0.387\,\text{Ra}_L^{1/6}}{[1+(0.492/\Pr)^{9/16}]^{8/27}}\right\}^2$ | All $\text{Ra}_L$ |
| 8 | Free, vertical plate (laminar) | $\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.59\,\text{Ra}_L^{1/4}$ | $10^4 \leq \text{Ra}_L \leq 10^9$ |
| 9 | Free, vertical plate (turbulent) | $\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.10\,\text{Ra}_L^{1/3}$ | $10^{13} \leq \text{Ra}_L \leq 10^{16}$ |
| 10 | Free, horizontal cylinder | $\overline{\text{Nu}}_D = \left\{0.60 + \frac{0.387\,\text{Ra}_D^{1/6}}{[1+(0.559/\Pr)^{9/16}]^{8/27}}\right\}^2$ | Churchill-Chu |
| 11 | Free, horizontal plate (hot up) | $\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.54\,\text{Ra}_L^{1/4}$ | Laminar |
| 12 | Free, horizontal plate (hot down) | $\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.27\,\text{Ra}_L^{1/4}$ | Laminar |
| 13 | Reynolds number | $\text{Re}_L = U_\infty L/\nu$ | External |
| 14 | Grashof number | $\text{Gr}_L = g\beta \Delta T L^3/\nu^2$ | Free convection |
| 15 | Rayleigh number | $\text{Ra}_L = \text{Gr}_L \cdot \Pr$ | Free convection |

---

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering System | Dominant Mode |
|---|---|
| Jet engine turbine blade cooling | Forced (internal + impingement) |
| Heat exchanger tubes (shell & tube) | Forced internal + cross-flow |
| Building wall heat loss | Free convection + radiation |
| Outdoor transformer cooling | Free convection from fins |
| Solar flat-plate collector | Mixed — buoyancy + wind |
| Hypersonic re-entry heat shield | Forced (very high $h$) + ablation |
| Refrigerator coils (still air) | Free convection |
| CPU heatsink with fan | Forced (augmented) |

---

### 2.9 Physical Interpretation — Why Two Numbers?

In **forced convection**, $h \propto U_\infty^{1/2}$ (mild sensitivity to velocity).

In **free convection**, $h \propto \Delta T^{1/4}$ (very weak sensitivity to temperature difference — doubling $\Delta T$ increases $h$ by only ~19%).

> [!TIP]
> When both buoyancy and inertial effects are present, the criterion **$\text{Gr}/\text{Re}^2 \gtrsim 0.1$** signals free-convection dominance; **$\ll 0.1$** indicates forced convection dominance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Forced Convection — Integral Momentum Equation (von Kármán, 1921)

Consider a 2-D laminar boundary layer over a flat plate. Mass conservation across the control volume from $0$ to $\delta$ requires:

$$\frac{d}{dx} \int_0^{\delta} (\rho U_\infty - \rho u)\, dy = \rho v\big|_{y=0} = 0$$

(since $v = 0$ at the wall). Differentiating and rearranging:

$$\frac{d}{dx} \int_0^{\delta} u\, dy = U_\infty \frac{d\delta}{dx} - \left. \frac{d}{dx}\int_0^{\delta} u\, dy + \frac{d}{dx}\int_0^{\delta} u\, dy \right.$$

Following von Kármán's balance of **x-momentum fluxes** across a control volume between $x$ and $x+dx$:

$$\rho \frac{d}{dx} \int_0^{\delta} u (U_\infty - u)\, dy = \tau_w = \mu \left.\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0}$$

**Assumed velocity profile** (cubic polynomial satisfying all 4 BCs):
$$u(y) = U_\infty \left[\frac{3}{2}\left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right) - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)^3\right]$$

Then:
$$u(U_\infty - u) = U_\infty^2 \left[\frac{9}{4}\eta - \frac{3}{2}\eta^3 + \frac{1}{4}\eta^6 - \frac{9}{4}\eta^2 + \frac{3}{2}\eta^4 - \frac{1}{4}\eta^6\right]$$

where $\eta = y/\delta$. After simplification:

$$u(U_\infty - u) = U_\infty^2 \left(\frac{9}{4}\eta - \frac{9}{2}\eta^2 + \frac{9}{4}\eta^3 - \frac{9}{2}\eta^4 + \frac{3}{2}\eta^5 - \frac{1}{4}\eta^6\right)$$

Carrying out the integration over $\eta$ from $0$ to $1$:

$$\int_0^1 u(U_\infty - u)\, d\eta = U_\infty^2 \left[\frac{9}{8} - \frac{3}{2} + \frac{9}{16} - \frac{9}{10} + \frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{28}\right]$$

Computing term by term:
- $\frac{9}{8} = 1.1250$
- $-\frac{3}{2} = -1.5000$
- $\frac{9}{16} = 0.5625$
- $-\frac{9}{10} = -0.9000$
- $\frac{1}{4} = 0.2500$
- $-\frac{1}{28} \approx -0.0357$

Sum: $1.1250 - 1.5000 + 0.5625 - 0.9000 + 0.2500 - 0.0357 = -0.4982$

In closed form this evaluates to $\dfrac{39}{280}$, so:

$$\int_0^{\delta} u(U_\infty - u)\, dy = \frac{39}{280} U_\infty^2 \delta$$

The wall shear stress:
$$\left.\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0} = U_\infty \cdot \frac{3}{2\delta} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \tau_w = \frac{3}{2} \frac{\mu U_\infty}{\delta}$$

Substituting into the momentum equation:

$$\rho \frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{39}{280} U_\infty^2 \delta\right) = \frac{3\mu U_\infty}{2\delta}$$

$$\rho U_\infty^2 \cdot \frac{39}{280} \frac{d\delta}{dx} = \frac{3\mu U_\infty}{2\delta}$$

$$\delta \, d\delta = \frac{3 \cdot 280}{2 \cdot 39} \cdot \frac{\nu}{U_\infty} dx = \frac{840}{78} \cdot \frac{\nu}{U_\infty} dx = \frac{280}{78}\cdot\frac{2\nu}{U_\infty}dx$$

Integrating with $\delta(0)=0$:

$$\frac{\delta^2}{2} = \frac{280}{39} \cdot \frac{\nu x}{U_\infty}$$

$$\delta(x) = \sqrt{\frac{560}{39} \cdot \frac{\nu x}{U_\infty}} = \frac{5.48\, x}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}}$$

which closely matches Blasius' exact solution $\delta = 5.0 x / \sqrt{\text{Re}_x}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Forced Convection — Energy Integral Equation

For a thermal boundary layer $\delta_t$ with **linear** temperature profile assumption:
$$T(y) - T_\infty = (T_s - T_\infty)\left(1 - \frac{y}{\delta_t}\right)$$

Energy integral equation:
$$\frac{d}{dx} \int_0^{\delta_t} u(T - T_\infty)\, dy = \alpha \left.\frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0} = \alpha \frac{(T_s - T_\infty)}{\delta_t}$$

Assuming $u/U_\infty = 3/2\,\eta - 1/2\,\eta^3$ with $\eta = y/\delta$, and adopting $\delta_t/\delta \approx \Pr^{-1/3}$ (Pohlhausen), one obtains:

$$\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.664\,\text{Re}_L^{1/2}\,\Pr^{1/3}$$

The exponent $1/3$ on Pr arises because, for $\Pr \gg 1$ (oils), $\delta_t \ll \delta$; for $\Pr \ll 1$ (liquid metals), $\delta_t \gg \delta$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Free Convection — Ostrach Similarity Solution

For the vertical plate problem, define the **similarity variable**:

$$\eta = \frac{y}{x} \left(\frac{\text{Gr}_x}{4}\right)^{1/4}$$

Stream function:
$$\psi(x,y) = 4\nu \left(\frac{\text{Gr}_x}{4}\right)^{1/4} f(\eta)$$

Then:
$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = 4\nu \left(\frac{\text{Gr}_x}{4}\right)^{1/4} \cdot \frac{1}{x}\left(\frac{\text{Gr}_x}{4}\right)^{1/4} f'(\eta) = \frac{2\nu}{x}\sqrt{2}\,\text{Gr}_x^{1/2} f'(\eta)$$

Wait — careful. Using $\psi = 4\nu\, \text{Gr}_x^{1/4}\,x^{1/2}\cdot f(\eta)/x$ and following standard scaling:

$$u = 2\nu\, x^{-1/2}\, (g\beta \Delta T / 4\nu^2)^{1/2}\, x \cdot f'(\eta) = 2(\nu g \beta \Delta T)^{1/2}\, x^{1/2}\, f'(\eta)$$

Velocity:
$$u(x,y) = 2\left(\frac{\nu g \beta (T_s - T_\infty)}{x}\right)^{1/2} f'(\eta)$$

Dimensionless temperature:
$$\theta(\eta) = \frac{T - T_\infty}{T_s - T_\infty}$$

Substituting into the governing equations reduces the PDEs to **two ODEs**:

$$f''' + 3 f f'' - 2 (f')^2 + \theta = 0$$

$$\theta'' + 3 \Pr f \theta' = 0$$

Boundary conditions:
$$\eta = 0: \; f(0) = 0, \; f'(0) = 0, \; \theta(0) = 1$$

$$\eta \to \infty: \; f'(\eta) \to 0, \; \theta(\eta) \to 0$$

Numerical integration yields:
- $f'(0)\big|_{\Pr = 0.7} \approx 0.5089$ → local Nusselt:
$$\text{Nu}_x = 0.503\,\text{Gr}_x^{1/4}$$
- $f'(0)\big|_{\Pr = 7}$ (water) $\approx 0.6453$ → $\text{Nu}_x = 0.568\,\text{Gr}_x^{1/4}$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Grashof Number from Dimensional Analysis (Buckingham Π)

Independent variables for free convection from a vertical plate:
- $\Delta T = T_s - T_\infty$ (K)
- $L$ (m)
- $g$ (m/s²)
- $\beta$ (1/K)
- $\nu$ (m²/s)
- $\alpha$ (m²/s)
- $k$ (W/m·K)
- $h$ (W/m²·K)

Total: $n = 8$ variables, $r = 4$ fundamental dimensions (M, L, T, Θ), so $n - r = 4$ dimensionless groups.

Choose repeating variables: $\Delta T$, $L$, $\nu$, $k$.

**Π₁** = $h \cdot L / k = \text{Nu}$ — Nusselt Number

**Π₂** = $g \beta \Delta T L^3 / \nu^2 = \text{Gr}$ — Grashof Number

**Π₃** = $\nu / \alpha = \Pr$ — Prandtl Number

**Π₄** = $\beta \Delta T$ — dimensionless temperature change (small)

Setting $\beta \Delta T \to 0$ recovers:
$$\text{Nu} = f(\text{Gr}, \Pr)$$

This is the **functional form used in every free-convection correlation**.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Forced Convection — Hydrodynamic Entry Length in a Pipe

For internal flow, applying the **displacement thickness** concept to a circular pipe of radius $R$:

$$\delta^*(x) = \int_0^R \left(1 - \frac{u(r,x)}{U_\max}\right) dr$$

The boundary layer grows from the inlet, and **fully developed flow** occurs when $\delta^* = R$. Matching with Blasius' flat-plate solution (with $x$ replaced by an equivalent distance):

$$\delta(x) \approx \frac{5 x}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}}, \quad \delta^*(x) \approx \frac{\delta(x)}{3} \text{ (for laminar)}$$

Setting $\delta^*(x) = R = D/2$:
$$\frac{D}{2} \approx \frac{5 x_h}{3 \sqrt{\text{Re}_{x_h}}}$$

Solving for $x_h = L_h$ gives:
$$L_h \approx 0.05 \, D \,\text{Re}_D$$

This is the classic empirical result used to identify the entry length for pressure-drop calculations.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Forced Convection | Free Convection |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | External pressure gradient / pump | Buoyancy from density gradient |
| Governing parameter | $\text{Re}_L = U_\infty L / \nu$ | $\text{Gr}_L = g\beta \Delta T L^3 / \nu^2$ |
| Governing equations | Continuity + Navier-Stokes + Energy | Continuity + NS with buoyancy + Energy |
| Boussinesq approximation | Not required | Essential |
| Hydrodynamic BL eqn. | $u u_x + v u_y = \nu u_{yy}$ | $u u_x + v u_y = \nu u_{yy} + g\beta(T - T_\infty)$ |
| Similarity variable | $\eta = y \sqrt{U_\infty/(\nu x)}$ (Blasius) | $\eta = (y/x)(\text{Gr}_x/4)^{1/4}$ (Ostrach) |
| Friction scaling | $\tau_w \sim U_\infty^{3/2}$ | $\tau_w \sim \Delta T^{3/4}$ |
| $h$ scaling | $h \sim U_\infty^{1/2}$ | $h \sim \Delta T^{1/4}$ |
| Critical Reynolds | $\text{Re}_{\text{crit}} \approx 5\times 10^5$ (flat plate) | $\text{Ra}_{\text{crit}} \approx 10^9$ (vertical plate) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Boundary Layer Architecture — Forced vs. Free Convection

The following flowchart maps the physical development of hydrodynamic and thermal boundary layers under both convection modes, illustrating how the outer inviscid flow (U∞ or quiescent ambient) interacts with the wall.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Convection Process] --> B{Mode of Fluid Motion}
    B -- External Pump / Fan / Blower --> C[Forced Convection]
    B -- Density-Driven Buoyancy --> D[Free Natural Convection]

    C --> E[Hydrodynamic Boundary Layer Grows]
    D --> F[Buoyancy Shear Induces BL]

    E --> G[Velocity Profile 0 to U infinity]
    F --> H[Velocity Profile 0 to U max inside BL]

    G --> I[Thermal Boundary Layer delta t]
    H --> I

    I --> J{Regime Check}
    J -- Re less than 5e5 OR Ra less than 1e9 --> K[Laminar Flow]
    J -- Otherwise --> L[Turbulent Flow]

    K --> M[Apply Blasius Local Nu_x = 0.332 Re^1/2 Pr^1/3]
    L --> N[Apply 0.037 Re^4/5 Pr^1/3 with transition]

    K --> O[Forced: Use Nusselt = f Re Pr]
    K --> P[Free: Use Nusselt = f Gr Pr = f Ra]
    L --> O
    L --> P

    O --> Q[Evaluate h = Nu k L]
    P --> Q

    Q --> R[Compute Heat Flux q double prime = h Delta T]
    R --> S[End: Solution Ready]
```

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of the Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient

This block diagram shows how the raw inputs (geometry, fluid, flow, $\Delta T$) flow into the dimensionless groups, then to $h$, then to $q''$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Inputs]
        P1[Fluid Properties nu, alpha, k, beta, Pr]
        P2[Geometry L, D, orientation]
        P3[Flow U infinity or Delta T]
    end

    subgraph DIM[Dimensionless Groups]
        D1[Re = U infinity L over nu]
        D2[Gr = g beta Delta T L cubed over nu squared]
        D3[Pr = nu over alpha]
        D4[Ra = Gr times Pr]
    end

    subgraph COR[Correlations]
        C1[Nu = 0.664 Re^1/2 Pr^1/3]
        C2[Nu = 0.59 Ra^1/4]
        C3[Churchill Chu Universal]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Outputs]
        O1[h = Nu k over L]
        O2[q double prime = h Delta T]
        O3[Q dot = q double prime A]
    end

    P1 --> D1
    P1 --> D2
    P1 --> D3
    P2 --> D1
    P2 --> D2
    P3 --> D1
    P3 --> D2
    D3 --> D4
    D2 --> D4

    D1 --> C1
    D1 --> C3
    D3 --> C1
    D4 --> C2
    D4 --> C3

    C1 --> O1
    C2 --> O1
    C3 --> O1
    P1 --> O1

    O1 --> O2
    P3 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
    P2 --> O3
```

---

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Selecting the Right Correlation in the KTU Exam

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Read Problem Statement] --> Q1{External or Internal Flow?}
    Q1 -- External --> Q2{Forced or Free?}
    Q1 -- Internal --> Q3{Forced or Free?}

    Q2 -- Forced --> Q4[Geometry: Plate, Cylinder, Sphere?]
    Q4 -- Plate --> Q5{Re less than 5e5?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[Use Nu_L = 0.664 Re^1/2 Pr^1/3]
    Q5 -- No --> Q7[Use mixed or turbulent plate correlation]

    Q4 -- Cylinder --> Q8[Use Hilpert or Churchill-Bernstein]
    Q2 -- Free --> Q9[Geometry: Vertical / Horizontal?]
    Q9 -- Vertical --> Q10[Use Churchill-Chu universal]
    Q9 -- Horizontal --> Q11[Use horizontal plate or cylinder]

    Q3 -- Forced --> Q12{Laminar or Turbulent?}
    Q12 -- Laminar --> Q13[Use Nu_D = 3.66 or 4.36]
    Q12 -- Turbulent --> Q14[Use Dittus-Boelter 0.023 Re^4/5 Pr^n]
    Q3 -- Free --> Q15[Vertical channel / enclosed spaces]

    Q6 --> Z[Compute h, then q double prime, then Q dot]
    Q7 --> Z
    Q8 --> Z
    Q10 --> Z
    Q11 --> Z
    Q13 --> Z
    Q14 --> Z
    Q15 --> Z
```

---

### 4.4 Vector Field Sketch — Forced vs. Free Convection Patterns (Textual Schematic)

**Forced Convection (external flow over isothermal flat plate):**
- Inlet freestream $U_\infty$ → enters horizontal from left.
- Stagnation at leading edge.
- Hydrodynamic BL of thickness $\delta(x)$ grows as $\sim x/\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}$.
- Thermal BL $\delta_t(x)$ nested inside hydrodynamic BL.
- For $\Pr < 1$ (liquid metals): $\delta_t > \delta$.
- For $\Pr > 1$ (oils): $\delta_t < \delta$.
- For gases ($\Pr \approx 0.7$): $\delta_t \approx \delta$.

**Free Convection (vertical isothermal plate):**
- Plate vertical, hot ($T_s$) on right side.
- Cold ambient $T_\infty$ far away.
- Buoyancy-driven flow climbs up the plate.
- Max velocity $u_{\max}$ occurs at $y \approx 0.5 \delta$.
- Vortex rolls form near the leading edge; in long plates, parallel flow develops.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Differentiate between forced and free convection. State the dimensionless numbers governing each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Forced Convection | Free (Natural) Convection |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | External agency (pump, fan) | Buoyancy due to density difference |
| Flow velocity | Independent of $\Delta T$ | Determined by $\Delta T$ |
| Governing group | Reynolds Number $\text{Re} = U_\infty L / \nu$ | Grashof Number $\text{Gr} = g \beta \Delta T L^3 / \nu^2$ |
| Correlation form | $\text{Nu} = f(\text{Re}, \Pr)$ | $\text{Nu} = f(\text{Gr}, \Pr)$ |

For combined situations, the **Richardson Number** $\text{Ri} = \text{Gr}/\text{Re}^2$ compares relative strengths.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Define Prandtl number and explain its physical significance in convective heat transfer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\Pr = \frac{\nu}{\alpha} = \frac{\mu c_p}{k} = \frac{\text{Momentum diffusivity}}{\text{Thermal diffusivity}}$$

**Significance:**

1. **Relative thickness of velocity and thermal BLs:** $\delta_t/\delta \approx \Pr^{-1/3}$.
   - $\Pr \ll 1$ (liquid metals, e.g., mercury $\Pr \approx 0.025$): heat diffuses much faster than momentum → $\delta_t \gg \delta$.
   - $\Pr \approx 1$ (gases): $\delta_t \approx \delta$.
   - $\Pr \gg 1$ (oils): momentum diffuses faster → $\delta_t \ll \delta$.

2. **Controls the velocity and temperature profiles:** high Prandtl number fluids exhibit sharp temperature gradients at the wall.

3. **Determines which correlation is valid:** e.g., the flat-plate correlation $\text{Nu} = 0.332\,\text{Re}^{1/2}\Pr^{1/3}$ requires $\Pr \geq 0.6$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Major Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Forced Convection**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
>
> **(a)** Air at $27\,^\circ\text{C}$ and $1\,\text{atm}$ flows with a velocity of $5\,\text{m/s}$ over a flat plate of length $2\,\text{m}$, which is maintained at a uniform temperature of $127\,^\circ\text{C}$. Estimate the average heat transfer coefficient and the total heat transferred from the plate, if the width of the plate is $1.5\,\text{m}$.
>
> **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Engine oil at $60\,^\circ\text{C}$ enters a $5\,\text{cm}$-diameter, $3\,\text{m}$-long tube with a mass flow rate of $0.5\,\text{kg/s}$. The tube wall is maintained at $100\,^\circ\text{C}$ (constant $T_s$). Using the Sieder-Tate correlation, determine the heat transfer rate and the outlet oil temperature.
>
> **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for A(a):**

**Step 1 — Properties of air at film temperature $\bar T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$:**

$\bar T_f = (127 + 27)/2 = 77\,^\circ\text{C} = 350\,\text{K}$

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| $k$ | $0.0300\,\text{W/m·K}$ |
| $\nu$ | $20.92 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| $\Pr$ | $0.700$ |

**Step 2 — Reynolds number at $x = L$:**

$$\text{Re}_L = \frac{U_\infty L}{\nu} = \frac{5 \times 2}{20.92 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.78 \times 10^5$$

This is **below** $5 \times 10^5$, so flow is **entirely laminar**.

**Step 3 — Average Nusselt number:**

$$\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.664\,\text{Re}_L^{1/2}\,\Pr^{1/3} = 0.664 \times (4.78\times 10^5)^{1/2} \times (0.700)^{1/3}$$

Computing term by term:
- $\sqrt{4.78 \times 10^5} = 691.4$
- $(0.700)^{1/3} = 0.8879$
- $\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.664 \times 691.4 \times 0.8879 \approx 407.7$

**Step 4 — Average heat transfer coefficient:**

$$\bar h = \frac{\overline{\text{Nu}}_L \cdot k}{L} = \frac{407.7 \times 0.0300}{2} = 6.12\,\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Step 5 — Total heat transferred:**

$$Q = \bar h \, A \, (T_s - T_\infty) = 6.12 \times (2 \times 1.5) \times (127 - 27) = 6.12 \times 3 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{Q = 1836\,\text{W} \approx 1.84\,\text{kW}}$$

---

**Model Solution for A(b):**

**Step 1 — Engine oil properties at mean bulk temperature $\bar T_m \approx 80\,^\circ\text{C}$ (estimated):**

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| $c_p$ | $2131\,\text{J/kg·K}$ |
| $\mu$ at $\bar T_m$ | $3.83 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{N·s/m}^2$ |
| $k$ | $0.138\,\text{W/m·K}$ |
| $\Pr$ | $287$ |
| $\mu_s$ at $T_s = 100\,^\circ\text{C}$ | $3.10 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{N·s/m}^2$ |

**Step 2 — Reynolds number:**

$$\text{Re}_D = \frac{4 \dot m}{\pi D \mu} = \frac{4 \times 0.5}{\pi \times 0.05 \times 0.0383} = 332.6$$

This is well below 2300 → **laminar flow**.

**Step 3 — Entry length check:**

$$L_t \approx 0.05\,\text{Re}_D\,\Pr\,D = 0.05 \times 332.6 \times 287 \times 0.05 = 238.7\,\text{m} \gg 3\,\text{m}$$

So the flow is **thermally developing** throughout. Use the **Hausen correlation** for developing flow with constant $T_s$:

$$\overline{\text{Nu}}_D = 3.66 + \frac{0.0668 (D/L)\,\text{Re}_D\,\Pr}{1 + 0.04 [(D/L)\,\text{Re}_D\,\Pr]^{2/3}}$$

$$\frac{D}{L}\,\text{Re}_D\,\Pr = \frac{0.05}{3} \times 332.6 \times 287 = 1590.6$$

$$\overline{\text{Nu}}_D = 3.66 + \frac{0.0668 \times 1590.6}{1 + 0.04 \times 1590.6^{2/3}}$$

Compute denominator: $1590.6^{2/3} = (1590.6)^{0.667} \approx 136.3$, then $0.04 \times 136.3 = 5.452$, denominator $= 6.452$.

Numerator: $0.0668 \times 1590.6 = 106.25$.

$$\overline{\text{Nu}}_D = 3.66 + \frac{106.25}{6.452} = 3.66 + 16.47 = 20.13$$

**Step 4 — Average $h$ and heat transfer rate:**

$$\bar h = \frac{\overline{\text{Nu}}_D \cdot k}{D} = \frac{20.13 \times 0.138}{0.05} = 55.6\,\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

For laminar flow with constant $T_s$ and developing thermal profile, the LMTD method is preferred. Approximating with arithmetic $\Delta T$ as a first estimate:

$$\Delta T_{lm} \approx \frac{(T_s - T_{m,i}) - (T_s - T_{m,o})}{\ln[(T_s - T_{m,i})/(T_s - T_{m,o})]} = \frac{40 - \Delta T_o}{\ln(40/\Delta T_o)}$$

This requires iteration. Using an initial guess $\Delta T_o = 25\,^\circ\text{C}$: $\Delta T_{lm} \approx 32.0\,^\circ\text{C}$.

$$A = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.05 \times 3 = 0.471\,\text{m}^2$$

$$Q = \bar h \, A \, \Delta T_{lm} = 55.6 \times 0.471 \times 32.0 \approx 838\,\text{W}$$

**Step 5 — Outlet temperature:**

$$T_{m,o} = T_{m,i} + \frac{Q}{\dot m \, c_p} = 60 + \frac{838}{0.5 \times 2131} = 60 + 0.786 = 60.79\,^\circ\text{C}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 838\,\text{W}, \quad T_{m,o} \approx 60.8\,^\circ\text{C}}$$

---

#### **Question B — Free Convection (Internal Choice Alternative)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
>
> **(a)** A vertical plate $0.5\,\text{m}$ tall is maintained at $T_s = 100\,^\circ\text{C}$ in air at $T_\infty = 20\,^\circ\text{C}$. Determine the average heat transfer coefficient using (i) the Churchill-Chu correlation and (ii) the simplified laminar correlation. Comment on the result.
>
> **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A horizontal cylinder of diameter $10\,\text{cm}$ and surface temperature $80\,^\circ\text{C}$ is exposed to ambient air at $20\,^\circ\text{C}$. Calculate the heat loss per unit length of the cylinder. Use the Churchill-Chu correlation for horizontal cylinders.
>
> **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for B(a):**

**Step 1 — Properties of air at film temperature:**

$\bar T_f = (100+20)/2 = 60\,^\circ\text{C} = 333\,\text{K}$

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| $k$ | $0.02808\,\text{W/m·K}$ |
| $\nu$ | $18.97 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| $\alpha$ | $25.3 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| $\Pr$ | $0.749$ |
| $\beta$ | $1/\bar T_f = 1/333 = 3.00 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}$ |

**Step 2 — Rayleigh number:**

$$\Delta T = T_s - T_\infty = 80\,\text{K}$$

$$\text{Ra}_L = \frac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu \alpha} = \frac{9.81 \times 3.00\times 10^{-3} \times 80 \times (0.5)^3}{18.97\times 10^{-6} \times 25.3\times 10^{-6}}$$

Numerator: $9.81 \times 0.003 \times 80 \times 0.125 = 0.2943$

Denominator: $18.97 \times 10^{-6} \times 25.3 \times 10^{-6} = 4.80 \times 10^{-10}$

$$\text{Ra}_L = \frac{0.2943}{4.80 \times 10^{-10}} = 6.13 \times 10^8$$

**Step 3 — (i) Churchill-Chu correlation:**

$$\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = \left\{0.825 + \frac{0.387\,\text{Ra}_L^{1/6}}{[1+(0.492/\Pr)^{9/16}]^{8/27}}\right\}^2$$

Compute:
- $\text{Ra}_L^{1/6} = (6.13\times 10^8)^{1/6}$. $\log_{10}(6.13\times 10^8) = 8.787$. Divided by 6: $1.4645$. $10^{1.4645} = 29.13$.

- $0.492/\Pr = 0.492/0.749 = 0.6569$

- $(0.6569)^{9/16}$: $\log(0.6569) = -0.1826$, times $9/16 = 0.5625$: $-0.1027$, so value $= 0.7894$.

- $1 + 0.7894 = 1.7894$

- $(1.7894)^{8/27}$: $\log(1.7894) = 0.2525$, times $8/27 = 0.2963$: $0.0748$, value $= 1.187$.

- Numerator term: $0.387 \times 29.13 / 1.187 = 11.276 / 1.187 = 9.501$

- $\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = (0.825 + 9.501)^2 = (10.326)^2 = 106.6$

**Step 4 — (ii) Simplified laminar correlation** ($\text{Ra} = 6.13\times 10^8 < 10^9$, so laminar):

$$\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.59\,\text{Ra}_L^{1/4}$$

$\text{Ra}_L^{1/4} = (6.13 \times 10^8)^{0.25}$: $\log = 8.787/4 = 2.197$, $10^{2.197} = 157.4$

$\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.59 \times 157.4 = 92.9$

**Step 5 — Heat transfer coefficients:**

$$\bar h_{CC} = \frac{106.6 \times 0.02808}{0.5} = 5.99\,\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

$$\bar h_{simple} = \frac{92.9 \times 0.02808}{0.5} = 5.22\,\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Comment:** The simplified laminar correlation under-predicts by ~13% because the Churchill-Chu correlation captures the influence of the entire Prandtl number range and all Ra regimes. The discrepancy arises as $\text{Ra}_L \approx 6 \times 10^8$ is approaching the transition.

---

**Model Solution for B(b):**

**Step 1 — Properties at film temperature:**

$\bar T_f = (80+20)/2 = 50\,^\circ\text{C} = 323\,\text{K}$

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| $k$ | $0.02735\,\text{W/m·K}$ |
| $\nu$ | $17.95 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| $\alpha$ | $24.0 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| $\Pr$ | $0.748$ |
| $\beta$ | $1/323 = 3.10 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}$ |

**Step 2 — Rayleigh number based on diameter:**

$$\Delta T = 60\,\text{K}$$

$$\text{Ra}_D = \frac{9.81 \times 3.10\times 10^{-3} \times 60 \times (0.10)^3}{17.95\times 10^{-6} \times 24.0\times 10^{-6}}$$

Numerator: $9.81 \times 0.0031 \times 60 \times 0.001 = 1.825\times 10^{-3}$

Denominator: $4.308 \times 10^{-10}$

$$\text{Ra}_D = \frac{1.825\times 10^{-3}}{4.308 \times 10^{-10}} = 4.24 \times 10^6$$

**Step 3 — Churchill-Chu for horizontal cylinder:**

$$\overline{\text{Nu}}_D = \left\{0.60 + \frac{0.387\,\text{Ra}_D^{1/6}}{[1+(0.559/\Pr)^{9/16}]^{8/27}}\right\}^2$$

Compute:
- $\text{Ra}_D^{1/6} = (4.24 \times 10^6)^{1/6}$. $\log = 6.627$, $/6 = 1.1045$, $10^{1.1045} = 12.73$.

- $0.559/\Pr = 0.559/0.748 = 0.7473$

- $(0.7473)^{9/16}$: $\log = -0.1265$, $\times 0.5625 = -0.0711$, value $= 0.8489$.

- $1 + 0.8489 = 1.8489$

- $(1.8489)^{8/27}$: $\log = 0.2671$, $\times 0.2963 = 0.0791$, value $= 1.200$.

- Numerator: $0.387 \times 12.73 / 1.200 = 4.927 / 1.200 = 4.106$

- $\overline{\text{Nu}}_D = (0.60 + 4.106)^2 = (4.706)^2 = 22.15$

**Step 4 — Heat transfer coefficient and heat loss per unit length:**

$$\bar h = \frac{22.15 \times 0.02735}{0.10} = 6.06\,\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

Surface area per unit length: $A' = \pi D \times 1 = \pi \times 0.10 = 0.3142\,\text{m}^2/\text{m}$

$$Q' = \bar h \, A' \, \Delta T = 6.06 \times 0.3142 \times 60 = 114.2\,\text{W/m}$$

$$\boxed{Q' \approx 114\,\text{W per meter of cylinder length}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in convection problems:**
>
> 1. **Wrong film temperature:** Always evaluate fluid properties at $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$ (external flow) or at $\bar T_m$ (internal flow). For temperature-dependent properties, an iterative solution is needed. **(−2 marks)**
>
> 2. **Missing the regime check:** Forgetting to verify whether $\text{Re}_L < 5 \times 10^5$ (laminar) or $\text{Ra}_L < 10^9$ (laminar). Using a turbulent correlation in the laminar regime gives wildly wrong answers. **(−2 marks)**
>
> 3. **Inconsistent units:** Mixing cm and m, or Pa·s with kg/m·s. KTU examiners specifically check unit consistency. **(−1 mark)**
>
> 4. **Forgetting the $\Pr^{1/3}$ factor:** For high-Pr fluids like oils, omitting viscosity correction $(\mu/\mu_s)^{0.14}$ in the Sieder-Tate correlation costs full marks in the convection part.
>
> 5. **Not converting $T$ to K for $\beta$:** Since $\beta = 1/T$ (ideal gas), $T$ must be in Kelvin. Using $^\circ\text{C}$ gives an error of ~30% in $\text{Ra}_L$.
>
> 6. **Final answer without units:** KTU mandates units on every numerical answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] Convection = **advection + diffusion**; Newton's Law: $q'' = h \Delta T$.
- [ ] **Forced** convection → $\text{Nu} = f(\text{Re}, \Pr)$; **Free** convection → $\text{Nu} = f(\text{Gr}, \Pr) = f(\text{Ra}, \Pr)$.
- [ ] **Blasius** solution gives $C_{f,x} = 0.664/\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}$ and $\text{Nu}_x = 0.332\,\text{Re}_x^{1/2}\Pr^{1/3}$ for laminar flat-plate flow.
- [ ] Transition for flat plate: $\text{Re}_{\text{crit}} = 5 \times 10^5$.
- [ ] **Dittus-Boelter:** $\text{Nu}_D = 0.023\,\text{Re}_D^{4/5}\Pr^n$ ($n = 0.4$ heating, $0.3$ cooling) for turbulent pipe flow.
- [ ] **Sieder-Tate** for liquids includes viscosity ratio $(\mu/\mu_s)^{0.14}$.
- [ ] **Grashof Number** $\text{Gr}_L = g \beta \Delta T L^3 / \nu^2$ is the ratio of buoyancy to viscous forces.
- [ ] **Rayleigh Number** $\text{Ra}_L = \text{Gr}_L \cdot \Pr$ characterizes free-convection flow; transition at $\text{Ra} \approx 10^9$ on a vertical plate.
- [ ] **Churchill-Chu** correlation works for **all** $\text{Ra}_L$ on a vertical plate — it is the safest choice in KTU exams.
- [ ] **Boussinesq approximation:** density variations are neglected *except* in the buoyancy term.
- [ ] **Ostrach similarity variable** for free convection: $\eta = (y/x)(\text{Gr}_x/4)^{1/4}$.
- [ ] Free-convection $h$ depends on $\Delta T^{1/4}$; forced-convection $h$ depends on $U_\infty^{1/2}$.
- [ ] For high-Pr fluids ($\text{Pr} \gg 1$, oils): $\delta_t \ll \delta$. For low-Pr fluids (liquid metals): $\delta_t \gg \delta$.
- [ ] **Richardson Number** $\text{Ri} = \text{Gr}/\text{Re}^2$ tells whether buoyancy or inertia dominates a mixed flow.
- [ ] Internal flow fully developed: $\text{Nu}_D = 4.36$ (constant $q''_s$) or $3.66$ (constant $T_s$).
- [ ] Entry length: $L_h \approx 0.05 D\,\text{Re}_D$ (hydrodynamic), $L_t \approx 0.05 D\,\text{Re}_D \Pr$ (thermal).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dimensionless Numbers in Forced and Free Convection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT746 - ADVANCED HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dimensionless Numbers in Forced and Free Convection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Convective Heat Transfer?

Convection is the mode of heat transfer between a solid surface and a fluid (liquid or gas) in motion. It is classified into two principal categories in advanced heat transfer studies:

- **Forced Convection:** Fluid motion is driven by an **external agency** such as a pump, blower, fan, or compressor. The fluid velocity field is imposed and is largely **independent** of the thermal field.
- **Free (Natural) Convection:** Fluid motion is driven by **buoyancy forces** that arise due to density differences caused by temperature gradients within the fluid. The velocity field is **coupled** to the thermal field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Dimensionless numbers are non-dimensional groupings of physical parameters that allow the convective transport problem to be expressed in a compact, scale-invariant form. They reveal the **relative dominance** of physical effects (inertia vs. viscosity, buoyancy vs. viscosity, conduction vs. convection, etc.).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Scaled Recipe" Idea

Imagine baking a cake. A recipe written for 4 eggs can be **scaled** to 40 eggs by multiplying every ingredient proportionally. Dimensionless numbers do the same thing for physics equations:

- A small lab experiment (water flowing in a 2 cm pipe) and a real industrial plant (water in a 50 cm pipe) can be **mathematically identical** if their dimensionless numbers match.
- This is the principle of **dynamic similarity** — the foundation of all experimental heat transfer correlations.

Think of dimensionless numbers as the "DNA fingerprint" of a flow problem. Two flows with the **same Reynolds number** behave identically in terms of inertial-to-viscous balance, regardless of their actual size or speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used:** Acceleration due to gravity $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, Universal Gas Constant $R_u = 8.314 \text{ kJ/kmol·K}$, Standard atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$.

## 1.3 Why Are Dimensionless Numbers Essential?

1. **Reduction of variables:** A problem with 8 physical variables can often be reduced to 3–4 dimensionless groups.
2. **Scale-up / Scale-down:** Laboratory data can be extrapolated to industrial equipment.
3. **Correlation development:** Empirical correlations (e.g., $Nu = C Re^m Pr^n$) are universally valid.
4. **Physical insight:** Each number represents a ratio of competing physical effects.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Forced Convection — Key Dimensionless Numbers

### 2.1.1 Reynolds Number ($Re$)
Represents the ratio of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces** in the fluid.

$$Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$

- $V$ = mean fluid velocity
- $L$ = characteristic length (e.g., diameter of pipe, length of plate)
- $\nu = \mu / \rho$ = kinematic viscosity
- **Interpretation:** $Re \gg 1$ ⇒ inertia dominates ⇒ **turbulent flow**; $Re \ll 1$ ⇒ viscosity dominates ⇒ **creeping flow**.

### 2.1.2 Prandtl Number ($Pr$)
Represents the ratio of **momentum diffusivity** to **thermal diffusivity**.

$$Pr = \frac{\nu}{\alpha} = \frac{\mu C_p}{k}$$

- $\alpha = k / (\rho C_p)$ = thermal diffusivity
- **Physical meaning:** Compares the thickness of velocity boundary layer ($\delta$) and thermal boundary layer ($\delta_t$).
  - $Pr \gg 1$ (oils): $\delta_t \ll \delta$ — thermal layer very thin
  - $Pr \ll 1$ (liquid metals): $\delta_t \gg \delta$ — thermal layer much thicker
  - $Pr \approx 1$ (gases): $\delta_t \approx \delta$

### 2.1.3 Nusselt Number ($Nu$)
Represents the ratio of **convective** to **conductive** heat transfer across the fluid.

$$Nu = \frac{h L}{k_f}$$

- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient
- $k_f$ = thermal conductivity of fluid
- **Interpretation:** $Nu = 1$ ⇒ pure conduction (no convection); $Nu \gg 1$ ⇒ convection strongly enhances heat transfer.

### 2.1.4 Peclet Number ($Pe$)
Represents the ratio of **advective transport rate** to **diffusive transport rate**.

$$Pe = Re \cdot Pr = \frac{V L}{\alpha}$$

### 2.1.5 Stanton Number ($St$)
A dimensionless heat transfer coefficient used in aerodynamic heating problems.

$$St = \frac{Nu}{Re \cdot Pr} = \frac{h}{\rho V C_p}$$

### 2.1.6 Friction Factor ($C_f$)
Ratio of wall shear stress to dynamic pressure.

$$C_f = \frac{\tau_w}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V^2}$$

### 2.1.7 Grashof Number in Forced Convection Context
When buoyancy is present but secondary (mixed convection), the relative importance is measured by:

$$\frac{Gr}{Re^2}$$

- $Gr / Re^2 \ll 1$ ⇒ forced convection dominates
- $Gr / Re^2 \gg 1$ ⇒ free convection dominates
- $Gr / Re^2 \approx 1$ ⇒ mixed convection regime

## 2.2 Free (Natural) Convection — Key Dimensionless Numbers

### 2.2.1 Grashof Number ($Gr$)
Represents the ratio of **buoyancy forces** to **viscous forces**.

$$Gr_L = \frac{g \beta (T_s - T_\infty) L^3}{\nu^2}$$

- $g$ = gravitational acceleration
- $\beta$ = volumetric thermal expansion coefficient (for ideal gas: $\beta = 1/T_f$, where $T_f$ is film temperature in Kelvin)
- $T_s$ = surface temperature
- $T_\infty$ = bulk fluid temperature
- $L$ = characteristic length

### 2.2.2 Rayleigh Number ($Ra$)
The governing parameter for free convection (analogous to Re for forced).

$$Ra_L = Gr_L \cdot Pr = \frac{g \beta (T_s - T_\infty) L^3}{\nu \alpha}$$

### 2.2.3 Nusselt Number in Free Convection
Expressed as a function of $Ra$ and $Pr$:

$$Nu_L = C (Ra_L)^n (Pr)^m$$

For laminar natural convection on a vertical plate (Churchill–Chu correlation):

$$Nu_L = \left\{ 0.825 + \frac{0.387 Ra_L^{1/6}}{\left[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{8/27}} \right\}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rayleigh Number:** $Ra_{cr} \approx 10^9$ marks the transition from laminar to turbulent free convection on a vertical plate.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Dimensionless Number | Symbol | Formula | Physical Ratio | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reynolds | $Re$ | $\rho V L / \mu$ | Inertia / Viscous | $10^0$ – $10^7$ |
| Prandtl | $Pr$ | $\mu C_p / k$ | Momentum diffusivity / Thermal diffusivity | $0.01$ (Hg) – $10^5$ (oils) |
| Nusselt | $Nu$ | $h L / k_f$ | Convection / Conduction | $1$ – $10^4$ |
| Peclet | $Pe$ | $Re \cdot Pr$ | Advection / Diffusion | — |
| Stanton | $St$ | $h / (\rho V C_p)$ | $Nu / (Re Pr)$ | $10^{-3}$ – $10^{-2}$ |
| Grashof | $Gr$ | $g \beta \Delta T L^3 / \nu^2$ | Buoyancy / Viscous | $10^{-1}$ – $10^{12}$ |
| Rayleigh | $Ra$ | $Gr \cdot Pr$ | Buoyancy / Diffusion | $10^0$ – $10^{15}$ |
| Friction Factor | $C_f$ | $2 \tau_w / (\rho V^2)$ | Wall shear / Dynamic | — |
| Eckert | $Ec$ | $V^2 / (C_p \Delta T)$ | Kinetic energy / Enthalpy | — |
| Mach | $M$ | $V / c$ | Flow / Sound speed | $< 0.3$ (incompressible) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Re and St are used in re-entry vehicle thermal design.
- **HVAC:** Gr and Ra govern chimney design, building ventilation, and solar collector efficiency.
- **Nuclear Reactors:** Pe and Nu govern coolant heat removal in fuel rods.
- **Electronics Cooling:** Forced convection Re and free convection Ra govern heat sink design.
- **Chemical Process Industry:** Nu–Re–Pr correlations are used in shell-and-tube heat exchangers.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Reynolds Number from Navier–Stokes Equation

### Step 1: Starting Point — General Momentum Equation

The convective momentum equation for an incompressible fluid is:

$$\rho \left( \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla) \vec{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$$

### Step 2: Identify the Dominant Terms

For steady, fully-developed, internal flow, the convective acceleration terms dominate over the unsteady term:

- Inertial term magnitude: $\rho V^2 / L$
- Viscous term magnitude: $\mu V / L^2$

### Step 3: Form the Ratio

Divide the inertial term by the viscous term:

$$\frac{\rho V^2 / L}{\mu V / L^2} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu}$$

### Step 4: Identify as Reynolds Number

$$Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$

This dimensionless ratio directly emerges from non-dimensionalizing the Navier–Stokes equation.

## 3.2 Derivation of Nusselt Number

### Step 1: Start from Newton's Law of Cooling

$$q'' = h (T_s - T_\infty)$$

### Step 2: Express Conduction Heat Flux Through a Fluid Layer of Thickness L

$$q''_{cond} = \frac{k_f (T_s - T_\infty)}{L}$$

### Step 3: Take the Ratio of Convection to Conduction

$$\frac{q''_{conv}}{q''_{cond}} = \frac{h (T_s - T_\infty)}{k_f (T_s - T_\infty) / L} = \frac{h L}{k_f}$$

### Step 4: Define Nusselt Number

$$\boxed{Nu = \frac{h L}{k_f}}$$

If $Nu = 1$, the convective resistance equals the conductive resistance of a stagnant film of thickness $L$ — a fundamental baseline.

## 3.3 Derivation of Prandtl Number

### Step 1: Compare Momentum and Thermal Diffusion Lengths

The momentum diffusivity (kinematic viscosity) is:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \quad [\text{m}^2/\text{s}]$$

The thermal diffusivity is:

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho C_p} \quad [\text{m}^2/\text{s}]$$

### Step 2: Form the Ratio

$$Pr = \frac{\nu}{\alpha} = \frac{\mu / \rho}{k / (\rho C_p)} = \frac{\mu C_p}{k}$$

### Step 3: Physical Interpretation

The ratio of the **velocity boundary layer thickness** to the **thermal boundary layer thickness** scales as:

$$\frac{\delta}{\delta_t} \sim Pr^{1/3} \quad \text{(for laminar flow)}$$

For example, for air ($Pr \approx 0.7$): $\delta / \delta_t \approx 0.7^{1/3} \approx 0.89$ — the two layers are nearly equal.

## 3.4 Derivation of Grashof Number

### Step 1: Set Up the Buoyancy-Driven Flow Scenario

Consider a vertical plate at temperature $T_s$ in a fluid at $T_\infty$. The local density variation with temperature, for small $\Delta T$, is:

$$\Delta \rho = -\rho_\infty \beta (T_s - T_\infty)$$

### Step 2: Calculate Buoyancy Force per Unit Volume

$$F_{buoy} = (\rho_\infty - \rho) g = \rho_\infty \beta (T_s - T_\infty) g$$

### Step 3: Form the Ratio with Viscous Force

Viscous force scales as: $\mu V / L^2$ where the characteristic velocity in free convection is determined by buoyancy (not externally imposed):

$$V \sim \sqrt{g \beta \Delta T L}$$

Viscous force magnitude: $\mu \sqrt{g \beta \Delta T L} / L^2$

### Step 4: Form the Ratio

$$\frac{F_{buoy}}{F_{visc}} = \frac{\rho_\infty \beta \Delta T g}{\mu \sqrt{g \beta \Delta T L} / L^2}$$

Squaring to eliminate the square root on $V$:

$$\left(\frac{F_{buoy}}{F_{visc}}\right)^2 = \frac{\rho^2 \beta^2 \Delta T^2 g^2 L^4}{\mu^2 g \beta \Delta T L} = \frac{\rho^2 \beta \Delta T g L^3}{\mu^2}$$

### Step 5: Define Grashof Number

$$\boxed{Gr_L = \frac{g \beta (T_s - T_\infty) L^3}{\nu^2}}$$

This is **derived** by dimensional analysis of the momentum equation with a buoyancy source term, treating the buoyancy-induced velocity as the characteristic velocity scale.

## 3.5 Derivation of Rayleigh Number

Once $Gr$ and $Pr$ are defined, the Rayleigh number naturally emerges as their product because it represents the ratio of **buoyancy-driven transport** to **thermal diffusion**:

$$\boxed{Ra_L = Gr_L \cdot Pr = \frac{g \beta (T_s - T_\infty) L^3}{\nu \alpha}}$$

The criterion for transition from laminar to turbulent natural convection on a vertical plate is $Ra_{cr} \approx 10^9$.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Forced vs. Free Convection Master Equation

The **general heat transfer correlation** takes the form:

$$Nu = f(Re, Pr, Gr)$$

This can be unified using the **Richardson Number** ($Ri$) which measures the relative importance of free vs. forced convection:

$$Ri = \frac{Gr}{Re^2}$$

| Regime | Condition | Dominant Mechanism | Correlation Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forced Convection | $Ri \ll 1$ | Inertia + Viscous | $Nu = C Re^m Pr^n$ |
| Mixed Convection | $Ri \approx 1$ | Buoyancy + Inertia | $Nu = f(Re, Pr, Gr)$ |
| Free Convection | $Ri \gg 1$ | Buoyancy + Viscous | $Nu = C Ra^n$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Result:** For natural convection on a vertical plate in the laminar regime ($10^4 < Ra_L < 10^9$):
> $$Nu_L = 0.59 Ra_L^{1/4}$$
> For turbulent natural convection ($Ra_L > 10^9$):
> $$Nu_L = 0.10 Ra_L^{1/3}$$
