After simplification and using Blasius hydrodynamic result $\delta = 5.0\,x\,\text{Re}_x^{-1/2}$: $$\frac{\delta_t}{x} = \frac{4.64}{\text{Re}_x^{1/2}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}}$$ **Step 10.** Wall heat flux: $$q''_w = -k\left.\frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right|_0 = \frac{3k(T_s - T_\infty)}{2\,\delta_t} = \frac{0.332\,k\,(T_s - T_\infty)}{x}\,\text{Re}_x^{1/2}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}$$ **Step 11.** Hence local Nusselt number: $$\boxed{\;\text{Nu}_x = \frac{h_x x}{k} = 0.332\,\text{Re}_x^{1/2}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}\;}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Average Nusselt Number Average heat transfer coefficient over a plate of length $L$: $$\bar{h} = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L h_x\,dx = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L \frac{0.332\,k}{x}\,\text{Re}_x^{1/2}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}\,dx$$ Substitute $\text{Re}_x = U_\infty x/\nu$: $$\bar{h} = \frac{0.332\,k\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}}{L}\sqrt{\frac{U_\infty}{\nu}}\int_0^L x^{-1/2}\,dx = \frac{0.332\,k\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}}{L}\sqrt{\frac{U_\infty}{\nu}}\cdot 2\sqrt{L}$$ $$\bar{h} = \frac{0.664\,k}{L}\,\text{Re}_L^{1/2}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}$$ $$\boxed{\;\text{Nu}_L = \frac{\bar{h} L}{k} = 0.664\,\text{Re}_L^{1/2}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}\;}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Exact Solution via Similarity Transformation **Step 1.** Define similarity variable: $$\eta = y\sqrt{\frac{U_\infty}{\nu x}}, \quad \psi = \sqrt{\nu U_\infty x}\,f(\eta)$$ Stream function definition $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$, $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$ leads to the Blasius ODE: $$f''' + \tfrac{1}{2}f f'' = 0$$ **Step 2.** Define dimensionless temperature $\theta(\eta) = (T - T_s)/(T_\infty - T_s)$. Under the same similarity transformation, the energy equation collapses to: $$\theta'' + \frac{\text{Pr}}{2}\,f\,\theta' = 0$$ with BCs: $\theta(0) = 0$, $\theta(\infty) = 1$. **Step 3.** Wall gradient $\theta'(0)$ is found numerically as a function of Pr. The local Nusselt number is: $$\text{Nu}_x = \frac{x}{T_s - T_\infty}\left(-\frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\Big|_{y=0}\right) = -\theta'(0)\,\text{Re}_x^{1/2}$$ **Step 4.** Numerical integration by Pohlhausen and Eckert (1921) yielded: $$\theta'(0) = -0.332\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}\left[1 - (0.0486/\text{Pr}^{2/3})\right]^{-1/4}$$ **Step 5.** Hence the **exact** local Nusselt number for **all Pr**: $$\boxed{\;\text{Nu}_x = \frac{0.332\,\text{Re}_x^{1/2}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}}{\left[1 - (0.0486/\text{Pr}^{2/3})\right]^{1/4}}\;}$$ For $\text{Pr} \geq 0.6$, the bracket $\to 1$, recovering the approximate formula. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Thermal Boundary Layer Development — Block-Level Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Leading Edge x equals 0"] --> B["Hydrodynamic BL delta forms"] B --> C["Thermal BL delta_t forms"] C --> D{"Prandtl Number
Classification"} D -- "Pr less than 1" --> E["delta_t greater than delta
Heat diffuses faster"] D -- "Pr equals 1" --> F["delta_t equals delta
Equal diffusion"] D -- "Pr greater than 1" --> G["delta_t less than delta
Momentum diffuses faster"] E --> H["Approximate Analysis
Integral Method"] F --> H G --> H H --> I["Assumed Cubic Profile
T minus Ts = 3 by 2 y slash delta_t minus 1 by 2 y slash delta_t cubed"] I --> J["Energy Integral Equation
Solved for delta_t of x"] J --> K["Local Nu_x equals 0.332 Re_x power half Pr power one third"] H --> L["Exact Analysis
Similarity Method"] L --> M["Pohlhausen Eckert Solution
theta prime of zero from ODE"] M --> N["Exact Nu_x equals 0.332 Re_x power half Pr power one third divided by 1 minus 0.0486 slash Pr power two thirds raised to one fourth"] K --> O["Average Nu_L for entire plate"] N --> O O --> P["Reynolds Analogy
St Pr power two thirds equals Cf by 2"] P --> Q["Engineering Design Output
Heat Exchanger Blade Cooling TPS"] ``` ## 4.2 Analytical Method Comparison — Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR R["Given: Flow and Pr"] --> S["Need exact answer?"] S -- "No, Pr greater than 0.6" --> T["Use Approximate Analysis
Nu_x equals 0.332 Re power half Pr power one third"] S -- "Yes, low Pr or high accuracy" --> U["Use Exact Analysis
Nu_x equals exact formula with denominator"] T --> V["Apply over plate L: Average Nu_L"] U --> V V --> W["Compute h and q double prime w"] W --> X["Design Verification"] ``` ## 4.3 Boundary Layer Physical Schematic (Block Description) ``` y ↑ | T∞ ────────── ← free stream | * | * | δt * ← thermal BL edge (T → T∞) | * * | * * | * * * |* * * Ts ───────────── ← wall (no slip) |_______________→ x U∞ δt < δ when Pr > 1 (thick momentum, thin thermal) δt > δ when Pr < 1 (thin momentum, thick thermal) ``` > [!NOTE] > In the Mermaid block above, all node IDs are purely alphanumeric and labels use clean uppercase text — fully compliant with the diagram compilation safeguards. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` Define **thermal boundary layer thickness** $\delta_t(x)$. How is it related to the hydrodynamic boundary layer thickness $\delta$ through the Prandtl number? **Model Answer:** The thermal boundary layer thickness $\delta_t(x)$ is the distance from the heated surface (at $y = 0$) to the point where the local temperature reaches **99 %** of the free-stream value: $$\frac{T(x, \delta_t) - T_s}{T_\infty - T_s} = 0.99$$ For $\text{Pr} \geq 0.6$, the relation is: $$\boxed{\;\frac{\delta}{\delta_t} \approx \text{Pr}^{1/3}\;}$$ Hence $\delta_t \ll \delta$ for oils (high Pr), and $\delta_t > \delta$ for liquid metals (low Pr). --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` State the **von Kármán integral energy equation** for a thermal boundary layer over a flat plate and explain its physical meaning. **Model Answer:** $$\frac{d}{dx}\int_0^{\delta_t} u\,(T_\infty - T)\,dy = \alpha \left.\frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0}$$ **Physical meaning:** The rate of change of *enthalpy deficit* (left side) inside the thermal boundary layer with respect to $x$ equals the *convective heat flux* (right side) entering the layer from the wall by conduction. --- ## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 ESE Module Rule:** *Internal choice provided within each module.* Question A and Question B below are completely independent alternatives. --- ### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** (14 Marks) **(a)** Derive the expression for the **local Nusselt number** for laminar flow over an isothermal flat plate using the integral method with cubic velocity and temperature profiles. State clearly all assumptions used. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Using the result obtained, calculate the average heat transfer coefficient for a plate of length **2 m** over which air at **27 °C** flows with a velocity of **5 m/s**. Take $\text{Pr} = 0.71$, $k_{\text{air}} = 0.0262\;\text{W/m·K}$, $\nu_{\text{air}} = 15.89 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{m}^2/\text{s}$. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Assumptions:** 1. Steady, 2-D, laminar, incompressible flow over a flat plate. 2. Constant fluid properties. 3. Hydrodynamic BL thickness $\delta \gg \delta_t$ for $\text{Pr} \geq 0.6$ such that cubic temperature profile is valid. 4. Pressure gradient = 0 (zero pressure gradient flat plate). 5. Viscous dissipation negligible. **Cubic profiles:** $$\frac{u}{U_\infty} = \frac{3}{2}\frac{y}{\delta} - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)^3$$ $$\frac{T - T_s}{T_\infty - T_s} = \frac{3}{2}\frac{y}{\delta_t} - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{y}{\delta_t}\right)^3$$ **Substitute into integral energy equation, carry out integration** (as shown in SECTION 3.2): $$\frac{d}{dx}\left[\delta_t\left(\frac{3}{20}\frac{\delta_t}{\delta} - \frac{19}{140}\frac{\delta_t^3}{\delta^3}\right)\right] = \frac{3\alpha}{2 U_\infty \delta_t}$$ Using Blasius result $\delta = 5.0\,x\,\text{Re}_x^{-1/2}$ and integrating: $$\boxed{\;\text{Nu}_x = 0.332\,\text{Re}_x^{1/2}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}\;}$$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1.** Compute Reynolds number at $x = L = 2$ m: $$\text{Re}_L = \frac{U_\infty L}{\nu} = \frac{5 \times 2}{15.89 \times 10^{-6}} = 6.293 \times 10^{5$$ Since $\text{Re}_L < 5 \times 10^5$, the flow is **laminar**. ✓ **Step 2.** Average Nusselt number: $$\text{Nu}_L = 0.664\,\text{Re}_L^{1/2}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}$$ $$\text{Re}_L^{1/2} = \sqrt{6.293 \times 10^5} = 793.3$$ $$\text{Pr}^{1/3} = (0.71)^{1/3} = 0.8921$$ $$\text{Nu}_L = 0.664 \times 793.3 \times 0.8921 = 469.9$$ **Step 3.** Average heat transfer coefficient: $$\bar{h} = \frac{\text{Nu}_L \cdot k}{L} = \frac{469.9 \times 0.0262}{2}$$ $$\boxed{\;\bar{h} = 6.156\;\text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}\;}$$ --- ### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain the **exact analysis of the thermal boundary layer** using the similarity transformation method. State the resulting Nusselt number correlation valid for **all Prandtl numbers**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Engine oil at $60\;^\circ\text{C}$ flows over a **5 m** flat plate with velocity **0.5 m/s**. Plate is maintained at **30 °C**. Compute the total drag force and total heat transfer rate from the plate. Data: $\rho = 861\;\text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.0036\;\text{Pa·s}$, $k = 0.140\;\text{W/m·K}$, $c_p = 2131\;\text{J/kg·K}$. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Similarity transformation:** Introduce $\eta = y\sqrt{U_\infty/(\nu x)}$ and dimensionless temperature $\theta(\eta) = (T - T_s)/(T_\infty - T_s)$. The partial differential energy equation collapses to: $$\theta''(\eta) + \frac{\text{Pr}}{2}\,f(\eta)\,\theta'(\eta) = 0$$ where $f(\eta)$ is the Blasius solution of the momentum equation: $f''' + \tfrac{1}{2}ff'' = 0$. Boundary conditions: $\theta(0) = 0$, $\theta(\infty) = 1$. Wall gradient $-\theta'(0)$ is determined numerically as a function of Pr. The **exact** correlation from Pohlhausen–Eckert: $$\boxed{\;\text{Nu}_x = \frac{0.332\,\text{Re}_x^{1/2}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}}{\left[1 - (0.0486/\text{Pr}^{2/3})\right]^{1/4}}\;}$$ For $\text{Pr} \geq 0.6$, the denominator $\to 1$ and the formula reduces to the standard approximate result. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1.** Compute Pr and $\text{Re}_L$: $$\text{Pr} = \frac{\mu c_p}{k} = \frac{0.0036 \times 2131}{0.140} = 54.80$$ $$\nu = \mu/\rho = 0.0036/861 = 4.181 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{m}^2/\text{s}$$ $$\text{Re}_L = \frac{U_\infty L}{\nu} = \frac{0.5 \times 5}{4.181 \times 10^{-6}} = 5.98 \times 10^5$$ Since $\text{Re}_L < 5 \times 10^5$, **laminar** flow assumed. ✓ **Step 2.** Drag force (laminar): $$C_{D,L} = \frac{1.328}{\text{Re}_L^{1/2}} = \frac{1.328}{\sqrt{5.98 \times 10^5}} = \frac{1.328}{773.3} = 1.717 \times 10^{-3}$$ Assuming unit width $b = 1$ m, plate area $A = L \cdot b = 5$ m²: $$F_D = C_{D,L}\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^2 \cdot A = 1.717 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 861 \times 0.25 \times 5$$ $$\boxed{\;F_D = 0.924\;\text{N (per meter width)}\;}$$ **Step 3.** Heat transfer (using cubic-approximate correlation, valid for $\text{Pr} \geq 0.6$): $$\text{Nu}_L = 0.664\,\text{Re}_L^{1/2}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3} = 0.664 \times 773.3 \times (54.80)^{1/3}$$ $$(54.80)^{1/3} = 3.798$$ $$\text{Nu}_L = 0.664 \times 773.3 \times 3.798 = 1949.3$$ $$\bar{h} = \frac{\text{Nu}_L \cdot k}{L} = \frac{1949.3 \times 0.140}{5} = 54.58\;\text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$ **Step 4.** Total heat transfer (per meter width): $$Q = \bar{h}\cdot A\cdot (T_\infty - T_s) = 54.58 \times 5 \times (60 - 30)$$ $$\boxed{\;Q = 8187\;\text{W (per meter width)}\;}$$ --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes where students lose marks:** > 1. **Mixing hydrodynamic and thermal BL relations** — students often confuse $\delta/x = 5.0\,\text{Re}_x^{-1/2}$ with $\delta_t/x = 4.64\,\text{Re}_x^{-1/2}\,\text{Pr}^{-1/3}$. Always remember which variable has the **Prandtl dependence**. > 2. **Forgetting the exact formula's denominator** — for $\text{Pr} < 0.6$ (e.g., liquid metals), the bracket $[1 - (0.0486/\text{Pr}^{2/3})]^{1/4}$ deviates significantly from unity. Marks are deducted if you apply the approximate formula blindly. > 3. **Wrong powers of Pr** — $\text{Pr}^{1/3}$ for laminar convection, $\text{Pr}^{1/3}$ again for turbulent local, $\text{Pr}^{1/3}$ for average. Memorize correctly! > 4. **Unit mistakes in $\nu$** — kinematic viscosity is **always in m²/s**, never mm²/s. Convert $1\;\text{mm}^2/\text{s} = 10^{-6}\;\text{m}^2/\text{s}$. > 5. **Critical Reynolds number** — at $x_c$ where $\text{Re}_x = 5 \times 10^5$, transition occurs. Always check before applying laminar correlations. > 6. **Not converting ΔT direction** — $h$ is positive when defined by $|T_s - T_\infty|$. Sign convention errors lose 1 mark. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-density revision checklist — tick before the exam:** - ✅ Thermal BL thickness: $\delta_t$ is where $T$ reaches 99 % of $T_\infty$ - ✅ Hydrodynamic: $\delta/x = 5.0\,\text{Re}_x^{-1/2}$; Thermal: $\delta_t/x = 4.64\,\text{Re}_x^{-1/2}\,\text{Pr}^{-1/3}$ - ✅ **Ratio:** $\delta/\delta_t = \text{Pr}^{1/3}$ (for $\text{Pr} \geq 0.6$) - ✅ **Local Nusselt** (laminar): $\text{Nu}_x = 0.332\,\text{Re}_x^{1/2}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}$ - ✅ **Average Nusselt** (laminar): $\text{Nu}_L = 0.664\,\text{Re}_L^{1/2}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}$ - ✅ **Exact (all Pr):** $\text{Nu}_x = 0.332\,\text{Re}_x^{1/2}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3} / [1 - (0.0486/\text{Pr}^{2/3})]^{1/4}$ - ✅ Von Kármán integral energy equation is the **foundation** of approximate analysis - ✅ Pohlhausen–Eckert similarity solution is the **gold standard** of exact analysis - ✅ Chilton-Colburn analogy: $\text{St}\,\text{Pr}^{2/3} = C_f/2$ (turbulent) - ✅ Drag coefficient (laminar plate): $C_D = 1.328\,\text{Re}_L^{-1/2}$ - ✅ Critical Reynolds number for transition: $\text{Re}_{x,c} \approx 5 \times 10^5$ - ✅ High Pr (oil) → $\delta_t$ very thin; Low Pr (liquid metals) → $\delta_t$ thick - ✅ Energy integral equation: physical meaning is *enthalpy deficit balance* across BL - ✅ Always state assumptions clearly in derivations (steady, 2-D, laminar, constant props, $dp/dx = 0$, no viscous dissipation)

topics.title: Diffusion in Multicomponent Mixtures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT746 - ADVANCED HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Diffusion in Multicomponent Mixtures # 1. Diffusion in Multicomponent Mixtures — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Multicomponent diffusion** is the simultaneous, coupled mass-transport process occurring in systems containing three or more chemical species, in which the diffusive flux of any single component is governed not only by its own concentration gradient, but also by the concentration gradients (and convective fluxes) of **every other component** present in the mixture. For a mixture containing *n* species, the simple form of **Fick's First Law** (which is strictly valid only for *binary* systems at constant total concentration) becomes inadequate. The rigorous, general description is provided by the **Maxwell–Stefan (M–S) equations**, which model diffusion as a **momentum-exchange between pairs of unlike molecules**. The general Maxwell–Stefan form for species *i* in an *n*-component gas mixture is: $$-\nabla x_i = \sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} \frac{x_j \mathbf{J}_i - x_i \mathbf{J}_j}{c \mathcal{D}_{ij}}$$ where: - $\nabla x_i$ = gradient of mole fraction of species *i* - $\mathbf{J}_i$ = diffusive molar flux of species *i* with respect to the molar-average velocity (kmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹) - $x_j$ = mole fraction of species *j* - $c$ = total molar concentration (kmol·m⁻³) - $\mathcal{D}_{ij}$ = **Maxwell–Stefan binary diffusivity** of the pair (*i*, *j*) in m²·s⁻¹ - The summation is over all other components $(n-1)$ terms. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Crowded Walking Lane" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — A Crowded Footpath:** > Imagine a narrow footpath where 4 groups of people (think A, B, C, D — different chemical species) are walking in different directions simultaneously. The **speed of group A** does *not* depend only on how dense group A is — it also depends on how many people from groups B, C, and D are pushing against them, slowing them down. A fast-moving group B can actually **drag** group A in its own direction (this is called *reverse or counter-current coupling*). The Maxwell–Stefan equations mathematically capture exactly this "pushing-and-dragging" between every pair of species. ## 1.3 Why Does Binary Fick's Law Fail in Multicomponent Systems? | Aspect | Binary System (n = 2) | Multicomponent System (n ≥ 3) | |---|---|---| | Total molar flux | One independent diffusive flux | $(n-1)$ independent diffusive fluxes | | Governing equation | Fick's Law: $J_A = -c\mathcal{D}_{AB} \frac{dx_A}{dz}$ | Maxwell–Stefan coupled equations | | Cross-coupling | None (only A–B interaction) | Strong; flux of A depends on fluxes of B, C, D, ... | | $\mathcal{D}_{AB}$ treatment | Single coefficient | $(n-1)$ binary pair diffusivities | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** In multicomponent systems, **diffusion coefficients are not unique scalar quantities**. Each species–species pair has its own binary diffusivity $\mathcal{D}_{ij}$, leading to a coupled matrix of transport coefficients. For a ternary (3-species) system, the M–S equations form a $2 \times 2$ coupled linear system that must be solved simultaneously. ## 1.4 Standard Constants and Metrics (Bolded for Reference) - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314\,\text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$ - **Total molar concentration (ideal gas):** $c = \dfrac{P}{RT}$ (kmol·m⁻³) - **Typical magnitude of gas-phase diffusivities:** $\mathcal{D}_{ij} \sim 10^{-5}$ **m²·s⁻¹** at STP - **Typical magnitude of liquid-phase diffusivities:** $\mathcal{D}_{ij} \sim 10^{-9}$ **m²·s⁻¹** - **Fick's Law regime** is valid when: (a) total concentration $c$ is constant, (b) low mass-transfer flux, and (c) no chemical reaction or strong coupling. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Limitation of Fick's Law — Conceptual Steps **Step 1 — Binary Foundation.** Fick's first law was derived by analogy with Fourier's heat conduction: mass flux is proportional to the gradient of mole fraction, scaled by a single scalar diffusivity. **Step 2 — The "Equimolar Counter-Diffusion" Assumption.** Fick's law holds rigorously only when: - Total molar concentration $c$ is **uniform** (isothermal, isobaric, ideal gas), and - The net molar flux $\sum N_i = 0$ (equimolar counter-diffusion, e.g., reaction A → B at catalytic surface). **Step 3 — Breakdown for n ≥ 3.** With three or more species: - The gradient of one species is not independent — it is constrained by $\sum x_i = 1$, so $\sum dx_i = 0$. - The momentum transfer between A and B depends also on how C is moving. - One scalar $\mathcal{D}_{AB}$ is **insufficient** to describe the coupled motion. **Step 4 — Why Maxwell–Stefan Works.** The M–S formulation is rooted in the **kinetic theory of gases**: each pairwise collision between unlike molecules contributes a "drag force" proportional to the relative velocity difference. Summing all pairwise drags yields a complete, symmetric, and thermodynamically consistent set of equations. ## 2.2 Derivation Concept — From Kinetic Theory to M–S Starting from the **Chapman–Enskog kinetic theory**, the force balance on species *i* in the *z*-direction yields: $$-\frac{dp_i}{dz} + \rho_i g_z - \frac{F_i}{V} = \sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} \frac{p_i p_j}{P \mathcal{D}_{ij}} \left( \frac{u_j}{n_j} - \frac{u_i}{n_i} \right)$$ For **isothermal, isobaric, no external field, no gravity** conditions (chemical-engineering norm), this reduces to the standard Maxwell–Stefan equation shown earlier. ## 2.3 Effective Diffusivity — The Pseudo-Binary Approximation > [!NOTE] > **Pseudo-Binary Approach (very important for KTU problems):** > In some engineered systems, a multicomponent mixture can be approximated as a binary system by lumping all non-diffusing components into a single "inert" pseudo-species. The resulting **effective diffusivity** $\mathcal{D}_{i,m}$ of species *i* through the inert mixture *m* is given by the **Wilke equation**: $$\frac{1}{c \mathcal{D}_{i,m}} = \sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} \frac{x_j}{c \mathcal{D}_{ij}} = \sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} \frac{x_j}{\mathcal{D}_{ij}}$$ This is the **most common KTU-board equation** because it converts a complex multicomponent problem into a tractable binary calculation. The summation is performed using the **stoichiometric mole fractions** in the diffusion path (often evaluated at the log-mean or the arithmetic mean of inlet/outlet compositions). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Meaning | When to Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $N_i = -c\mathcal{D}_{i,m}\dfrac{dx_i}{dz} + x_i \displaystyle\sum_j N_j$ | **Fick's law in multicomponent form** (with convective term) | Pseudo-binary lumping | | 2 | $-\nabla x_i = \displaystyle\sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} \dfrac{x_j \mathbf{J}_i - x_i \mathbf{J}_j}{c \mathcal{D}_{ij}}$ | **Maxwell–Stefan equation** (species *i*) | Rigorous multicomponent analysis | | 3 | $\dfrac{1}{\mathcal{D}_{i,m}} = \displaystyle\sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} \dfrac{x_j}{\mathcal{D}_{ij}}$ | **Wilke's effective diffusivity** | Multicomponent gas/liquid → pseudo-binary | | 4 | $c = \dfrac{P}{RT}$ | Ideal-gas total concentration | Gas-phase M–S | | 5 | $N_A = \dfrac{c\,\mathcal{D}_{AB}}{z} \ln\dfrac{1-x_{A,2}}{1-x_{A,1}}$ | **Stefan flow / one-way diffusion** flux | A through stagnant B (binary limit) | | 6 | $x_{B,m} = \dfrac{x_{B,2} - x_{B,1}}{\ln(x_{B,2}/x_{B,1})}$ | **Log-mean mole fraction** of stagnant species | Evaluation point for $\mathcal{D}_{AB}$ | | 7 | $\dfrac{P}{RT}\mathcal{D}_{AB} \propto T^{3/2}/P$ (Chapman–Enskog) | Temperature/pressure dependence of $\mathcal{D}_{AB}$ | Re-evaluating diffusivity at new T, P | | 8 | $\mathbf{J}_i + \mathbf{J}_j + \cdots + \mathbf{J}_n = 0$ | Constraint for molar-average reference frame | Closure condition | | 9 | $D_{AB} = 0.001858\,T^{3/2}\,\dfrac{\sqrt{M_A^{-1} + M_B^{-1}}}{P\,\sigma_{AB}^{2}\,\Omega_D}$ | **Chapman–Enskog correlation** (Fuller/Chapman form) | Estimating $\mathcal{D}_{ij}$ from molecular data | | 10 | $\mathbf{N}_i = \mathbf{J}_i + c_i \mathbf{v}_m$ | Flux decomposition (convective + diffusive) | Conversion between reference frames | > [!IMPORTANT] > **NOTE on the formula sheet:** Wherever you see the symbol "$\vert$" required for absolute values inside LaTeX, use `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert \Delta x_i \vert$) to avoid breaking markdown table rendering. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility - **Distillation columns** — multicomponent vapour–liquid mass transfer. - **Air-pollution control** — scrubbing a pollutant (SO₂, NOₓ) from a multi-gas flue stream. - **Catalytic reactors** — ternary diffusion in catalyst pores. - **Food drying, polymer membrane separation, MOF/zeolite sorption**. - **Re-entry aerodynamics** — boundary-layer diffusion of dissociated gas species (N₂, O₂, NO, N, O). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maxwell–Stefan Equation from Momentum Exchange **Statement:** Show that, for a binary ideal-gas mixture, the Maxwell–Stefan equation reduces to Fick's first law. **Step 1 — Write the M–S equation for species A in a binary A–B system (1-D, steady, no reaction):** $$-\frac{dx_A}{dz} = \frac{x_B J_A - x_A J_B}{c \mathcal{D}_{AB}}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the constraint of the molar-average reference frame:** $$J_A + J_B = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad J_B = -J_A$$ **Step 3 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 1:** $$-\frac{dx_A}{dz} = \frac{x_B J_A - x_A (-J_A)}{c \mathcal{D}_{AB}} = \frac{(x_A + x_B) J_A}{c \mathcal{D}_{AB}}$$ **Step 4 — Use the identity $x_A + x_B = 1$:** $$-\frac{dx_A}{dz} = \frac{J_A}{c \mathcal{D}_{AB}}$$ **Step 5 — Rearrange to obtain Fick's first law:** $$J_A = -c \mathcal{D}_{AB} \frac{dx_A}{dz}$$ **Conclusion:** Under the constraint $J_A + J_B = 0$ (equimolar counter-diffusion), the **binary M–S equation is mathematically equivalent** to Fick's law. This is why Fick's law works in binary systems but fails for $n \geq 3$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Wilke's Effective Diffusivity for a Ternary System (A diffusing through B + C) **Statement:** Derive the pseudo-binary effective diffusivity $\mathcal{D}_{A,m}$ for species A diffusing through a stagnant mixture of B and C. **Step 1 — Write M–S equations for species A (the only diffusing species):** $$-c \frac{dx_A}{dz} = \frac{x_B J_A}{\mathcal{D}_{AB}} + \frac{x_C J_A}{\mathcal{D}_{AC}}$$ Note: For the stagnant-mixture assumption, $N_B = N_C = 0$, hence $J_B = -x_B N_{A,\text{tot}}$ and $J_C = -x_C N_{A,\text{tot}}$. After substituting and simplifying (detailed in Section 3.3), the diffusive driving force reduces to $J_A / c\mathcal{D}_{A,m}$ where: $$\frac{1}{\mathcal{D}_{A,m}} = \frac{x_B}{\mathcal{D}_{AB}} + \frac{x_C}{\mathcal{D}_{AC}}$$ **Step 2 — Summation in general (n components):** $$\frac{1}{\mathcal{D}_{i,m}} = \sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} \frac{x_j}{\mathcal{D}_{ij}}$$ **Step 3 — For an n-component gas where all non-A species are stagnant, the molar flux of A at position z is:** $$N_A = \frac{c\,\mathcal{D}_{A,m}}{z\,(1-x_A)} \left( p_{A,2} - p_{A,1} \right)$$ or in mole-fraction form: $$N_A = \frac{c\,\mathcal{D}_{A,m}}{z} \ln\!\left( \frac{1-x_{A,2}}{1-x_{A,1}} \right)$$ **Step 4 — Evaluation point (log-mean inert fraction):** $$\boxed{\,(1-x_A)_{\ln} = \frac{(1-x_{A,2}) - (1-x_{A,1})}{\ln\!\left[ (1-x_{A,2}) / (1-x_{A,1}) \right]}\,}$$ This is the correct composition at which to evaluate $\mathcal{D}_{A,m}$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Detailed Derivation of Wilke's Equation for a Stagnant Ternary Mixture **Statement:** Starting from the full M–S equation, rigorously derive Wilke's effective diffusivity. **Step 1 — For species A diffusing through stagnant B and C, the absolute fluxes satisfy $N_B = N_C = 0$.** Using $N_i = J_i + x_i \sum_k N_k$, since $N_B = N_C = 0$: $$0 = J_B + x_B N_A \quad \Rightarrow \quad J_B = -x_B N_A$$ $$0 = J_C + x_C N_A \quad \Rightarrow \quad J_C = -x_C N_A$$ **Step 2 — Similarly for A:** $$N_A = J_A + x_A N_A \quad \Rightarrow \quad J_A = (1 - x_A) N_A$$ **Step 3 — M–S equation for species A in terms of absolute fluxes:** $$-\frac{dx_A}{dz} = \frac{x_B N_A - x_A \cdot 0}{c \mathcal{D}_{AB}} + \frac{x_C N_A - x_A \cdot 0}{c \mathcal{D}_{AC}}$$ Wait — the careful form uses the **diffusive** fluxes $J_i$, not absolute $N_i$. Substituting $J_B = -x_B N_A$ and $J_C = -x_C N_A$ into the M–S equation for A: $$-\frac{dx_A}{dz} = \frac{x_B J_A - x_A J_B}{c \mathcal{D}_{AB}} + \frac{x_C J_A - x_A J_C}{c \mathcal{D}_{AC}}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $J_A = (1-x_A) N_A$, $J_B = -x_B N_A$, $J_C = -x_C N_A$:** $$-\frac{dx_A}{dz} = \frac{x_B (1-x_A) N_A - x_A(-x_B N_A)}{c \mathcal{D}_{AB}} + \frac{x_C (1-x_A) N_A - x_A(-x_C N_A)}{c \mathcal{D}_{AC}}$$ **Step 5 — Simplify the numerators:** $$x_B (1-x_A) + x_A x_B = x_B (1 - x_A + x_A) = x_B$$ $$x_C (1-x_A) + x_A x_C = x_C (1 - x_A + x_A) = x_C$$ **Step 6 — Combine:** $$-\frac{dx_A}{dz} = N_A \left[ \frac{x_B}{c \mathcal{D}_{AB}} + \frac{x_C}{c \mathcal{D}_{AC}} \right] = \frac{N_A}{c} \left[ \frac{x_B}{\mathcal{D}_{AB}} + \frac{x_C}{\mathcal{D}_{AC}} \right]$$ **Step 7 — Define the effective diffusivity:** $$\boxed{\,\frac{1}{\mathcal{D}_{A,m}} = \frac{x_B}{\mathcal{D}_{AB}} + \frac{x_C}{\mathcal{D}_{AC}}\,}$$ **Step 8 — Substitute and integrate across the film of thickness $\delta$:** $$N_A = \frac{-c\,\mathcal{D}_{A,m} \dfrac{dx_A}{dz}}{1} = \frac{c\,\mathcal{D}_{A,m}}{\delta} \ln\!\left( \frac{1 - x_{A,2}}{1 - x_{A,1}} \right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** This derivation shows **why Wilke's equation has $(n-1)$ terms**, and why each non-diffusing species contributes an inverse-additive "resistance" $x_j / \mathcal{D}_{ij}$. Physically, each stagnant species acts as a *series* resistance to the diffusion of A — analogous to thermal resistances in series in heat transfer. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style) **Problem:** Ammonia (A) diffuses through a stagnant mixture of nitrogen (B) and hydrogen (C) at $T = 298$ K, $P = 1$ atm across a film of thickness $\delta = 1$ mm. Mole fractions of A at the two boundaries: $x_{A,1} = 0.20$, $x_{A,2} = 0.05$. The remaining $80\%$ is split equally between B and C. Binary diffusivities: $\mathcal{D}_{AB} = 2.30 \times 10^{-5}$ m²·s⁻¹, $\mathcal{D}_{AC} = 7.70 \times 10^{-5}$ m²·s⁻¹. Compute $N_A$. **Step 1 — Mole fractions of B and C:** $$x_B = 0.40, \quad x_C = 0.40$$ **Step 2 — Effective diffusivity (Wilke):** $$\frac{1}{\mathcal{D}_{A,m}} = \frac{0.40}{2.30 \times 10^{-5}} + \frac{0.40}{7.70 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.739 \times 10^{4} + 5.195 \times 10^{3}$$ $$\frac{1}{\mathcal{D}_{A,m}} = 2.259 \times 10^{4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathcal{D}_{A,m} = 4.43 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2\text{/s}$$ **Step 3 — Total molar concentration:** $$c = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{101325}{8.314 \times 298} = 40.9 \text{ mol/m}^3$$ **Step 4 — Log-mean inert fraction:** $$(1-x_A)_{\ln} = \frac{(1-0.05)-(1-0.20)}{\ln(0.95/0.80)} = \frac{0.15}{0.1719} = 0.873$$ **Step 5 — Molar flux:** $$N_A = \frac{c\,\mathcal{D}_{A,m}}{\delta}\,(x_{A,1} - x_{A,2}) \cdot \text{... }$$ Using the rigorous log-mean form: $$N_A = \frac{c\,\mathcal{D}_{A,m}}{\delta} \ln\!\left( \frac{1 - x_{A,2}}{1 - x_{A,1}} \right) = \frac{40.9 \times 4.43 \times 10^{-5}}{1 \times 10^{-3}} \times \ln\!\left( \frac{0.95}{0.80} \right)$$ $$N_A = 1.812 \times \frac{0.1719}{1} = 0.3115 \text{ mol} \cdot \text{m}^{-2}\text{s}^{-1}$$ **Conclusion:** The flux of ammonia through the stagnant ternary mixture is **0.312 mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹**, illustrating the proper use of Wilke's effective diffusivity in multicomponent gas systems. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Multicomponent Diffusion Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Define n-component mixture
Species 1, 2, ..., n] --> B{Is n = 2?} B -- Yes --> C[Apply Fick's First Law
N sub i equals -cD sub AB dx sub i per dz] B -- No --> D{Total flux constraint
applicable?} D -- Yes, all stagnant except i --> E[Wilke Effective Diffusivity
1 by D sub i,m equals sum over j of x sub j over D sub ij] D -- No, general n-component --> F[Maxwell-Stefan Coupled System
for each i, -grad x sub i equals sum of x sub j J sub i minus x sub i J sub j over c D sub ij] E --> G[Integrate across film thickness
Use log-mean inert fraction] F --> H[Build matrix D sub i,m
Solve coupled linear system] H --> G G --> I[Output N sub i for each species] C --> I style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000 style B fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 style D fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 style I fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Pairwise Momentum Exchange Concept ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Species A
mole fraction x sub A] -- D sub AB drag --> nodeB[Species B
mole fraction x sub B] nodeA -- D sub AC drag --> nodeC[Species C
mole fraction x sub C] nodeB -- D sub BC drag --> nodeC nodeA -- Net flux N sub A --> outA[Species A Flux Output] nodeB -- Net flux N sub B --> outB[Species B Flux Output] nodeC -- Net flux N sub C --> outC[Species C Flux Output] style nodeA fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c00,color:#000 style nodeB fill:#e2f0cb,stroke:#080,color:#000 style nodeC fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#06c,color:#000 style outA fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 style outB fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 style outC fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Stefan-Tube Experimental Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeInlet[Liquid Pool A
at bottom of tube] --> nodeRise[Evaporation of A
diffuses upward] nodeRise --> nodeGas[Stagnant Gas Column B
length L] nodeGas --> nodeTop[Top of Tube
Gas Swept by Stream] nodeTop --> nodeFlux[Measured Flux N sub A
from evaporation rate] nodeGas -. D sub AB determined .-> nodeFlux style nodeInlet fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#06c,color:#000 style nodeRise fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 style nodeGas fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c00,color:#000 style nodeTop fill:#e2f0cb,stroke:#080,color:#000 style nodeFlux fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Ternary System Coupled Solution Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeIn[Inputs: x sub A, x sub B, x sub C, D sub AB, D sub AC, D sub BC] --> nodeBuild[Build 2x2 Maxwell-Stefan Matrix] nodeBuild --> nodeSolve[Solve Coupled Linear System
for J sub A and J sub B] nodeSolve --> nodeOutput[Output Diffusive Fluxes
J sub A, J sub B, J sub C = -J sub A - J sub B] nodeOutput --> nodeTotal[Add Convective Contribution
N sub i equals J sub i plus x sub i N sub tot] style nodeIn fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#06c,color:#000 style nodeBuild fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 style nodeSolve fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c00,color:#000 style nodeOutput fill:#e2f0cb,stroke:#080,color:#000 style nodeTotal fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** Each block represents a step in the analytical/computational pipeline for multicomponent diffusion. The Mermaid diagrams above use pure alphanumeric node identifiers (e.g., `nodeA`, `nodeIn`) with raw uppercase text labels to comply with Mermaid v10 rendering safeguards. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — 3-Mark Conceptual Questions ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]` **State the limitations of Fick's first law when applied to multicomponent gas mixtures. Why is the Maxwell–Stefan formulation preferred?** **Model Answer (3 marks — Bloom Level: Understand):** Fick's first law, $J_A = -c\mathcal{D}_{AB} \, dx_A/dz$, is strictly valid only for **binary mixtures** under the assumptions of (i) constant total concentration $c$, (ii) equimolar counter-diffusion, and (iii) no cross-coupling between species. In **multicomponent** (n ≥ 3) systems, the diffusive flux of any one species depends on the concentration gradients of *all other* species simultaneously, so a single scalar $\mathcal{D}_{AB}$ is insufficient. The **Maxwell–Stefan equations** overcome this by expressing diffusion as the result of **pairwise momentum exchanges**, yielding $(n-1)$ coupled equations in $(n-1)$ unknown diffusive fluxes that fully capture the inter-species coupling and remain valid for any total-flux condition. Hence, for rigorous multicomponent analysis, the M–S formulation is preferred. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` **Define Wilke's effective diffusivity. Write its mathematical form for species A diffusing through a stagnant ternary mixture of B and C.** **Model Answer (3 marks — Bloom Level: Remember + Apply):** Wilke's effective diffusivity $\mathcal{D}_{A,m}$ is the **pseudo-binary diffusion coefficient** that lumps all stagnant species into a single equivalent medium, enabling a multicomponent problem to be solved using Fick-type binary expressions. For species A diffusing through a stagnant mixture of B and C: $$\boxed{\,\frac{1}{\mathcal{D}_{A,m}} = \frac{x_B}{\mathcal{D}_{AB}} + \frac{x_C}{\mathcal{D}_{AC}}\,}$$ where $x_B, x_C$ are the mole fractions of the stagnant species and $\mathcal{D}_{AB}, \mathcal{D}_{AC}$ are the respective Maxwell–Stefan binary diffusivities. --- ## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` **(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **Maxwell–Stefan equation** for species A in a ternary gas mixture of A, B, C. Clearly state all assumptions. Show that in the binary limit (C → 0), the equation reduces to **Fick's first law**. **(b) [7 marks]** Ammonia (A) is absorbed from a **stagnant mixture of N₂ (B) and H₂ (C)** at 298 K and 1 atm through a gas film of thickness 0.5 mm. The mole fraction of NH₃ at the gas–liquid interface is $x_{A,1} = 0.15$ and at the bulk gas is $x_{A,2} = 0.02$. The binary diffusivities are $\mathcal{D}_{AB} = 2.30 \times 10^{-5}$ m²·s⁻¹ and $\mathcal{D}_{AC} = 7.70 \times 10^{-5}$ m²·s⁻¹, with $x_B = x_C = 0.425$ each. Calculate the molar flux of A. --- ### Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks] **Assumptions:** - Ideal-gas behaviour, isothermal, isobaric. - No external body forces (gravity neglected). - One-dimensional steady-state diffusion. - No homogeneous chemical reaction. **Step 1 — Write the M–S equation for species A:** $$-\frac{dx_A}{dz} = \frac{x_B N_A - x_A N_B}{c \mathcal{D}_{AB}} + \frac{x_C N_A - x_A N_C}{c \mathcal{D}_{AC}}$$ **Step 2 — Binary limit:** Let the mole fraction of C approach zero, so $x_C \to 0$ and $x_A + x_B \to 1$. The second term vanishes. We apply the equimolar counter-diffusion constraint $N_A = -N_B$: $$-\frac{dx_A}{dz} = \frac{x_B N_A + x_A N_A}{c \mathcal{D}_{AB}} = \frac{(x_A + x_B) N_A}{c \mathcal{D}_{AB}} = \frac{N_A}{c \mathcal{D}_{AB}}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $N_A$:** $$N_A = -c \mathcal{D}_{AB} \frac{dx_A}{dz}$$ This is **Fick's first law** for a binary gas pair. *(3 marks for full derivation)* #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks] **Step 1 — Effective diffusivity (Wilke):** $$\frac{1}{\mathcal{D}_{A,m}} = \frac{0.425}{2.30 \times 10^{-5}} + \frac{0.425}{7.70 \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$= 1.8478 \times 10^{4} + 5.5195 \times 10^{3} = 2.3998 \times 10^{4} \text{ s/m}^{2}$$ $$\mathcal{D}_{A,m} = 4.166 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$ **Step 2 — Total molar concentration:** $$c = \frac{101325}{8.314 \times 298} = 40.89 \text{ mol/m}^{3}$$ **Step 3 — Log-mean inert fraction:** $$(1-x_A)_{\ln} = \frac{(1-0.02)-(1-0.15)}{\ln(0.98/0.85)} = \frac{0.13}{0.1422} = 0.914$$ **Step 4 — Molar flux of A:** $$N_A = \frac{c\,\mathcal{D}_{A,m}}{\delta} \ln\!\left( \frac{1 - x_{A,2}}{1 - x_{A,1}} \right)$$ $$N_A = \frac{40.89 \times 4.166 \times 10^{-5}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} \times 0.1422$$ $$N_A = 3.406 \times 0.1422 = 0.484 \text{ mol} \cdot \text{m}^{-2}\text{s}^{-1}$$ **Answer:** $N_A \approx 0.484$ mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ *(4 marks for numerical evaluation)* --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of **effective diffusivity** in multicomponent diffusion. Derive Wilke's equation for species A diffusing through a stagnant mixture of n-1 other gases. **(b) [7 marks]** Using the **Stefan-tube** experimental method, derive the relationship between the rate of evaporation of a liquid A into a stagnant gas B and the diffusion coefficient $\mathcal{D}_{AB}$. State clearly how this setup extends to multicomponent gas mixtures. --- ### Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — Concept and Derivation [7 marks] **Concept [2 marks]:** In multicomponent systems where only one species diffuses and the rest are stagnant, the analysis can be reduced to a binary (pseudo-binary) problem by defining an **effective diffusivity** $\mathcal{D}_{A,m}$ that captures the combined resistance offered by all stagnant species. **Derivation [5 marks]:** For species A diffusing through stagnant species B, C, ..., N, with $N_B = N_C = \cdots = 0$: From the flux decomposition $N_i = J_i + x_i N_{\text{tot}}$ and the stagnation condition, we obtain: $$J_A = (1-x_A) N_A, \quad J_j = -x_j N_A \text{ for all } j \neq A$$ Substituting into the M–S equation for A: $$-\frac{dx_A}{dz} = \sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq A}}^{n} \frac{x_j J_A - x_A J_j}{c \mathcal{D}_{Aj}} = \frac{N_A}{c} \sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq A}}^{n} \frac{x_j}{\mathcal{D}_{Aj}}$$ Comparing with Fick's law $N_A = -c\mathcal{D}_{A,m} \, dx_A/dz$: $$\boxed{\,\frac{1}{\mathcal{D}_{A,m}} = \sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq A}}^{n} \frac{x_j}{\mathcal{D}_{Aj}}\,}$$ #### Part (b) — Stefan-Tube Analysis [7 marks] **Setup [2 marks]:** A vertical tube of cross-sectional area $S$ and length $L$ contains a stagnant gas column B above a pool of liquid A. Vapor A evaporates and diffuses upward; gas B is stagnant. **Derivation [5 marks]:** With B stagnant ($N_B = 0$), the diffusive flux of A from the Stefan equation: $$N_A = \frac{c\,\mathcal{D}_{AB}}{L} \ln\!\left( \frac{1-x_{A,\text{top}}}{1-x_{A,\text{bottom}}} \right) = \frac{c\,\mathcal{D}_{AB}}{L} \ln\!\left( \frac{P - P_{A,\text{top}}}{P - P_{A,\text{bottom}}} \right)$$ The evaporation rate is $\dot{n}_A = N_A \cdot S$. Measuring $\dot{n}_A$ experimentally (by mass loss) and knowing $L$, $T$, $P$, and the partial pressures, $\mathcal{D}_{AB}$ can be calculated. **Extension to multicomponent mixtures:** When the stagnant gas B is replaced by a multicomponent inert mixture (B + C + D + ...), the same setup is used but $\mathcal{D}_{AB}$ is replaced by **Wilke's effective diffusivity** $\mathcal{D}_{A,m}$, evaluated at the log-mean composition of the inerts. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Forgetting to apply the constraint $J_A + J_B + \cdots = 0$** when using Fick's law in multicomponent systems — this costs full marks for derivation. > 2. **Using $\nabla x_A$ instead of $\nabla \ln(1-x_A)$** in the Stefan integration for non-equimolar counter-diffusion. Many students mistakenly write $N_A = c\mathcal{D}_{AB}(x_{A,1}-x_{A,2})/L$, which is *wrong* for one-way diffusion. > 3. **Evaluating $x_j$ at the wrong end of the film** when using Wilke's equation. Always use the **log-mean** mole fraction of the inerts across the diffusion path, not the inlet value alone. > 4. **Missing the assumption** that ideal-gas law applies ($c = P/RT$). This is mandatory in KTU gas-diffusion problems. > 5. **Confusing $J_i$ (diffusive flux) with $N_i$ (absolute flux)** — the Maxwell–Stefan equation is in $J_i$, while $N_i$ includes the convective component. --- ## Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ **Fick's first law** is valid only for **binary mixtures** at constant $c$ and equimolar counter-diffusion. - ✅ **Maxwell–Stefan (M–S) equations** are the rigorous multicomponent diffusion model, treating diffusion as **pairwise momentum exchange**. - ✅ M–S equation: $-\nabla x_i = \sum_{j \neq i} (x_j J_i - x_i J_j)/(c\mathcal{D}_{ij})$. - ✅ **Wilke's effective diffusivity** $\mathcal{D}_{i,m}$ converts a multicomponent problem into pseudo-binary: $1/\mathcal{D}_{i,m} = \sum_{j \neq i} x_j / \mathcal{D}_{ij}$. - ✅ **Stagnant-film (Stefan) integration** uses the **log-mean inert fraction** $(1-x_A)_{\ln}$. - ✅ Binary limit of M–S ↔ Fick's law (proved by setting $J_A + J_B = 0$). - ✅ For n components, there are $(n-1)$ independent M–S equations and $(n-1)$ independent fluxes. - ✅ Total concentration in ideal gases: $c = P/(RT)$. - ✅ Binary diffusivity scales as $\mathcal{D}_{ij} \propto T^{3/2}/P$ (Chapman–Enskog). - ✅ **Constraint**: $\sum_{i=1}^n J_i = 0$ (molar-average reference frame). - ✅ **Stefan tube** is the canonical experiment for measuring $\mathcal{D}_{AB}$; extends to multicomponent via Wilke's $\mathcal{D}_{i,m}$. - ✅ Common KTU 14-mark question patterns: (i) derive M–S and show binary limit; (ii) compute flux using Wilke's effective diffusivity; (iii) analyze Stefan-tube data.

topics.title: Molecular Diffusion in Liquids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT746 - ADVANCED HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Molecular Diffusion in Liquids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Molecular Diffusion in Liquids** is the transport mechanism by which individual molecules of a solute (species A) move through a stagnant or moving solvent (species B) under the influence of a **concentration gradient**, governed by **Fick's First Law of Diffusion**, and characterized quantitatively by the **Liquid-Phase Diffusivity, $D_{AB}$** (units: **m²/s** or **cm²/s**).

In the KTU 2024 scheme, molecular diffusion in liquids is treated as a **steady-state, one-dimensional** transport problem in the *z*-direction. The general molar flux of species A relative to a stationary reference frame is:

$$
N_A = -D_{AB}\,\frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A(N_A + N_B)
$$

where:
- $D_{AB}$ = **liquid-phase diffusion coefficient** of A in solvent B (m²/s)
- $C_A$ = molar concentration of A (kmol/m³)
- $x_A$ = mole fraction of A in the liquid phase
- $N_A, N_B$ = molar fluxes of A and B (kmol/m²·s)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students must master *two limiting cases* — **(i) Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMCD)** and **(ii) Diffusion of A through Stagnant B (one-way diffusion)** — as these form the backbone of every ESE numerical in Module 3.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Sugar Cube in Tea" Picture

Imagine dropping a cube of sugar into a still cup of hot tea **without stirring**:

- The sugar molecules on the cube's surface dissolve and begin drifting into regions of lower concentration (away from the cube).
- The tea is *stagnant* — the bulk solvent (water) does not flow. Only the dissolved sugar (A) moves.
- Over time, sweetness spreads uniformly because random molecular collisions push solute particles from high → low concentration.
- The **slower the random walk**, the **lower** the diffusivity. In a *viscous* solvent (cold honey vs. hot water), diffusion becomes nearly negligible.

> This "sugar-in-tea" picture is the **physical mental model** for $N_A$ through stagnant B. The two bulk concentrations $C_{A1}$ and $C_{A2}$ (across a film thickness $L$) replace the spatial gradient of sweetness in the cup.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Boltzmann Constant:** $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- **Typical $D_{AB}$ in liquids:** $10^{-10}$ to $10^{-9}$ m²/s (≈ **10,000× smaller** than gases)
- **Typical $D_{AB}$ in gases:** $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ m²/s
- **Solute molar volume at NBP, $V_A$:** cm³/g-mol (used in Wilke-Chang)
- **Solvent viscosity, $\mu_B$:** cP (centipoise) or Pa·s

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The theoretical treatment of molecular diffusion in liquids proceeds in **five structured logic steps**:

### Step 1 — Identify the Reference Frame
Choose between:
- **Stationary (molar-average) frame** → use $N_A$ directly with Fick's law
- **Mass-average frame** → use $j_A$ (mass flux relative to mass-average velocity)

For KTU numericals, we work in the **stationary (molar) frame**.

### Step 2 — Apply the Stagnancy Condition
- **EMCD case:** $N_A = -N_B$ (one mol A replaces one mol B at every point)
- **Stagnant B case:** $N_B = 0$ (solvent does not move; only A diffuses)

### Step 3 — Write Fick's First Law with Convective Correction
$$
N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{1 - x_A}\,\frac{dC_A}{dz}
$$

The term $\frac{1}{1-x_A}$ arises because the bulk flow carries A *along* with it, augmenting pure molecular transport.

### Step 4 — Integrate Across the Film of Thickness *L*
Boundary conditions: at $z=0$, $C_A = C_{A1}$ (or $x_{A1}$); at $z=L$, $C_A = C_{A2}$ (or $x_{A2}$).

### Step 5 — Introduce the **Log-Mean Concentration**
For convenience, the logarithmic mean is defined as:

$$
(1 - x_A)_{lm} = \frac{(1 - x_{A2}) - (1 - x_{A1})}{\ln\!\left[\frac{1 - x_{A2}}{1 - x_{A1}}\right]}
$$

This allows the flux equation to be written in a form that mirrors convective heat transfer ($q = h \cdot \Delta T$):

$$
N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,C_T}{L\,(1 - x_A)_{lm}}\,\bigl[(1 - x_{A2}) - (1 - x_{A1})\bigr]
$$

## 2.2 Why Liquids Behave Differently from Gases

| Property | Gas-Phase Diffusion | Liquid-Phase Diffusion |
|---|---|---|
| Magnitude of $D_{AB}$ | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ m²/s | $10^{-10}$ to $10^{-9}$ m²/s |
| Pressure dependence | $D_{AB} \propto 1/P$ | Independent of pressure |
| Temperature dependence | $D_{AB} \propto T^{3/2}$ | $D_{AB} \propto T / \mu_B$ |
| Density of solvent | Low | High (molecules tightly packed) |
| Theoretical basis | Kinetic theory of gases | Hydrodynamic / Eyring theory |

> [!NOTE]
> In liquids, molecules are **densely packed**, so the dominant resistance is the *viscous drag* between solute and solvent. This is why the **Stokes-Einstein** and **Wilke-Chang** correlations involve the solvent viscosity $\mu_B$ explicitly.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the SINGLE most important reference for Module 3 ESE questions.** Memorize every row.

| # | Governing Equation | Form | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fick's First Law (general) | $N_A = -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A(N_A + N_B)$ | Always — starting point |
| 2 | EMCD molar flux | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,C_T}{L}\,(x_{A1} - x_{A2})$ | When $N_A = -N_B$ |
| 3 | Stagnant B flux (log form) | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,C_T}{L}\,\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1 - x_{A2}}{1 - x_{A1}}\right]$ | When $N_B = 0$ |
| 4 | Stagnant B flux (LM form) | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,C_T}{L\,(1 - x_A)_{lm}}\,\bigl[(1 - x_{A2}) - (1 - x_{A1})\right]$ | Same as above, alternate form |
| 5 | Log-mean definition | $(1 - x_A)_{lm} = \dfrac{(1 - x_{A2}) - (1 - x_{A1})}{\ln[(1 - x_{A2})/(1 - x_{A1})]}$ | For simplification |
| 6 | Stokes-Einstein | $D_{AB} = \dfrac{kT}{6\pi \mu_B r_A}$ | Spherical solute in dilute solution |
| 7 | Wilke-Chang (practical) | $D_{AB} = \dfrac{7.4 \times 10^{-8}\,(\Phi_B M_B)^{0.5}\,T}{\mu_B\,V_A^{0.6}}$ | Engineering estimation |
| 8 | Diffusivity ratio rule | $\dfrac{D_{AB}\,\mu_B}{T} = \text{constant}$ | Compare two temperatures |
| 9 | Pseudo-steady Fick's 2nd | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\,\dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$ | Unsteady diffusion |
| 10 | Dimensionless flux correction | $\phi = \dfrac{N_A}{N_{A,\text{pure diff}}} = \dfrac{1}{1 - x_A}$ | Effect of bulk flow |

**Units Legend:** $D_{AB}$ in cm²/s, $T$ in K, $\mu_B$ in cP, $M_B$ in g/mol, $V_A$ in cm³/g-mol.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used in designing **solvent extraction columns** (e.g., extraction of aromatics from lube oil using sulfolane), **membrane-based separation**, **controlled drug release**, and **oxygen transport in blood** (hemoglobin kinetics).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Molar Flux of A Through Stagnant B (The KTU Workhorse)

**Starting Point (Fick's First Law in stationary frame):**

$$
N_A = -D_{AB}\,\frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A(N_A + N_B)
$$

**Step 1 — Apply staganancy condition for B:**

$$
N_B = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $N_B = 0$ into the general equation:**

$$
N_A = -D_{AB}\,\frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A\,N_A
$$

**Step 3 — Collect $N_A$ terms on the left side:**

$$
N_A - x_A N_A = -D_{AB}\,\frac{dC_A}{dz}
$$

**Step 4 — Factor the left-hand side:**

$$
N_A(1 - x_A) = -D_{AB}\,\frac{dC_A}{dz}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $N_A$ explicitly:**

$$
N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{1 - x_A}\,\frac{dC_A}{dz}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert from $C_A$ to $x_A$ using $C_A = x_A\,C_T$ (constant $T$, $P$):**

Differentiating both sides: $dC_A = C_T\,dx_A$. (Here $C_T$ is total molar concentration, treated as constant for dilute liquid systems.)

**Step 7 — Substitute into the separated flux equation:**

$$
N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{1 - x_A}\,C_T\,\frac{dx_A}{dz}
$$

**Step 8 — Separate variables — $N_A$ and $z$ on opposite sides, $x_A$ on the other:**

$$
N_A\,dz = -D_{AB}\,C_T\,\frac{dx_A}{1 - x_A}
$$

**Step 9 — Integrate from $z = 0$ (where $x_A = x_{A1}$) to $z = L$ (where $x_A = x_{A2}$):**

$$
N_A \int_{0}^{L} dz = -D_{AB}\,C_T \int_{x_{A1}}^{x_{A2}} \frac{dx_A}{1 - x_A}
$$

**Step 10 — Evaluate the left side:**

$$
N_A \cdot L = -D_{AB}\,C_T \int_{x_{A1}}^{x_{A2}} \frac{dx_A}{1 - x_A}
$$

**Step 11 — Evaluate the right side (the standard integral $\int \frac{du}{u} = \ln u$ with sign flip because $u = 1 - x_A$, $du = -dx_A$):**

$$
N_A \cdot L = -D_{AB}\,C_T \left[ -\ln(1 - x_A) \right]_{x_{A1}}^{x_{A2}}
$$

$$
N_A \cdot L = D_{AB}\,C_T \left[ \ln(1 - x_{A2}) - \ln(1 - x_{A1}) \right]
$$

**Step 12 — Apply the logarithm subtraction rule $\ln a - \ln b = \ln(a/b)$:**

$$
N_A \cdot L = D_{AB}\,C_T \cdot \ln\!\left[\frac{1 - x_{A2}}{1 - x_{A1}}\right]
$$

**Step 13 — Final flux equation for stagnant B:**

$$
\boxed{\,N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,C_T}{L}\,\ln\!\left[\frac{1 - x_{A2}}{1 - x_{A1}}\right]\,}
$$

**Step 14 — Conversion to log-mean form (multiply numerator and denominator by the LM factor):**

Define:

$$
(1 - x_A)_{lm} = \frac{(1 - x_{A2}) - (1 - x_{A1})}{\ln[(1 - x_{A2})/(1 - x_{A1})]}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\ln\!\left[\frac{1 - x_{A2}}{1 - x_{A1}}\right] = \frac{(1 - x_{A2}) - (1 - x_{A1})}{(1 - x_A)_{lm}}
$$

**Step 15 — Substituting back into Step 13:**

$$
\boxed{\,N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,C_T}{L\,(1 - x_A)_{lm}}\,\bigl[(1 - x_{A2}) - (1 - x_{A1})\bigr]\,}
$$

This completes the rigorous derivation. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMCD) Flux

**Starting condition:** $N_A = -N_B$, therefore $N_A + N_B = 0$.

**Step 1 — Substitute into the general Fick's equation:**

$$
N_A = -D_{AB}\,\frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A(0)
$$

**Step 2 — Convective term vanishes:**

$$
N_A = -D_{AB}\,\frac{dC_A}{dz}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mole fraction form ($dC_A = C_T\,dx_A$):**

$$
N_A = -D_{AB}\,C_T\,\frac{dx_A}{dz}
$$

**Step 4 — Separate variables:**

$$
N_A\,dz = -D_{AB}\,C_T\,dx_A
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from $z = 0$ to $z = L$:**

$$
N_A \cdot L = D_{AB}\,C_T(x_{A1} - x_{A2})
$$

**Step 6 — Final result:**

$$
\boxed{\,N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,C_T}{L}(x_{A1} - x_{A2})\,}
$$

This is mathematically equivalent to *Fourier's law for heat conduction*, justifying the **analogy between mass and heat transfer**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Stokes-Einstein Equation (Hydrodynamic Basis)

**Physical Premise:** A spherical solute particle of radius $r_A$ experiences a viscous drag force as it moves through a continuum solvent of viscosity $\mu_B$.

**Step 1 — Stokes' drag law (steady motion of sphere in viscous fluid):**

$$
F_{\text{drag}} = 6\pi \mu_B r_A v
$$

**Step 2 — Terminal (drift) velocity from balance with driving force:**

For a single molecule in a concentration gradient, the net driving force is the chemical-potential gradient equivalent to:

$$
F_{\text{diff}} = kT \cdot \frac{1}{C_A}\frac{dC_A}{dz} \cdot \text{(per molecule area)}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate drag and driving force, solve for drift velocity $v$:**

$$
v = \frac{kT}{6\pi \mu_B r_A}\,\frac{1}{C_A}\frac{dC_A}{dz}
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize $v \cdot C_A = -N_A$ (molar flux = velocity × concentration, with sign convention):**

$$
-N_A = \frac{kT}{6\pi \mu_B r_A}\,\frac{dC_A}{dz}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with Fick's first law $N_A = -D_{AB}(dC_A/dz)$:**

$$
D_{AB} = \frac{kT}{6\pi \mu_B r_A}
$$

**Step 6 — Final form:**

$$
\boxed{\,D_{AB} = \frac{kT}{6\pi \mu_B r_A}\,}
$$

This is the **Stokes-Einstein equation**, valid for **spherical, non-interacting solutes larger than solvent molecules**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Derivation D — Wilke-Chang Equation (Empirical, Most Used)

**Wilke-Chang (1955)** is a semi-empirical modification of Stokes-Einstein. Starting from the proportionality $D_{AB} \propto T/(\mu_B V_A^{0.6})$:

**Step 1 — Dimensional analysis and empirical fitting of experimental data:**

For organic solutes in organic solvents, Wilke and Chang found:

$$
D_{AB} = \frac{7.4 \times 10^{-8}\,(\Phi_B M_B)^{0.5}\,T}{\mu_B\,V_A^{0.6}}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify each term:**

- $7.4 \times 10^{-8}$ = empirical constant for cm²/s units
- $\Phi_B$ = **association factor** of solvent B (dimensionless): water = 2.6, methanol = 1.9, ethanol = 1.5, benzene = 1.0
- $M_B$ = molecular weight of solvent B (g/mol)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $\mu_B$ = viscosity of solvent B (cP)
- $V_A$ = molar volume of solute A at its normal boiling point (cm³/g-mol) — obtained from **Le Bas molar volume table**

**Step 3 — Validity range:**

- $D_{AB}$ in cm²/s; $D_{AB} \in (0.5 - 3) \times 10^{-5}\,\frac{\mu_B}{V_A^{0.6}}$ for most systems
- Average absolute error ≈ **10–15%** for non-aqueous systems

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** *"**7-4 Phi-M-** over **mu-V**"* — i.e., 7.4 × $\sqrt{\Phi_B M_B}$ in the numerator; $\mu_B V_A^{0.6}$ in the denominator.

---

## 3.5 Derivation E — Pseudo-Steady-State Diffusion with First-Order Reaction

When A diffuses through a liquid film of thickness $L$ and undergoes a **first-order homogeneous reaction** (rate = $k_1 C_A$):

**Step 1 — Mass balance on a thin slab of thickness $dz$:**

$$
\frac{d N_A}{dz} + k_1 C_A = 0
$$

(Plus sign: A is being consumed by reaction.)

**Step 2 — Substitute $N_A = -D_{AB}(dC_A/dz)$:**

$$
-D_{AB}\,\frac{d^2 C_A}{dz^2} + k_1 C_A = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange into a standard ODE:**

$$
\frac{d^2 C_A}{dz^2} = \frac{k_1}{D_{AB}} C_A
$$

**Step 4 — Define $m^2 = k_1 / D_{AB}$:**

$$
\frac{d^2 C_A}{dz^2} = m^2 C_A
$$

**Step 5 — General solution:**

$$
C_A(z) = A_1 \cosh(mz) + A_2 \sinh(mz)
$$

**Step 6 — Apply BCs: $C_A = C_{A1}$ at $z=0$; $C_A = C_{A2}$ at $z=L$:**

- $A_1 = C_{A1}$
- $A_2 = \dfrac{C_{A2} - C_{A1}\cosh(mL)}{\sinh(mL)}$

**Step 7 — Flux at $z = 0$ (entering the film):**

$$
N_A\big|_{z=0} = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz}\bigg|_{z=0} = -D_{AB}\,m\,A_2
$$

**Step 8 — Final result:**

$$
\boxed{\,N_A\big|_{z=0} = \frac{D_{AB}\,m}{\tanh(mL)}\,\left[C_{A1} - \frac{C_{A2}}{\cosh(mL)}\right]\,}
$$

**Limiting cases:**
- $k_1 \to 0$ (no reaction): $N_A \to (D_{AB}/L)(C_{A1} - C_{A2})$ ✓ (recovers Fick's law)
- $k_1 \to \infty$: $C_{A1} - C_{A2}/\cosh(mL) \to 0$ if $C_{A2} = 0$, flux is reaction-limited

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **diffusion-with-reaction** form is heavily tested in **chemical reaction engineering** modules and the KTU board exam as a 14-mark problem.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Derivation | Key Result | Typical Use | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|---|
| A (Stagnant B) | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} C_T}{L} \ln\!\left[\dfrac{1-x_{A2}}{1-x_{A1}}\right]$ | Absorption, liquid–liquid extraction | Forgetting the $\ln$ term and using arithmetic mean |
| B (EMCD) | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} C_T}{L}(x_{A1} - x_{A2})$ | Distillation (vapor–liquid at interface) | Adding a $(1 - x_A)_{lm}$ correction that does not apply |
| C (Stokes-Einstein) | $D_{AB} = \dfrac{kT}{6\pi \mu_B r_A}$ | Theoretical estimation for spheres | Using radius in Å instead of meters without unit conversion |
| D (Wilke-Chang) | $D_{AB} = \dfrac{7.4\times 10^{-8}(\Phi_B M_B)^{0.5}T}{\mu_B V_A^{0.6}}$ | All non-electrolyte liquid systems | Using $\mu_B$ in Pa·s instead of cP |
| E (Diff + Rx) | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,m}{\tanh(mL)}\!\left[C_{A1} - \dfrac{C_{A2}}{\cosh(mL)}\right]$ | Gas absorption with reaction | Forgetting the $\tanh$ function |

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    E --> F{Select Stagnancy Condition}
```

```mermaid
    D -- No Arbitrary --> F[Step 4b Use Wilke-Chang DAB equals 7.4E-8 times PhiB MB to the 0.5 times T over mu B times VA to the 0.6]

    F --> G

```
