# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question, 3 Marks]**

**State Fick's First Law of Molecular Diffusion. How is it modified for diffusion of A through stagnant B?**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

Fick's First Law states that the molar flux of a species by molecular diffusion is proportional to its concentration gradient:

$$
N_A = -D_{AB}\,\frac{dC_A}{dz}
$$

For **diffusion of A through stagnant B** (where $N_B = 0$), the convective term $x_A(N_A + N_B)$ must be retained. The modified form becomes:

$$
N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{1 - x_A}\,\frac{dC_A}{dz}
$$

After integration:

$$
N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,C_T}{L}\,\ln\!\left[\frac{1 - x_{A2}}{1 - x_{A1}}\right]
$$

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question, 3 Marks]**

**Differentiate between molecular diffusion in liquids and gases. Give two key distinctions.**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

| Sl. No. | Liquids | Gases |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Diffusivity is very small: $10^{-10}$ to $10^{-9}$ m²/s | Diffusivity is large: $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ m²/s |
| 2 | Diffusivity is **independent of pressure** | Diffusivity varies **inversely with pressure** ($D_{AB} \propto 1/P$) |
| 3 | Depends on solvent viscosity $\mu_B$ (Stokes-Einstein / Wilke-Chang) | Predicted by **kinetic theory of gases** (Hirschfelder) |
| 4 | Molecules are densely packed | Molecules are far apart |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### 🔷 Question A — Stagnant-B Diffusion Through a Liquid Film **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, 14 Marks]**

A liquid mixture of ethanol (A) and water (B) is in contact with a **stagnant water film** of thickness $L = 2\,\text{mm}$. At the gas–liquid interface ($z = 0$), the mole fraction of ethanol is $x_{A1} = 0.18$, and at the bottom of the film ($z = L$), $x_{A2} = 0.05$. The total molar concentration is $C_T = 55.5\,\text{kmol/m}^3$ (essentially water density). The liquid-phase diffusivity is $D_{AB} = 1.2 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$.

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Derive the expression for the molar flux of ethanol $N_A$ diffusing through stagnant water. Clearly state the assumptions made.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the steady-state molar flux $N_A$ in kmol/(m²·s). Compare this with the EMCD result.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state, one-dimensional diffusion
2. Solvent B (water) is stagnant, i.e., $N_B = 0$
3. Constant temperature and pressure
4. $D_{AB}$ is independent of concentration
5. Constant total molar concentration $C_T$

**Starting Equation:**

$$
N_A = -D_{AB}\,\frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A\,N_A
$$

(obtained by substituting $N_B = 0$ into the general Fick's law.)

**Rearranging:**

$$
N_A(1 - x_A) = -D_{AB}\,\frac{dC_A}{dz}
$$

Substituting $C_A = x_A C_T$:

$$
N_A(1 - x_A) = -D_{AB}\,C_T\,\frac{dx_A}{dz}
$$

**Separating variables and integrating:**

$$
N_A \int_0^L dz = -D_{AB}\,C_T \int_{x_{A1}}^{x_{A2}} \frac{dx_A}{1 - x_A}
$$

**Evaluating:**

$$
N_A L = D_{AB}\,C_T \ln\!\left[\frac{1 - x_{A2}}{1 - x_{A1}}\right]
$$

**Final Expression:**

$$
\boxed{\,N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,C_T}{L}\,\ln\!\left[\frac{1 - x_{A2}}{1 - x_{A1}}\right]\,}
$$

[7 marks — 2 for assumptions, 3 for derivation, 2 for final form]

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Substitute values (all in SI units):**

- $D_{AB} = 1.2 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- $C_T = 55.5\,\text{kmol/m}^3$
- $L = 2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$
- $x_{A1} = 0.18$, $x_{A2} = 0.05$

**Step 2 — Compute the log term:**

$$
\frac{1 - x_{A2}}{1 - x_{A1}} = \frac{1 - 0.05}{1 - 0.18} = \frac{0.95}{0.82} = 1.15854
$$

$$
\ln(1.15854) = 0.14718
$$

**Step 3 — Compute flux:**

$$
N_A = \frac{(1.2 \times 10^{-9}) \times (55.5)}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \times 0.14718
$$

$$
N_A = \frac{6.66 \times 10^{-8}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \times 0.14718
$$

$$
N_A = 3.33 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.14718
$$

$$
\boxed{\,N_A = 4.902 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — EMCD Comparison:**

For EMCD: $N_A^{EMCD} = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,C_T}{L}(x_{A1} - x_{A2})$

$$
N_A^{EMCD} = \frac{(1.2 \times 10^{-9})(55.5)}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \times (0.18 - 0.05)
$$

$$
N_A^{EMCD} = 3.33 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.13 = 4.329 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}
$$

**Step 5 — Ratio (Stagnant B / EMCD):**

$$
\frac{N_A^{\text{stagnant}}}{N_A^{EMCD}} = \frac{4.902 \times 10^{-6}}{4.329 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.132
$$

**Inference:** The stagnant-B flux is **~13% higher** than EMCD because the bulk flow of A (in the direction of diffusion) supplements the molecular flux in the stagnant case. The factor $1/(1 - x_A)_{lm}$ accounts for this enhancement.

[7 marks — 2 for substitution, 2 for arithmetic, 2 for EMCD comparison, 1 for inference]

---

### 🔷 Question B — Wilke-Chang Diffusivity Estimation **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, 14 Marks]**

Estimate the liquid-phase diffusivity of **acetic acid (A)** in **benzene (B)** at **25 °C** using the **Wilke-Chang equation**. The viscosity of benzene at 25 °C is $\mu_B = 0.60\,\text{cP}$. The molar mass of benzene is $M_B = 78.1\,\text{g/mol}$. The molar volume of acetic acid at its normal boiling point is $V_A = 95.1\,\text{cm}^3/\text{g·mol}$. The association factor for benzene is $\Phi_B = 1.0$.

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** State the Wilke-Chang equation and identify each term with units.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Calculate $D_{AB}$ in cm²/s and m²/s, and comment on whether the value is reasonable for a liquid system.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Equation and Terms [7 Marks]**

**Wilke-Chang Equation:**

$$
D_{AB} = \frac{7.4 \times 10^{-8}\,(\Phi_B M_B)^{0.5}\,T}{\mu_B\,V_A^{0.6}}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Value (this problem) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D_{AB}$ | Diffusivity of A in B | ? | cm²/s |
| $\Phi_B$ | Association factor of solvent B | 1.0 | dimensionless |
| $M_B$ | Molecular weight of solvent B | 78.1 | g/mol |
| $T$ | Absolute temperature | 298.15 | K |
| $\mu_B$ | Viscosity of solvent B | 0.60 | cP |
| $V_A$ | Molar volume of solute A at NBP | 95.1 | cm³/g·mol |

[7 marks — 2 for equation, 5 for table of terms with units]

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute $(\Phi_B M_B)^{0.5}$:**

$$
(1.0 \times 78.1)^{0.5} = (78.1)^{0.5} = 8.8374
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $V_A^{0.6}$:**

$$
(95.1)^{0.6}
$$

Using logarithms: $\ln(95.1) = 4.5541$, so $0.6 \times 4.5541 = 2.7325$, hence:

$$
e^{2.7325} = 15.36
$$

(Equivalently, $95.1^{0.6} \approx 15.36$.)

**Step 3 — Numerator:**

$$
7.4 \times 10^{-8} \times 8.8374 \times 298.15
$$

$$
= 7.4 \times 10^{-8} \times 2634.7
$$

$$
= 1.9497 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 4 — Denominator:**

$$
0.60 \times 15.36 = 9.216
$$

**Step 5 — Final value of $D_{AB}$:**

$$
D_{AB} = \frac{1.9497 \times 10^{-4}}{9.216} = 2.116 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{cm}^2/\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,D_{AB} \approx 2.12 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{cm}^2/\text{s} = 2.12 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Reasonableness check:**

Typical $D_{AB}$ in liquids is $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}\,\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$. The computed value $2.12 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$ lies within this range. **The result is physically reasonable.**

For comparison, the **experimental value** reported in literature for acetic acid in benzene at 25 °C is approximately $2.09 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$, giving an error of only ~1.2%. The Wilke-Chang correlation is highly accurate for this system.

[7 marks — 2 for Step 1–2, 2 for Steps 3–4, 2 for final value, 1 for reasonableness]

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on this topic (Dec 2023 / June 2024 trends):**
>
> 1. **Units mismatch (loss: 1–2 marks):** Using $\mu_B$ in Pa·s instead of cP in the Wilke-Chang equation gives a value **1000× too small**. Always confirm: **cP is the Wilke-Chang standard unit.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the convective term (loss: 2–3 marks):** Writing $N_A = -D_{AB}(dC_A/dz)$ without $x_A(N_A + N_B)$ in stagnant-B problems. The convective term IS the entire reason for the logarithmic flux equation.
>
> 3. **Wrong log-mean evaluation (loss: 1 mark):** Using arithmetic mean $(1 - x_{A1} + 1 - x_{A2})/2$ instead of the true logarithmic mean.
>
> 4. **Confusing $r_A$ vs $V_A$ in Stokes-Einstein vs Wilke-Chang (loss: 1 mark):** $r_A$ is the molecular radius (m); $V_A$ is molar volume (cm³/g-mol). They are not interchangeable.
>
> 5. **Sign convention error (loss: 1 mark):** Forgetting to put a negative sign when integrating from $x_{A1}$ to $x_{A2}$ if $x_{A1} > x_{A2}$ (flux should be positive in the $+z$ direction).
>
> 6. **EMCD applied wrongly (loss: 2 marks):** Using EMCD equation in **absorption** problems where the solvent is genuinely stagnant. EMCD applies to **distillation interfaces**; absorption uses stagnant-B.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Fick's First Law:** $N_A = -D_{AB}(dC_A/dz) + x_A(N_A + N_B)$
- [x] **EMCD case** ($N_A = -N_B$): Use the **linear** flux equation $N_A = (D_{AB}C_T/L)(x_{A1} - x_{A2})$
- [x] **Stagnant-B case** ($N_B = 0$): Use the **logarithmic** flux equation with $(1-x_A)_{lm}$
- [x] **Log-mean** is the *only* valid averaging method for stagnant-B problems
- [x] **Stokes-Einstein** applies to *spherical*, *large* solutes in *dilute* solutions
- [x] **Wilke-Chang** is the *engineering workhorse*; remember units of $\mu_B$ are **cP**, $V_A$ are **cm³/g-mol**
- [x] **Association factor $\Phi_B$:** water = 2.6, methanol = 1.9, ethanol = 1.5, non-polar solvents = 1.0
- [x] **Diffusivity rule:** $D_{AB} \mu_B / T = $ constant (use for temperature extrapolation)
- [x] **Pressure** has negligible effect on liquid $D_{AB}$
- [x] **Typical $D_{AB}$** in liquids: $10^{-10}$ to $10^{-9}$ m²/s — **always quote powers of 10 correctly**
- [x] **Diffusion + first-order reaction** uses the **hyperbolic solution** with $\tanh$ and $\cosh$
- [x] **Mass transfer resistance** $R = L/(D_{AB} C_T)$ in stagnant B; $R = L/D_{AB}$ in EMCD (on molar basis)
- [x] **KTU board pattern:** Part A = definition/numerical on Wilke-Chang; Part B = full derivation + numerical on stagnant B (14 marks = 7 derivation + 7 calculation)
- [x] **Bulk flow correction factor:** $\phi = 1/(1 - x_A)$ — always $> 1$, meaning **convection aids diffusion** in stagnant-B
- [x] **Common SI trap:** $1\,\text{cm}^2/\text{s} = 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ — convert Wilke-Chang output to m²/s when applying it to SI-based flux equations

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** *"SLeS"* — **S**tagant-B uses **L**og, **E**MCD uses **S**imple linear equation. If you remember "SLeS", you will never confuse the two cases in the exam hall.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diffusivity in Porous Solids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT746 - ADVANCED HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Diffusivity in Porous Solids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In mass transfer operations, most industrially relevant reactions and separations involve **porous catalysts**, **membranes**, **drying solids**, and **adsorbents** where the diffusing species must travel through the intricate void network of a solid matrix. The transport of mass through such a structure is quantified by a single effective transport parameter called the **Effective Diffusivity**, denoted as $D_{eff}$ (or $D_{eA}$ for species A).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effective Diffusivity ($D_{eff}$):** It is defined as the proportionality constant that relates the molar flux of a diffusing species through the porous solid to the concentration gradient of that species, accounting for the geometric and structural characteristics of the porous medium (porosity and tortuosity).

Mathematically, the diffusive flux per unit total cross-sectional area in a porous solid is expressed as:

$$N_A = -D_{eff} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

where $C_A$ is the concentration of species A per unit volume of the porous solid, and $z$ is the spatial coordinate along the direction of diffusion.

### 1.2 The Three Critical Structural Parameters of a Porous Solid

A porous solid is characterized by three geometric descriptors that govern its mass transport behavior:

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Porosity | $\varepsilon$ | Fraction of void (empty) volume in the solid | 0.0 – 1.0 |
| Tortuosity | $\tau$ | Ratio of actual path length to straight-line length of the pore | 1.5 – 10 |
| Pore Diameter | $d_p$ | Average diameter of the pore channels | 0.5 nm – 50 µm |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: A Crowd Escaping a Maze

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "People Evacuating a Building":**
> Imagine 1000 people trying to exit a building through a single hallway. The "porosity" is analogous to the width of the hallway (a wider hallway = more people can pass per second = higher $\varepsilon$). The "tortuosity" is analogous to the twists and turns of the hallway — if people must walk 50 meters of zig-zag corridor to cover a straight-line distance of 10 meters, the tortuosity is 5. A person walking the maze covers a longer path, takes more time, and the **effective flow rate decreases**. Similarly, in a porous solid, molecules must navigate tortuous, winding paths through the void space, drastically reducing the effective transport rate compared to free-space (open) diffusion.

### 1.4 Why "Effective" Diffusivity?

In free gas or liquid phase (no solid obstruction), the molecular diffusivity $D_{AB}$ is well-defined by the Chapman-Enskog or Stokes-Einstein equations. However, in a porous solid, the actual diffusion coefficient is **always lower** than the free-space value because:

- Only a fraction $\varepsilon$ of the cross-section is available for flow.
- The path length is longer by a factor $\tau$.
- Wall collisions may dominate over molecule-molecule collisions in narrow pores (Knudsen effect).

Thus, the **effective diffusivity** bundles all these microstructural resistances into a single, lumped, measurable transport coefficient.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Diffusion Regimes Inside Porous Pores

Depending on the relative magnitude of the **pore diameter** ($d_p$) and the **mean free path of the gas molecules** ($\lambda$), three distinct diffusion regimes arise:

#### Regime 1: Bulk (Molecular) Diffusion
- **Condition:** $\lambda \ll d_p$ (pore is much larger than molecular mean free path)
- **Mechanism:** Molecule-molecule collisions dominate; molecule-wall collisions are negligible.
- **Governing Coefficient:** Molecular diffusivity $D_{AB}$
- **Physical Picture:** Behaves like diffusion in open space, but constrained by the pore geometry.

#### Regime 2: Knudsen Diffusion
- **Condition:** $\lambda \gg d_p$ (pore is smaller than molecular mean free path)
- **Mechanism:** Molecule-wall collisions dominate; molecule-molecule collisions are negligible.
- **Governing Coefficient:** Knudsen diffusivity $D_{KA}$
- **Physical Picture:** Each molecule bounces independently off the pore walls, with no "awareness" of other molecules.

#### Regime 3: Transition (Combined) Diffusion
- **Condition:** $\lambda \approx d_p$
- **Mechanism:** Both molecule-molecule and molecule-wall collisions are significant.
- **Governing Coefficient:** Combined diffusivity via the **Bosanquet equation**.

### 2.2 Knudsen Diffusivity: Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Knudsen Diffusivity** is the cornerstone of porous-solid mass transfer. It originates from kinetic theory applied to a single molecule bouncing off flat, parallel walls separated by distance $d_p$.

**Step 1:** A molecule of mass $m$ at temperature $T$ has an average thermal speed $\bar{c}$:

$$\bar{c} = \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}}$$

**Step 2:** The mean free path in Knudsen regime is limited by the pore diameter, not by intermolecular spacing:

$$\lambda_K = d_p$$

**Step 3:** Combining these, the Knudsen diffusivity is:

$$D_{KA} = \frac{1}{3} d_p \bar{c} = \frac{d_p}{3} \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M_A}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Knudsen diffusivity depends **only on temperature, molecular weight, and pore size** — it is independent of total system pressure. This is a unique fingerprint that distinguishes it from bulk molecular diffusion $D_{AB}$, which scales as $1/P$.

### 2.3 Effective Diffusivity in Porous Solids — The Master Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a binary gas mixture (A + B) diffusing through an isothermal, isobaric porous solid, the effective diffusivity is given by:

$$D_{eff,A} = \frac{\varepsilon}{\tau} \left( \frac{1}{D_{KA,A}} + \frac{1}{D_{AB}} \right)^{-1} = \frac{\varepsilon}{\tau} \left[ \frac{D_{KA,A} \cdot D_{AB}}{D_{KA,A} + D_{AB}} \right]$$

This is the **harmonic-mean form of the Bosanquet equation** modified for porous media. The $\varepsilon / \tau$ term is often called the **structural factor** or **tortuosity factor**.

For the limiting cases:
- **Large pores** ($D_{KA} \gg D_{AB}$): $D_{eff,A} \to \frac{\varepsilon}{\tau} D_{AB}$ → Bulk diffusion controls
- **Small pores** ($D_{KA} \ll D_{AB}$): $D_{eff,A} \to \frac{\varepsilon}{\tau} D_{KA}$ → Knudsen diffusion controls

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Key Variables | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Effective Diffusivity (Binary) | $D_{eff} = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{\tau}\left(\dfrac{1}{D_{KA}}+\dfrac{1}{D_{AB}}\right)^{-1}$ | $\varepsilon, \tau, D_{KA}, D_{AB}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 2 | Knudsen Diffusivity | $D_{KA} = \dfrac{d_p}{3}\sqrt{\dfrac{8RT}{\pi M_A}}$ | $d_p, T, M_A$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 3 | Molecular Diffusivity (Chapman-Enskog) | $D_{AB} = 0.001858 \dfrac{T^{3/2}}{P \sigma_{AB}^2 \Omega_{D}} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{M_A}+\dfrac{1}{M_B}}$ | $T, P, \sigma_{AB}, M_A, M_B$ | $\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 4 | Combined Diffusivity (Bosanquet) | $D_{c} = \left(\dfrac{1}{D_{KA}}+\dfrac{1}{D_{AB}}\right)^{-1}$ | $D_{KA}, D_{AB}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 5 | Knudsen Number Criterion | $Kn = \dfrac{\lambda}{d_p}$ | $\lambda, d_p$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Mean Free Path of Gas | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \pi d_m^2 P}$ | $T, d_m, P$ | m |
| 7 | Flux in Porous Solid | $N_A = -D_{eff} \dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ | $D_{eff}, C_A$ | $\text{mol}/(\text{m}^2\cdot\text{s})$ |
| 8 | Porosity | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{V_{void}}{V_{total}}$ | $V_{void}, V_{total}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Tortuosity | $\tau = \dfrac{L_{actual}}{L_{straight}}$ | $L_{actual}, L_{straight}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Modified Flux Form (per void area) | $N_A = -\dfrac{\varepsilon}{\tau} D_{c} \dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ | $\varepsilon, \tau, D_c, C_A$ | $\text{mol}/(\text{m}^2\cdot\text{s})$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Heterogeneous Catalysis:** Predicting reaction rates in catalyst pellets (e.g., catalytic cracking in petroleum refining).
- **Gas Separation Membranes:** Designing ceramic and polymeric membranes for $O_2/N_2$ or $CO_2/CH_4$ separation.
- **Drying of Porous Materials:** Modeling moisture removal from food, wood, ceramics, and pharmaceutical granules.
- **Fuel Cells:** Predicting transport of $H_2$, $O_2$, and water vapor through porous electrodes.
- **Activated Carbon Adsorption:** Estimating breakthrough curves in industrial scrubbers.
- **Geological Carbon Sequestration:** Modeling $CO_2$ migration through reservoir rocks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Knudsen Diffusivity from Kinetic Theory of Gases

> [!IMPORTANT]
> We start with a single gas molecule of species A, mass $m_A$, confined between two parallel flat plates separated by a distance equal to the pore diameter $d_p$. We assume the gas is dilute enough that the molecule collides only with the walls (Knudsen regime).

**Step 1 — Average molecular speed from Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution:**

For a Maxwell-Boltzmann gas at thermal equilibrium, the average speed (not RMS speed) of a molecule is given by:

$$\bar{c} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k_B T}{\pi m_A}} = \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M_A}}$$

Here, the conversion $k_B / m_A = R / M_A$ has been used (where $R$ is the universal gas constant and $M_A$ is the molar mass in kg/kmol).

**Step 2 — Defining the Knudsen mean free path:**

In the Knudsen regime, the dominant collision is with the wall, so the mean free path is set by the pore diameter:

$$\lambda_K = d_p$$

This is a geometric constraint, not a thermal one. Molecule-molecule collisions are assumed to be absent.

**Step 3 — Relationship between diffusivity, mean free path, and speed:**

For one-dimensional diffusion, kinetic theory gives a general relationship:

$$D = \frac{1}{3} \bar{c} \lambda$$

This is a universal result linking macroscopic diffusivity to microscopic molecular motion. The factor 1/3 arises from averaging over a 3-D isotropic distribution of velocities and projecting onto a single axis.

**Step 4 — Substituting Knudsen-specific values:**

Plugging the Knudsen mean free path $\lambda_K = d_p$ and the average speed into the general equation:

$$D_{KA} = \frac{1}{3} \bar{c} \, d_p$$

$$D_{KA} = \frac{1}{3} d_p \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M_A}}$$

$$\boxed{D_{KA} = \frac{d_p}{3} \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M_A}}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** Since $R$ and $\pi$ are constants, $D_{KA}$ is a function of only three operational parameters: pore size, temperature, and molecular weight. Notably, **$D_{KA}$ is independent of total system pressure** — a key signature used experimentally to identify when Knudsen flow dominates.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: The Bosanquet Equation for Combined Diffusion

In the transition regime, both bulk and Knudsen mechanisms act **in series**. The molecule must first travel ballistically (Knudsen) and then bulk-collide (molecular). The two diffusional resistances add, leading to the **Bosanquet relation**:

**Step 1 — Resistance analogy:**

In an electrical circuit with two resistors in series, the total resistance is the sum:

$$R_{total} = R_1 + R_2$$

For diffusion, the "resistance" is the reciprocal of the diffusivity. So:

$$\frac{1}{D_{combined}} = \frac{1}{D_{AB}} + \frac{1}{D_{KA}}$$

**Step 2 — Inverting the sum:**

$$D_{combined} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{D_{AB}} + \dfrac{1}{D_{KA}}}$$

**Step 3 — Common-denominator manipulation:**

$$D_{combined} = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot D_{KA}}{D_{AB} + D_{KA}}$$

**Step 4 — Embedding into porous solid geometry:**

The pore geometry (porosity $\varepsilon$ and tortuosity $\tau$) acts as a multiplicative attenuation on the intrinsic combined diffusivity:

$$D_{eff} = \frac{\varepsilon}{\tau} \cdot \frac{D_{AB} \cdot D_{KA}}{D_{AB} + D_{KA}}$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check (asymptotic limits):**

- If $D_{KA} \gg D_{AB}$: $D_{eff} \to \dfrac{\varepsilon}{\tau} D_{AB}$ (bulk-controlled, large pores)
- If $D_{KA} \ll D_{AB}$: $D_{eff} \to \dfrac{\varepsilon}{\tau} D_{KA}$ (Knudsen-controlled, small pores)

---

### 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Standard Numerical)

**Problem:** Helium ($M_A = 4 \, g/mol$) diffuses through a porous catalyst pellet with average pore diameter $d_p = 100 \, nm$, porosity $\varepsilon = 0.45$, and tortuosity $\tau = 2.5$. The bulk molecular diffusivity of He in air is $D_{AB} = 7.2 \times 10^{-5} \, m^2/s$ at the operating conditions of $T = 400 \, K$ and $P = 1 \, atm$. Calculate the effective diffusivity of He in the pellet.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Knudsen diffusivity:**

$$D_{KA} = \frac{d_p}{3} \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M_A}}$$

$$D_{KA} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-9}}{3} \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 8.314 \times 400}{\pi \times 0.004}}$$

$$D_{KA} = \frac{10^{-7}}{3} \sqrt{\frac{26604.8}{0.012566}}$$

$$D_{KA} = \frac{10^{-7}}{3} \sqrt{2.117 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$D_{KA} = \frac{10^{-7}}{3} \times 1455.0 = 4.85 \times 10^{-5} \, m^2/s$$

**Step 2 — Compute combined diffusivity (Bosanquet):**

$$D_{combined} = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot D_{KA}}{D_{AB} + D_{KA}}$$

$$D_{combined} = \frac{(7.2 \times 10^{-5})(4.85 \times 10^{-5})}{(7.2 \times 10^{-5}) + (4.85 \times 10^{-5})}$$

$$D_{combined} = \frac{34.92 \times 10^{-10}}{12.05 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.898 \times 10^{-5} \, m^2/s$$

**Step 3 — Apply the porous-structure factor:**

$$D_{eff} = \frac{\varepsilon}{\tau} \cdot D_{combined}$$

$$D_{eff} = \frac{0.45}{2.5} \times 2.898 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$D_{eff} = 0.18 \times 2.898 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\boxed{D_{eff} = 5.216 \times 10^{-6} \, m^2/s}$$

**Interpretation:** The effective diffusivity is ~14× smaller than the free-molecular diffusivity, showing the dramatic transport penalty imposed by the porous structure.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Diffusion Regimes Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Gas Molecule Entering Porous Solid"] --> B["Compute Knudsen Number Kn = lambda / d_p"]
    B --> C{"Compare Kn to Threshold 0.01 and 10"}
    C -->|Kn less than 0.01| D["BULK MOLECULAR DIFFUSION REGIME"]
    C -->|Kn between 0.01 and 10| E["TRANSITION REGIME"]
    C -->|Kn greater than 10| F["KNUDSEN DIFFUSION REGIME"]
    D --> G["Use D_AB from Chapman-Enskog"]
    E --> H["Use Bosanquet: 1 over D equals 1 over D_KA plus 1 over D_AB"]
    F --> I["Use D_KA = d_p over 3 times sqrt 8RT over pi M_A"]
    G --> J["Apply structural factor: D_eff equals epsilon over tau times D_AB"]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K["Effective Diffusivity D_eff in Porous Solid"]
    K --> L["Compute Flux: N_A equals minus D_eff times dC_A over dz"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block Architecture of Porous-Solid Mass Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EXT["External Bulk Gas Phase"]
        GB["Bulk Concentration C_A_bulk"]
    end
    subgraph FILM["External Film Layer"]
        FL["Film Resistance: k_c"]
    end
    subgraph PELL["Porous Solid Pellet"]
        SURF["External Surface of Pellet"]
        PORE["Internal Pore Network: void fraction epsilon, path length factor tau, pore diameter d_p"]
        SITE["Internal Active Sites: catalyst or adsorbent"]
    end
    EXT --> FILM
    FL --> SURF
    SURF --> PORE
    PORE --> SITE
    SITE --> PORE
    PORE --> SURF
    SURF --> FL
    FL --> EXT
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Resistance-in-Series Model for Mass Transfer in a Catalyst Pellet

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1["R1: External Film Resistance 1 over k_c"] --> R2["R2: Pore Diffusion Resistance L over D_eff"]
    R2 --> R3["R3: Surface Reaction Resistance 1 over k_rho_s"]
    R3 --> R4["Total Resistance Drives Overall Rate"]
    style R1 fill:#f9e,stroke:#333
    style R2 fill:#bcf,stroke:#333
    style R3 fill:#fcb,stroke:#333
    style R4 fill:#9f9,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> The three resistance blocks correspond to the three sequential hurdles a diffusing molecule must overcome: crossing the boundary film outside the pellet, traversing the tortuous pore network inside, and finally reacting at (or adsorbing onto) an active site. The pore-diffusion block is precisely where $D_{eff}$ plays its dominant role.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** Define the term **Knudsen diffusivity**. Under what conditions does Knudsen diffusion become the controlling mechanism of mass transfer in a porous solid?

**Model Answer:**
Knudsen diffusivity is the diffusion coefficient applicable when the mean free path of the diffusing gas molecules is much larger than the pore diameter of the porous solid, so that molecule-wall collisions dominate over molecule-molecule collisions. It is given by the relation:

$$D_{KA} = \frac{d_p}{3} \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M_A}}$$

The Knudsen diffusion regime dominates when the ratio of mean free path to pore diameter (the Knudsen number) satisfies $Kn > 10$. Practically, this occurs in microporous and mesoporous materials (typical pore sizes < 50 nm) and at low operating pressures.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** What is **tortuosity** in the context of a porous solid? How does it influence the effective diffusivity?

**Model Answer:**
Tortuosity $\tau$ is defined as the ratio of the actual distance a molecule travels along a tortuous pore path to the straight-line distance between the two endpoints:

$$\tau = \frac{L_{actual}}{L_{straight}}$$

It is always greater than or equal to 1. The effective diffusivity is inversely proportional to tortuosity, as captured in the structural factor $\varepsilon / \tau$. A higher tortuosity means a longer path, more wall collisions, and a lower effective diffusivity. For most industrial catalysts, $\tau$ ranges between 1.5 and 10.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the Knudsen diffusivity of a gas through a cylindrical pore, starting from the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Hydrogen ($M_A = 2.016 \, g/mol$) is diffusing at $T = 500 \, K$ through the pores of a nickel catalyst pellet. The pore diameter is $d_p = 50 \, nm$, the porosity is $\varepsilon = 0.50$, and the tortuosity is $\tau = 3.0$. The molecular diffusivity of $H_2$ in the carrier gas at this temperature and 1 atm pressure is $D_{AB} = 6.0 \times 10^{-5} \, m^2/s$. Calculate the Knudsen diffusivity, the combined diffusivity, and the effective diffusivity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Knudsen diffusivity:**

The Maxwell-Boltzmann speed distribution gives the average speed:

$$\bar{c} = \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M_A}}$$

In a pore of diameter $d_p$, the Knudsen mean free path is geometric, not collisional:

$$\lambda_K = d_p$$

Using the kinetic-theory link $D = \frac{1}{3} \bar{c} \lambda$:

$$D_{KA} = \frac{1}{3} d_p \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M_A}}$$

**(b) Numerical calculation:**

Knudsen diffusivity:

$$D_{KA} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-9}}{3} \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 8.314 \times 500}{\pi \times 0.002016}}$$

$$D_{KA} = 1.667 \times 10^{-8} \times \sqrt{5.254 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$D_{KA} = 1.667 \times 10^{-8} \times 2292.2 = 3.82 \times 10^{-5} \, m^2/s$$

Combined diffusivity (Bosanquet):

$$D_{combined} = \frac{(6.0 \times 10^{-5})(3.82 \times 10^{-5})}{(6.0 + 3.82) \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{22.92}{9.82} \times 10^{-5}$$

$$D_{combined} = 2.334 \times 10^{-5} \, m^2/s$$

Effective diffusivity:

$$D_{eff} = \frac{0.50}{3.0} \times 2.334 \times 10^{-5} = 3.89 \times 10^{-6} \, m^2/s$$

---

#### Question B (Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the **Bosanquet equation** for combined Knudsen and molecular diffusion in porous solids. Show mathematically that it reduces to bulk-diffusion-controlled and Knudsen-diffusion-controlled limiting cases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Methane ($CH_4$, $M_A = 16 \, g/mol$) diffuses through a porous membrane with $d_p = 1 \, \mu m$, $\varepsilon = 0.6$, $\tau = 2.0$. The operating conditions are $T = 350 \, K$, $P = 2 \, atm$. The free-gas molecular diffusivity of $CH_4$ in air at 1 atm and 350 K is $D_{AB}^{\circ} = 2.2 \times 10^{-5} \, m^2/s$. Compute the effective diffusivity. Comment on which regime controls. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Bosanquet equation derivation and limits:**

The two mechanisms act as **resistances in series**, so their diffusional resistances add:

$$\frac{1}{D_{c}} = \frac{1}{D_{AB}} + \frac{1}{D_{KA}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D_{c} = \frac{D_{AB} D_{KA}}{D_{AB} + D_{KA}}$$

For the porous solid, multiply by the structural factor $\varepsilon / \tau$:

$$D_{eff} = \frac{\varepsilon}{\tau} \cdot \frac{D_{AB} D_{KA}}{D_{AB} + D_{KA}}$$

- **Limit 1** ($D_{KA} \gg D_{AB}$, large pores): The denominator $\approx D_{KA}$, so $D_{eff} \to \frac{\varepsilon}{\tau} D_{AB}$ → Bulk diffusion controls.
- **Limit 2** ($D_{KA} \ll D_{AB}$, small pores): The denominator $\approx D_{AB}$, so $D_{eff} \to \frac{\varepsilon}{\tau} D_{KA}$ → Knudsen diffusion controls.

**(b) Numerical computation:**

Correct $D_{AB}$ for 2 atm (since $D_{AB} \propto 1/P$):

$$D_{AB} = \frac{2.2 \times 10^{-5}}{2} = 1.1 \times 10^{-5} \, m^2/s$$

Knudsen diffusivity:

$$D_{KA} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-6}}{3} \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 8.314 \times 350}{\pi \times 0.016}}$$

$$D_{KA} = 3.333 \times 10^{-7} \times \sqrt{4.628 \times 10^{5}}$$

$$D_{KA} = 3.333 \times 10^{-7} \times 680.3 = 2.268 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2/s$$

Since $D_{KA} \gg D_{AB}$, bulk molecular diffusion controls. Combined diffusivity:

$$D_{c} = \frac{(1.1 \times 10^{-5})(2.268 \times 10^{-4})}{(1.1 \times 10^{-5}) + (2.268 \times 10^{-4})}$$

$$D_{c} \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-5} \, m^2/s \quad \text{(Knudsen term is negligible)}$$

Effective diffusivity:

$$D_{eff} = \frac{0.6}{2.0} \times 1.1 \times 10^{-5} = 3.3 \times 10^{-6} \, m^2/s$$

**Comment:** Because the pore diameter (1 µm) is much larger than the molecular mean free path at 2 atm, Knudsen effects are negligible, and the system operates firmly in the **bulk molecular diffusion regime**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (as flagged by KTU Board Examiners):**
> 1. **Forgetting to correct $D_{AB}$ for pressure:** $D_{AB} \propto 1/P$. If a problem gives $D_{AB}$ at 1 atm and asks for 5 atm, you MUST scale down. Many students lose 2-3 marks here.
> 2. **Mixing units in the Knudsen formula:** $M_A$ MUST be in kg/kmol (SI) to pair with $R = 8.314 \, J/(mol \cdot K)$. Using g/mol without unit conversion yields a wrong answer off by $\sqrt{1000}$.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\varepsilon / \tau$ factor:** The Bosanquet equation gives the intrinsic combined diffusivity, NOT the effective one. The structural factor is mandatory in the final answer.
> 4. **Computing the harmonic mean of $D_{AB}$ and $D_{KA}$ instead of the harmonic mean of their reciprocals:** The harmonic mean of $D_{AB}$ and $D_{KA}$ is the AVERAGE — wrong. The correct form is the **sum of reciprocals** inverted.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things to Remember Before the KTU Exam:**
> - **Three diffusion regimes** are classified by the Knudsen number $Kn = \lambda / d_p$: bulk ($Kn < 0.01$), transition ($0.01 < Kn < 10$), Knudsen ($Kn > 10$).
> - **Knudsen diffusivity** formula: $D_{KA} = \dfrac{d_p}{3} \sqrt{\dfrac{8 R T}{\pi M_A}}$ — independent of pressure.
> - **Molecular diffusivity** $D_{AB}$ — depends inversely on total pressure (use Chapman-Enskog or corrected $D_{AB}^{\circ}/P$).
> - **Bosanquet equation** — harmonic mean of $D_{AB}$ and $D_{KA}$ via reciprocals: $D_c = \left(1/D_{AB} + 1/D_{KA}\right)^{-1}$.
> - **Effective diffusivity** — $D_{eff} = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{\tau} D_c$ — the master equation for porous-solid mass transfer.
> - **Tortuosity** $\tau$ is always $\geq 1$; typical catalyst pellets have $\tau \approx 1.5$ to $5$.
> - **Porosity** $\varepsilon < 1$ for real solids; it represents the void fraction available for diffusion.
> - In **large pores / high pressure**, bulk diffusion controls. In **small pores / low pressure**, Knudsen controls.
> - **Surface diffusion** (not in standard formula) is an additional mechanism for adsorbed species on pore walls — significant at low temperatures.
> - **Remember the units:** $D$ in $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, $d_p$ in m, $T$ in K, $M_A$ in kg/kmol, $R = 8.314 \, J/(mol \cdot K)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT746 - ADVANCED HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Knudsen Diffusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Knudsen Diffusion** is a mass-transfer mechanism that dominates when a gas flows through a porous medium whose characteristic pore dimension is comparable to or smaller than the **mean free path** of the diffusing molecules. Under these conditions, molecule–wall collisions occur far more frequently than molecule–molecule collisions, and the transport rate is governed entirely by the kinetic energy of the gas molecules and the geometry of the pore walls rather than by ordinary molecular diffusion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Reference:** Knudsen diffusion is the transport of gases in capillaries and porous solids where the Knudsen number ($Kn$) is greater than unity. The Knudsen diffusivity is independent of pressure, and depends only on **pore radius, temperature and molecular weight**.

## 1.2 The Knudsen Number — The Governing Parameter

The dimensionless group that selects the regime of diffusion in a pore is the **Knudsen number**:

$$Kn = \frac{\lambda}{D_p} = \frac{\lambda}{2 r_p}$$

where $\lambda$ is the mean free path of the gas molecules, $D_p$ the pore diameter, and $r_p$ the pore radius.

| Regime | Knudsen Number | Dominant Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Free-molecular (Knudsen) | $Kn > 1$ | Molecule–wall collisions |
| Transition / Slip flow | $0.01 < Kn < 1$ | Both mechanisms comparable |
| Continuum (Molecular) | $Kn < 0.01$ | Molecule–molecule collisions |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bumper-Car Hallway"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine two hallways. In a *wide* hallway (large pore), people walk freely and occasionally bump into each other — this is **molecular diffusion**. In a *narrow* hallway (small pore), people cannot take more than a single step without tapping a wall — this is **Knudsen diffusion**. The wall tap (collision with the pore wall) replaces the person-to-person collision as the randomising event, and the wall texture (pore radius) becomes the controlling parameter.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Key Metrics

- **Boltzmann constant:** $k_B = 1.380649 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$
- **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- **Mean molecular speed (Maxwell):** $\langle c \rangle = \sqrt{\dfrac{8RT}{\pi M}}$
- **Reference Knudsen number boundary:** $Kn \approx 1$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mean Free Path — The Molecular Yardstick

The mean free path is the average distance a molecule travels between successive collisions. From kinetic theory, considering a moving molecule striking stationary targets, the correction factor $\sqrt{2}$ is introduced when both the target and projectile molecules are moving.

$$\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\, d^2\, P}$$

with $d$ being the collision diameter and $P$ the absolute pressure.

### Key Physical Observations
- $\lambda \propto T$ (increases with temperature)
- $\lambda \propto 1/P$ (decreases with pressure)
- $\lambda \propto 1/d^2$ (smaller molecules → longer mean free path)

## 2.2 Knudsen Diffusivity — The Wall-Driven Diffusion Coefficient

The Knudsen diffusivity $D_K$ for a cylindrical pore of radius $r_p$ is derived by recognising that a molecule travels an axial displacement of order $r_p$ before striking the wall, with frequency $\langle c \rangle / (2 r_p)$. Combining with the Einstein random-walk relation:

$$D_K = \frac{1}{3}\, D_p\, \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}} = \frac{2}{3}\, r_p\, \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pressure Independence:** $D_K$ is **independent of total pressure**. This is its signature fingerprint. Doubling the pressure doubles the molecular density, but halves the mean free path proportionally, leaving the wall-collision frequency — and hence $D_K$ — unchanged.

## 2.3 Knudsen Flux Equation

Using Fick's first law applied to a porous medium with porosity $\varepsilon$ and tortuosity $\tau$:

$$N_{A,K} = -\frac{D_{K,eff}}{\tau}\, \varepsilon\, \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

where the effective Knudsen diffusivity in the porous pellet is $D_{K,eff} = D_K \cdot \varepsilon / \tau$.

## 2.4 Combined Molecular + Knudsen Diffusion (Bosanquet Relation)

In the transition regime, both fluxes act in parallel. The reciprocal addition rule (Bosanquet, 1944) gives the effective diffusivity:

$$\frac{1}{D_{eff}} = \frac{1}{D_{AB}} + \frac{1}{D_{K,eff}}$$

When $D_{K,eff} \ll D_{AB}$, Knudsen resistance controls; when $D_{AB} \ll D_{K,eff}$, ordinary molecular diffusion controls.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean free path | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\, d^2\, P}$ | m | Decreases with $P$ |
| Knudsen number | $Kn = \lambda / D_p$ | dimensionless | Regime selector |
| Knudsen diffusivity | $D_K = \dfrac{2}{3} r_p \sqrt{\dfrac{8RT}{\pi M}}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | Pressure-independent |
| Mean molecular speed | $\langle c \rangle = \sqrt{\dfrac{8RT}{\pi M}}$ | m/s | Maxwell-Boltzmann |
| Effective pore diffusivity | $D_{eff} = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{\tau} \left( \dfrac{1}{D_{AB}} + \dfrac{1}{D_K} \right)^{-1}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | Bosanquet form |
| Knudsen flux | $N_{A,K} = - D_{K,eff}\,(dC_A/dz)$ | $\text{mol}/(\text{m}^2\,\text{s})$ | Fick's law form |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Knudsen diffusion is the limiting mechanism in heterogeneous catalysis (zeolites, MOFs, catalyst pellets), membrane gas separation (ceramic and carbon molecular sieves), and gas diffusion electrodes in fuel cells. The Knudsen selectivity $\sqrt{M_2/M_1}$ is the basis of many commercial membrane separations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Mean Free Path from Kinetic Theory

**Setup:** Consider a single molecule moving with average speed $\langle c \rangle$ through a gas of stationary target molecules of diameter $d$ and number density $n$.

**Step 1 — Swept volume per unit time.**
In time $\Delta t$, the molecule sweeps a cylindrical volume of cross-sectional area $\pi d^2$ and length $\langle c \rangle \Delta t$:

$$V_{swept} = \pi d^2 \langle c \rangle \Delta t$$

**Step 2 — Number of collisions in $\Delta t$.**
Each target centre inside this swept volume triggers a collision. Because targets are also moving in random directions, the relative speed is $\sqrt{2}\,\langle c \rangle$, and the effective swept volume per unit time becomes $\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^2 \langle c \rangle$.

**Step 3 — Number density of targets.**
From the ideal gas law, $P V = N k_B T$, so $n = P/(k_B T)$.

**Step 4 — Collisions per unit time (collision frequency).**

$$\nu = \sqrt{2}\,\pi d^2 \langle c \rangle n$$

**Step 5 — Mean free path (distance per collision).**
By definition $\lambda = \langle c \rangle / \nu$:

$$\lambda = \frac{\langle c \rangle}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^2 \langle c \rangle n} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^2 n}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $n = P/(k_B T)$.**

$$\boxed{\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^2 P}}$$

**Sanity check:** For air at 25 °C, 1 atm, $d \approx 3.7 \times 10^{-10}$ m, this gives $\lambda \approx 66$ nm. For a typical catalyst pore of 10 nm, $Kn \approx 6.6 \gg 1$, confirming Knudsen dominance. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Knudsen Diffusivity from Molecular Random Walk

**Setup:** Molecule inside a long cylindrical pore of radius $r_p$, mean speed $\langle c \rangle = \sqrt{8RT/(\pi M)}$. Collisions occur only with the wall.

**Step 1 — Axial step per wall collision.**
Between successive wall hits, the molecule travels along the pore axis a distance of order $r_p$. By averaging over all radial positions and all reflection angles, the *root-mean-square* axial displacement per wall collision is:

$$\overline{\Delta z} = \frac{2 r_p}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 2 — Time between successive wall collisions.**
The average chord length a molecule traverses between two wall strikes inside a cylinder of radius $r_p$ equals $\frac{\pi r_p}{2}$ in 3D random direction; the corresponding mean time is:

$$\tau = \frac{\pi r_p}{2 \langle c \rangle}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Einstein's 1-D random-walk formula.**
For one-dimensional diffusion, the mean-square displacement after $N$ steps each of variance $\sigma^2$ grows as $N \sigma^2 = t \sigma^2 / \tau$. Equating to $2 D_K t$:

$$2 D_K t = \frac{t \sigma^2}{\tau} \quad\Rightarrow\quad D_K = \frac{\sigma^2}{2 \tau}$$

**Step 4 — Insert $\sigma = 2 r_p / \sqrt{3}$ and $\tau = \pi r_p/(2\langle c \rangle)$.**

$$D_K = \frac{\left( 2 r_p / \sqrt{3} \right)^2}{2 \cdot \pi r_p / (2 \langle c \rangle)} = \frac{4 r_p^2 / 3}{ \pi r_p / \langle c \rangle } = \frac{4 r_p \langle c \rangle}{3 \pi}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\langle c \rangle = \sqrt{8 R T / (\pi M)}$.**

$$D_K = \frac{4 r_p}{3 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M}} = \frac{2}{3} r_p \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M}}$$

$$\boxed{D_K = \frac{1}{3} D_p \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M}} = \frac{2}{3} r_p \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M}}}$$

**Conversion to a KTU-friendly numerical constant:**
$D_K$ in $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ with $r_p$ in m, $T$ in K, $M$ in kg/kmol:

$$D_K \approx 3.067 \times 10^{-5}\, r_p \sqrt{\frac{T}{M}} \quad [\text{m}^2/\text{s}]$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Combined Diffusion in the Transition Regime (Bosanquet)

**Step 1 — Two independent flux contributions.**
In the transition regime the total flux of species A is the sum of the molecular (concentration-gradient driven) and Knudsen (wall-driven) parts:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} - D_K \frac{dC_A}{dz} = -(D_{AB} + D_K)\frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

This *linear* addition is **incorrect** when fluxes are mutually constrained by the stagnant-film constraint.

**Step 2 — Real coupling (Stephan-Maxwell constraint).**
For equimolar counter-diffusion through a stagnant film, the correct addition uses **harmonic** mean (resistances-in-series analogy):

$$\frac{1}{D_{eff}} = \frac{1}{D_{AB}} + \frac{1}{D_K}$$

**Step 3 — Final combined flux with effective pore geometry.**
For a porous pellet of porosity $\varepsilon$ and tortuosity $\tau$:

$$N_A = -\frac{\varepsilon}{\tau}\, \left[ \frac{1}{D_{AB}} + \frac{1}{D_K} \right]^{-1} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

**Step 4 — Limiting cases (verification).**

- $D_{AB} \ll D_K \Rightarrow D_{eff} \to D_{AB}$ (molecular diffusion controls)
- $D_K \ll D_{AB} \Rightarrow D_{eff} \to D_K$ (Knudsen diffusion controls)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why harmonic, not arithmetic?** Because the molecular and Knudsen mechanisms share the same driving force ($dC_A/dz$) but operate through independent *resistances* that must sum. Just as electrical resistances in series add reciprocally, diffusion resistances in series add reciprocally — leading to Bosanquet's formula.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Hydrogen diffuses through a porous catalyst pellet of mean pore radius $r_p = 100$ nm at 300 K and 1 atm. Compute (i) the mean free path and (ii) the Knudsen diffusivity. Collision diameter $d = 2.92 \times 10^{-10}$ m; $M = 2.016$ kg/kmol.

**Solution:**

**Step (i) — Mean free path.**

$$\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\, d^2 P} = \frac{(1.381 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\, (2.92 \times 10^{-10})^2 (1.013 \times 10^{5})}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{4.143 \times 10^{-21}}{3.746 \times 10^{-14}} \approx 1.106 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m} = 110.6\ \text{nm}$$

**Step (ii) — Knudsen diffusivity.**

$$\langle c \rangle = \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M}} = \sqrt{\frac{8 (8.314)(300)}{\pi (2.016)}} = \sqrt{3152.4} \approx 56.15\ \text{m/s}$$

Wait — that unit is kg/(kmol), so M in kg/kmol must match. Let me redo with M = 2.016 × 10⁻³ kg/mol:

$$\langle c \rangle = \sqrt{\frac{8 (8.314)(300)}{\pi (2.016 \times 10^{-3})}} \approx 1776\ \text{m/s}$$

$$D_K = \frac{2}{3} (100 \times 10^{-9}) \sqrt{\frac{8 (8.314)(300)}{\pi (2.016 \times 10^{-3})}} = \frac{2}{3}(10^{-7})(1776)$$

$$\boxed{D_K \approx 1.184 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}}$$

**Knudsen number check:** $Kn = \lambda / D_p = 110.6 / 200 = 0.553$. Since $0.01 < Kn < 1$, this lies in the **transition regime** where combined molecular-Knudsen flux must be considered.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Knudsen Diffusion Analysis

The mermaid diagram below traces the analytical workflow that a chemical engineer follows when characterising Knudsen-controlled transport inside a porous catalyst pellet.

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start([Gas mixture enters porous pellet]) --> RegimeIdentify[Identify transport regime]
    RegimeIdentify --> KnCalc[Compute Knudsen number Kn]
    KnCalc --> KnCheck{Kn regime}
    KnCheck --> KnHigh[Kn greater than 1: Knudsen dominant]
    KnCheck --> KnMid[Kn between 0.01 and 1: Transition]
    KnCheck --> KnLow[Kn less than 0.01: Molecular dominant]
    KnHigh --> DKCompute[Compute Knudsen diffusivity DK]
    KnMid --> CombineDiff[Compute combined diffusivity: Bosanquet formula]
    KnLow --> DABCompute[Use ordinary molecular diffusivity DAB]
    DKCompute --> FluxCalc[Compute molar flux via Ficks law]
    CombineDiff --> FluxCalc
    DABCompute --> FluxCalc
    FluxCalc --> PoreAdjust[Apply porosity and tortuosity correction]
    PoreAdjust --> FinalFlux([Effective molar flux for design])
```

## 4.2 Pore-Scale Collision Mechanism Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    GasA[Gas molecule A] --> Move1[Travel distance r_p]
    Move1 --> WallHit1[Collide with pore wall]
    WallHit1 --> Move2[Reflect and travel another r_p]
    Move2 --> WallHit2[Collide with wall again]
    WallHit2 --> Move3[Continue random axial walk]
    Move3 --> BulkFlow[Net axial diffusion flux NK]
    Note1[No molecule-molecule collisions occur when Kn greater than 1] -.-> Move1
    Note2[Wall geometry controls the diffusion] -.-> WallHit1
```

## 4.3 Regime Decision Tree (Textual Reference)

```
       Mean free path λ
              │
              ▼
     Compute Kn = λ / D_p
              │
   ┌──────────┼──────────┐
   │          │          │
   ▼          ▼          ▼
Kn > 1    0.01-1     Kn < 0.01
   │          │          │
   ▼          ▼          ▼
Knudsen   Transition   Molecular
Regime    Regime       Regime
   │          │          │
   ▼          ▼          ▼
Use DK    Use DK and   Use DAB
 only      DAB together  only
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual mapping:** The two Mermaid block diagrams above abstract the wall-collision physics (4.2) and the engineering decision workflow (4.1) into a single graphical story. The textual tree (4.3) provides the exam-ready 3-line answer that examiners expect in a 7-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: Define Knudsen diffusion. Under what condition does it become the dominant mechanism of mass transfer in a porous medium?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Knudsen diffusion is the mechanism of gas transport through porous media in which the diffusing molecules collide more frequently with the pore walls than with each other. *(1 mark)*
> It becomes dominant when the mean free path $\lambda$ of the gas molecules is comparable to or larger than the pore diameter $D_p$, i.e. when the Knudsen number $Kn = \lambda / D_p > 1$. *(2 marks)*
> Under this condition the Knudsen diffusivity $D_K = \frac{2}{3} r_p \sqrt{8RT/(\pi M)}$ becomes the controlling transport coefficient, and it is independent of the total system pressure.

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is the Knudsen number? Explain its significance in classifying diffusion regimes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Knudsen number is defined as the dimensionless ratio of the mean free path $\lambda$ to a characteristic pore dimension $D_p$: $Kn = \lambda/D_p$. *(1 mark)*
> It classifies diffusion into three regimes: $Kn > 1$ (Knudsen/free-molecular regime), $0.01 < Kn < 1$ (transition/slip regime), and $Kn < 0.01$ (continuum/molecular regime). *(2 marks)*
> Its practical significance lies in selecting the correct diffusion model — Knudsen, combined Bosanquet, or ordinary molecular — when analysing mass transfer in catalyst pellets, membranes, and porous electrodes.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Knudsen Diffusion Problems:**
> - **Forgetting to convert $M$ units:** Many students plug molecular weight in kg/kmol directly into $\sqrt{T/M}$ inside $D_K$, producing an answer that is off by a factor of $\sqrt{1000}$. Always convert $M$ to kg/mol *or* keep the constant $\sqrt{8R/\pi}$ with units checked.
> - **Mixing up $D_p$ and $r_p$:** The KTU formula uses either $D_p$ with coefficient $1/3$ or $r_p$ with coefficient $2/3$. A common slip is using $D_p$ together with $2/3$, giving an answer wrong by a factor of 2.
> - **Neglecting porosity/tortuosity in pellets:** Bare $D_K$ applies to a *single straight cylindrical pore*. For real catalyst pellets the examiner expects $D_{K,eff} = (\varepsilon/\tau) D_K$.
> - **Forgetting the $\sqrt{2}$ in mean free path:** The classical mean free path has a $\sqrt{2}$ correction. Marks are deducted if it is omitted.

---

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the Knudsen diffusivity $D_K$ starting from the kinetic theory of gases. State clearly all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gas A ($M_A = 32$ kg/kmol) diffuses through a porous catalyst pellet at 400 K. The pellet has a mean pore radius of 50 nm and porosity 0.45 with tortuosity 3.0. Calculate: (i) the bare Knudsen diffusivity $D_K$, (ii) the effective Knudsen diffusivity $D_{K,eff}$, and (iii) the molar flux if the concentration gradient is $dC_A/dz = 5 \times 10^{3}$ mol/m⁴. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Assumptions (1 mark):
1. Cylindrical pore of constant radius $r_p$.
2. Molecule–wall collisions dominate (no molecule–molecule interactions).
3. Molecular reflections from the wall are diffuse.
4. Gas is ideal.

Step 1: Mean molecular speed from Maxwell–Boltzmann (1 mark):

$$\langle c \rangle = \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}}$$

Step 2: Time between successive wall collisions $\tau = \pi r_p/(2 \langle c \rangle)$ (1 mark).

Step 3: RMS axial displacement per wall collision $\sigma = 2 r_p / \sqrt{3}$ (1 mark).

Step 4: Einstein's 1-D diffusion relation $D_K = \sigma^2/(2\tau)$ (1 mark):

$$D_K = \frac{4 r_p^2/3}{2 \cdot \pi r_p/(2\langle c \rangle)} = \frac{4 r_p \langle c \rangle}{3 \pi}$$

Step 5: Substitute $\langle c \rangle$ (1 mark):

$$\boxed{D_K = \frac{2}{3}\, r_p\, \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}}}$$

Step 6: Note $D_K$ is independent of $P$ (1 mark).

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

(i) Bare $D_K$ (3 marks):

$$\langle c \rangle = \sqrt{\frac{8 (8.314)(400)}{\pi (32/1000)}} = \sqrt{\frac{26604.8}{0.1005}} = \sqrt{264{,}725} \approx 514.5\ \text{m/s}$$

$$D_K = \frac{2}{3} (50 \times 10^{-9}) (514.5) \approx 1.715 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

(ii) Effective $D_{K,eff}$ (2 marks):

$$D_{K,eff} = \frac{\varepsilon}{\tau} D_K = \frac{0.45}{3.0} \times 1.715 \times 10^{-5} = 2.57 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

(iii) Molar flux (2 marks):

$$N_{A,K} = -D_{K,eff} \frac{dC_A}{dz} = -(2.57 \times 10^{-6})(5 \times 10^{3}) = -1.285 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{mol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$$

Magnitude $|N_{A,K}| \approx 1.29 \times 10^{-2}$ mol/(m²·s); the negative sign denotes flux down the concentration gradient.

---

### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the three regimes of diffusion in porous media based on the Knudsen number. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Hydrogen ($M = 2.016$ kg/kmol) and nitrogen ($M = 28$ kg/kmol) are diffusing through a porous membrane of mean pore radius 200 nm at 350 K and 2 atm. Compute: (i) the mean free path of hydrogen, (ii) the Knudsen diffusivity for each gas, and (iii) the ideal Knudsen selectivity for H₂/N₂ separation. Take collision diameter $d_{H_2} = 2.92 \times 10^{-10}$ m. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Three regimes with diagram (7 marks):**

Knudsen number definition (1 mark): $Kn = \lambda / D_p$.

*Knudsen / Free-molecular regime* ($Kn > 1$) (2 marks): $\lambda \gg D_p$. Molecule–wall collisions dominate. Diffusivity $D_K = \frac{2}{3} r_p \sqrt{8RT/(\pi M)}$, independent of pressure. Common in microporous catalysts, MOFs, membranes.

*Transition / Slip regime* ($0.01 < Kn < 1$) (2 marks): Both mechanisms comparable. Effective diffusivity given by Bosanquet: $1/D_{eff} = 1/D_{AB} + 1/D_K$. Frequent in mesopores (2–50 nm) under moderate pressure.

*Continuum / Molecular regime* ($Kn < 0.01$) (1 mark): $\lambda \ll D_p$. Ordinary Fickian diffusion dominates with $D_{AB}$ from Chapman–Enskog theory.

(1 mark) for the neat sketch showing three pore widths with arrows indicating either wall- or molecule-collision dominance.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

(i) Mean free path of H₂ (3 marks):

$$\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\, d^2 P} = \frac{(1.381 \times 10^{-23})(350)}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\, (2.92 \times 10^{-10})^2 (2 \times 1.013 \times 10^{5})}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{4.834 \times 10^{-21}}{7.59 \times 10^{-14}} \approx 6.37 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{m} = 63.7\ \text{nm}$$

(ii) Knudsen diffusivities (3 marks):

For H₂ ($M = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol):

$$D_{K,H_2} = \frac{2}{3}(200 \times 10^{-9})\sqrt{\frac{8(8.314)(350)}{\pi(2.016 \times 10^{-3})}} = 6.86 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

For N₂ ($M = 28 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol):

$$D_{K,N_2} = \frac{2}{3}(200 \times 10^{-9})\sqrt{\frac{8(8.314)(350)}{\pi(28 \times 10^{-3})}} = 2.30 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

(iii) Ideal Knudsen selectivity (1 mark):

$$\alpha_{H_2/N_2}^{Kn} = \frac{D_{K,H_2}}{D_{K,N_2}} = \sqrt{\frac{M_{N_2}}{M_{H_2}}} = \sqrt{\frac{28}{2.016}} \approx 3.73$$

This matches the textbook ideal selectivity for H₂/N₂ separation in Knudsen-mode membranes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Knudsen diffusion** = wall-collision-dominated transport in pores where $Kn = \lambda/D_p > 1$.
- [x] **Mean free path:** $\lambda = k_B T/(\sqrt{2}\,\pi\, d^2 P)$; decreases with $P$, increases with $T$.
- [x] **Knudsen diffusivity:** $D_K = \dfrac{2}{3}\, r_p\, \sqrt{8RT/(\pi M)}$ — **pressure independent**.
- [x] **Mean molecular speed:** $\langle c \rangle = \sqrt{8RT/(\pi M)}$ — Maxwell-Boltzmann.
- [x] **Regime boundary:** $Kn = 1$; transition regime $0.01 < Kn < 1$; molecular regime $Kn < 0.01$.
- [x] **Bosanquet combined diffusivity:** $1/D_{eff} = 1/D_{AB} + 1/D_K$ (resistances in series).
- [x] **Pellet-level correction:** $D_{K,eff} = (\varepsilon/\tau) D_K$.
- [x] **Knudsen selectivity** for binary separation: $\alpha_{A/B}^{Kn} = \sqrt{M_B/M_A}$ (lighter gas is preferred).
- [x] **Industrial relevance:** catalyst pellets (FCC, hydrocracking), gas-separation membranes, fuel-cell GDEs, MOFs, zeolites.
- [x] **Examiner's trap:** always convert $M$ to kg/mol when using $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) inside the square root.
- [x] **Examiner's trap:** distinguish single-pore $D_K$ from pellet-level $D_{K,eff}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unsteady State Diffusion – Basic Diffusion Equation; Convective Transport and Interphase Mass Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT746 - ADVANCED HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Unsteady State Diffusion – Basic Diffusion Equation; Convective Transport and Interphase Mass Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Unsteady State Diffusion – Basic Diffusion Equation

### Formal Academic Definition
**Unsteady (Transient) Diffusion** is the time-dependent mass transfer process in which the concentration of a species at any point in a medium changes with time. The fundamental governing equation is **Fick's Second Law of Diffusion**, expressed mathematically as:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \nabla^2 C_A + R_A$$

where $C_A$ is the concentration of species A (kmol/m³), $t$ is time (s), $D_{AB}$ is the binary diffusivity (m²/s), $\nabla^2$ is the Laplacian operator, and $R_A$ is the rate of generation/consumption of A per unit volume due to chemical reaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Fick's Second Law is the cornerstone equation of transient mass transfer, directly analogous to Fourier's Law in transient heat conduction. Students must master its derivation from the **species mass balance on a differential control volume**.

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)
Imagine a drop of ink placed in a still glass of water. Initially, the ink is concentrated at a single point. As time passes, the colour spreads outward until the water becomes uniformly tinted. This is **transient diffusion in action**. The rate of spreading depends on the *diffusivity* of ink in water — a higher diffusivity means faster spreading. Mathematically, Fick's Second Law tells us *how fast* the concentration at any point changes with time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** *Steady-state* diffusion (Fick's First Law) has $\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = 0$ — concentrations are constant with time. *Unsteady-state* diffusion has $\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} \neq 0$ — concentrations evolve with time until equilibrium is reached.

### Physical Constants & Metrics
- **Diffusivity ($D_{AB}$):** typically $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-9}$ m²/s
- **Typical units of concentration:** kmol/m³ (SI), mol/L (CGS)
- **Schmidt Number ($Sc$):** dimensionless ratio of momentum to mass diffusivity

---

## 1.2 Convective Mass Transport

### Formal Academic Definition
**Convective Mass Transfer** is the transport of a species between a moving fluid and a surface (solid–fluid or fluid–fluid interface) or between two fluid phases. It is governed by the combined action of **molecular diffusion** and **bulk fluid motion (convection)**.

The general convective flux equation is:

$$N_A = k_c (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$$

where $N_A$ is the molar flux (kmol/m²·s), $k_c$ is the convective mass transfer coefficient (m/s), $C_{A,s}$ is the concentration at the surface, and $C_{A,\infty}$ is the bulk concentration.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of blowing air over a cup of hot tea. The water vapour rising from the tea surface is carried away faster by the moving air (forced convection) compared to still air. The **mass transfer coefficient** $k_c$ is essentially a *lumped parameter* that captures how efficiently the bulk flow removes vapour from the surface. Higher wind speed ⇒ higher $k_c$ ⇒ faster evaporation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always identify the driving force for mass transfer correctly. For gases it is often expressed in partial pressure $\Delta p_A$, requiring a different coefficient ($k_G$ in kmol/m²·s·Pa).

---

## 1.3 Interphase Mass Transfer

### Formal Academic Definition
**Interphase Mass Transfer** refers to mass transfer occurring between two immiscible phases in contact (e.g., gas–liquid, liquid–liquid, or gas–solid). The classic model is **Whitman's Two-Film Theory (1923)**, which assumes that:
1. Two stagnant films exist on either side of the interface.
2. Mass transfer occurs by steady-state molecular diffusion through each film.
3. Local equilibrium exists at the interface.

The driving force across the two films is described by the **overall mass transfer coefficient** $K_y$ or $K_x$:

$$\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x}$$

where $m$ is the slope of the equilibrium curve ($y^* = mx^*$).

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a sponge (liquid phase) absorbing perfume vapour (gas phase). The perfume molecules must first **diffuse through the stagnant air layer** adjacent to the sponge (gas film), then **dissolve and diffuse through the liquid film** on the sponge surface, and finally be absorbed into the bulk sponge. The overall rate is controlled by the *slowest* of these two steps — analogous to thermal resistances in series.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Concept:** The **controlling resistance** determines whether the overall coefficient is dominated by the gas phase or the liquid phase. For a highly soluble gas (e.g., NH₃ in water), $m$ is small, and the gas-phase resistance controls. For a sparingly soluble gas (e.g., O₂ in water), the liquid-phase resistance dominates.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Derivation Roadmap: Fick's Second Law

The transient diffusion equation is derived by applying the **principle of conservation of mass** to a differential element. The logical flow is:

1. **Start with Fick's First Law** — defines flux in terms of concentration gradient.
2. **Construct a mass balance** on a differential control volume: (In − Out + Generation = Accumulation).
3. **Apply Taylor series expansion** to express flux divergence.
4. **Combine equations** and take the limit as the element shrinks to zero.
5. **Arrive at Fick's Second Law** in 1-D, 2-D, and 3-D forms.

### Why This Matters
This equation is to mass transfer what Fourier's heat equation is to heat transfer. It enables the **prediction of concentration profiles during transient processes** such as drying, leaching, gas absorption, catalyst poisoning, and drug release from polymers.

---

## 2.2 Forms of Fick's Second Law in Different Coordinate Systems

### (a) Cartesian (Rectangular) Coordinates
$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\left(\frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}\right)$$

### (b) Cylindrical Coordinates
$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = \frac{D_{AB}}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{D_{AB}}{r^2}\frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial \theta^2} + D_{AB}\frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$$

### (c) Spherical Coordinates
$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = \frac{D_{AB}}{r^2}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r^2\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial r}\right)$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols Used:** $C$ = concentration, $D$ = diffusivity, $t$ = time, $x$ = distance, $N_A$ = molar flux, $k_c$ = mass transfer coefficient, $Sc$ = Schmidt number, $Sh$ = Sherwood number.

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Fick's First Law (1-D) | $N_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$ | $N_A$ in kmol/m²·s |
| 2 | Fick's Second Law (1-D, no reaction) | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$ | Parabolic PDE |
| 3 | Transient slab solution (semi-infinite) | $\dfrac{C_A - C_{A0}}{C_{As} - C_{A0}} = \mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{D_{AB}t}}\right)$ | $\mathrm{erfc}$ = complementary error function |
| 4 | Penetration depth | $\delta_p = 4\sqrt{D_{AB}t}$ | Used in penetration theory |
| 5 | Convective mass transfer (concentration basis) | $N_A = k_c(C_{As} - C_{A\infty})$ | $k_c$ in m/s |
| 6 | Convective mass transfer (mol fraction, gas) | $N_A = k_y(y_{As} - y_{A\infty})$ | $k_y$ in kmol/m²·s |
| 7 | Convective mass transfer (partial pressure) | $N_A = k_G(p_{As} - p_{A\infty})$ | $k_G$ in kmol/m²·s·Pa |
| 8 | Overall mass transfer (gas–liquid) | $\dfrac{1}{K_y} = \dfrac{1}{k_y} + \dfrac{m}{k_x}$ | Resistances in series |
| 9 | Sherwood Number | $Sh = \dfrac{k_c L}{D_{AB}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Schmidt Number | $Sc = \dfrac{\nu}{D_{AB}}$ | $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity |
| 11 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu} = \dfrac{vL}{\nu}$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Chilton–Colburn Analogy | $j_D = j_H \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dfrac{Sh}{Re Sc^{1/3}} = \dfrac{Nu}{Re Pr^{1/3}}$ | Heat–Mass Transfer analogy |
| 13 | Penetration Theory (Higbie) | $k_c = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_e}}$ | $t_e$ = exposure time |
| 14 | Surface Renewal Theory (Danckwerts) | $k_c = \sqrt{D_{AB} s}$ | $s$ = surface renewal rate (s⁻¹) |
| 15 | Film Theory | $k_c = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{\delta_f}$ | $\delta_f$ = effective film thickness |
| 16 | Mass transfer Biot Number | $Bi_m = \dfrac{k_c L_c}{D_{AB}}$ | $L_c$ = characteristic length |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Formula Note:** Equations 3, 6, 8, 12, and 13 appear most frequently in KTU board questions. Memorize them with **all symbols and units**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| Chemical Engineering | Gas absorption, distillation, drying, leaching |
| Biomedical | Drug delivery through membranes, oxygen transport in blood |
| Environmental | Pollutant dispersion, soil remediation, atmospheric diffusion |
| Food Processing | Moisture migration, salt diffusion in foods, packaging permeability |
| Metallurgy | Carburizing/nitriding of steel, oxidation of metals |
| Semiconductor | Dopant diffusion in silicon wafer fabrication |

---

## 2.5 Comparison of Interphase Mass Transfer Theories

| Theory | Proposed By | Key Assumption | Limitation |
|--------|-------------|----------------|------------|
| **Film Theory** | Whitman (1923) | Stagnant film of thickness $\delta_f$; steady diffusion | Unrealistic assumption of stagnant film |
| **Penetration Theory** | Higbie (1935) | Fluid elements exposed to interface for equal contact time $t_e$ | Predicts contact time; requires empirical $t_e$ |
| **Surface Renewal Theory** | Danckwerts (1951) | Surface elements replaced randomly; characterized by renewal rate $s$ | Most general; still requires $s$ from experiment |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Fick's Second Law (1-D)

### Step 1: Start with Fick's First Law
For one-dimensional diffusion along the $x$-direction, the molar flux at any plane is given by Fick's First Law:

$$J_A = -D_{AB}\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}$$

### Step 2: Define a Differential Control Volume
Consider a thin slab of thickness $dx$ and unit cross-sectional area $A = 1$ m². The concentration $C_A$ depends on both position $x$ and time $t$.

### Step 3: Write the Mass Balance
For a species A undergoing no chemical reaction, the species balance on the control volume is:

$$\text{Rate of In} - \text{Rate of Out} = \text{Rate of Accumulation}$$

The flux entering at position $x$ is $J_A(x)$, and the flux leaving at position $x+dx$ is $J_A(x+dx)$.

$$J_A(x) \cdot A - J_A(x+dx) \cdot A = \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t}(A \cdot dx)$$

### Step 4: Expand Using Taylor Series
Express $J_A(x+dx)$ as a Taylor expansion about $x$:

$$J_A(x+dx) = J_A(x) + \frac{\partial J_A}{\partial x}dx + \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^2 J_A}{\partial x^2}(dx)^2 + \ldots$$

Neglecting higher-order terms (since $dx$ is infinitesimal):

$$J_A(x+dx) \approx J_A(x) + \frac{\partial J_A}{\partial x}dx$$

### Step 5: Substitute into the Mass Balance
$$J_A(x) - \left[J_A(x) + \frac{\partial J_A}{\partial x}dx\right] = \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t}\,dx$$

Simplifying:

$$-\frac{\partial J_A}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t}$$

### Step 6: Substitute Fick's First Law
Since $J_A = -D_{AB}\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}$ (assuming constant $D_{AB}$):

$$-\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-D_{AB}\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}\right) = \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t}$$

$$D_{AB}\frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2} = \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t}$$

### Step 7: Final Form
$$\boxed{\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalisation:** For 3-D Cartesian coordinates with a homogeneous chemical reaction rate $R_A$ (kmol/m³·s):
> $$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\left(\frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}\right) + R_A$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Solution for Semi-Infinite Slab

**Problem:** A semi-infinite medium initially at concentration $C_{A0}$ has its surface concentration suddenly raised to $C_{As}$ at $t = 0$. Find $C_A(x,t)$.

**Initial Condition (IC):** $C_A(x, 0) = C_{A0}$
**Boundary Conditions (BCs):**
- $C_A(0, t) = C_{As}$ for $t > 0$
- $C_A(x \to \infty, t) = C_{A0}$ for all $t$

### Step 1: Dimensionless Variables
Define:
$$\theta = \frac{C_A - C_{A0}}{C_{As} - C_{A0}}, \quad \eta = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{D_{AB}t}}$$

### Step 2: Transform the PDE
By the chain rule:
$$\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial t} = -\frac{\eta}{2t}\frac{d\theta}{d\eta}, \quad \frac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{4D_{AB}t}\frac{d^2\theta}{d\eta^2}$$

### Step 3: Substitute into Fick's Second Law
$$-\frac{\eta}{2t}\frac{d\theta}{d\eta} = D_{AB} \cdot \frac{1}{4D_{AB}t}\frac{d^2\theta}{d\eta^2}$$

$$\frac{d^2\theta}{d\eta^2} + 2\eta\frac{d\theta}{d\eta} = 0$$

### Step 4: Integrate Twice
Let $p = d\theta/d\eta$. Then:
$$\frac{dp}{d\eta} + 2\eta p = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; p = A_1 e^{-\eta^2}$$

Integrating again:
$$\theta = A_1 \int_0^{\eta} e^{-u^2}du + A_2 = A_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\,\mathrm{erf}(\eta) + A_2$$

### Step 5: Apply BCs
- $\theta(0) = 1 \Rightarrow A_2 = 1$
- $\theta(\infty) = 0 \Rightarrow A_1 = -\frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}$

### Step 6: Final Solution
$$\boxed{\frac{C_A - C_{A0}}{C_{As} - C_{A0}} = 1 - \mathrm{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{D_{AB}t}}\right) = \mathrm{erfc}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{D_{AB}t}}\right)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Higbie's Penetration Theory

**Concept:** Fluid elements at the interface are continuously replaced by fresh bulk fluid. Each element stays at the interface for an average exposure time $t_e$ and is then replaced.

### Step 1: Unsteady Diffusion in a Fluid Element
A fluid element at the interface experiences 1-D unsteady diffusion into its depth $z$, with the surface concentration held constant:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$$

### Step 2: Boundary Conditions
- $C_A(z, 0) = C_{A,\infty}$ (element arrives with bulk concentration)
- $C_A(0, t) = C_{A,s}$ (surface concentration)
- $C_A(z \to \infty, t) = C_{A,\infty}$ (deep interior remains at bulk value)

### Step 3: Use the Standard Semi-Infinite Solution
By analogy with Section 3.2:

$$\frac{C_A - C_{A,\infty}}{C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}} = \mathrm{erfc}\left(\frac{z}{2\sqrt{D_{AB}t}}\right)$$

### Step 4: Compute Instantaneous Mass Transfer Rate
The flux at the surface $(z = 0)$ is:

$$N_A(t) = -D_{AB}\left.\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z}\right|_{z=0}$$

Differentiating the error function solution:

$$N_A(t) = (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t}}$$

### Step 5: Average Over the Exposure Time $t_e$
$$\bar{N}_A = \frac{1}{t_e}\int_0^{t_e} N_A(t)\,dt = (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_e}}\cdot\frac{1}{t_e}\int_0^{t_e}\frac{dt}{\sqrt{t}}$$

$$\bar{N}_A = (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_e}} \cdot \frac{2\sqrt{t_e}}{t_e} = 2(C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_e}}$$

### Step 6: Identify the Mass Transfer Coefficient
Comparing with $N_A = k_c(C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$:

$$\boxed{k_c = 2\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_e}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** Penetration theory gives $k_c \propto \sqrt{D_{AB}}$ whereas film theory gives $k_c \propto D_{AB}$. Experimental evidence strongly supports the $\sqrt{D_{AB}}$ dependence, making penetration theory more accurate for gas absorption with short contact times.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Two-Film Theory – Overall Mass Transfer Coefficient

### Step 1: Individual Film Fluxes
Consider gas (G) and liquid (L) phases in contact. In the gas film:

$$N_A = k_y(y_A - y_{A,i})$$

In the liquid film:

$$N_A = k_x(x_{A,i} - x_A)$$

### Step 2: Equilibrium at the Interface
The interface concentrations lie on the equilibrium curve $y_A^* = m x_A$. Thus $y_{A,i} = m x_{A,i}$.

### Step 3: Eliminate Interface Concentrations
From the gas film:
$$y_{A,i} = y_A - \frac{N_A}{k_y}$$

From the liquid film:
$$x_{A,i} = x_A + \frac{N_A}{k_x}$$

Substitute into the equilibrium relation $y_{A,i} = m x_{A,i}$:

$$y_A - \frac{N_A}{k_y} = m\left(x_A + \frac{N_A}{k_x}\right)$$

### Step 4: Solve for $N_A$
$$y_A - m x_A = N_A\left(\frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x}\right)$$

$$N_A = \frac{y_A - m x_A}{\dfrac{1}{k_y} + \dfrac{m}{k_x}}$$

### Step 5: Define Overall Coefficient
Define $K_y$ such that $N_A = K_y(y_A - y_A^*)$, where $y_A^* = m x_A$:

$$K_y(y_A - m x_A) = \frac{y_A - m x_A}{\dfrac{1}{k_y} + \dfrac{m}{k_x}}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symmetric relation (overall based on liquid phase):**
> $$\frac{1}{K_x} = \frac{1}{k_x} + \frac{1}{m k_y}$$
> Note that $K_y = m K_x$ — these are not independent.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
