# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define local and overall mass transfer coefficients. How is the average mass transfer coefficient obtained from the local one?**

**Model Answer:**

The **local mass transfer coefficient** $k_{local}$ is the proportionality factor at a specific point on the transfer surface that relates the local flux to the local driving force:

$$N_{A,\,local} = k_{local}\,\Delta C_{A,\,local}$$

The **overall (or average) mass transfer coefficient** $k_{avg}$ is the spatially averaged value that, when multiplied by a representative bulk driving force, gives the **total** mass transfer rate over the entire area $A$:

$$k_{avg} = \frac{1}{A}\int_A k_{local}\,dA$$

For a one-dimensional surface of length $L$:

$$k_{avg} = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L k_{local}(x)\,dx$$

The overall coefficient is the engineer's working tool — it condenses the entire spatially varying local behavior into a single, design-ready number. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**State the relationship between the overall mass transfer coefficient $K_y$ and the individual phase coefficients $k_y$ and $k_x$. When is the gas phase said to be "controlling"?**

**Model Answer:**

The two-phase overall coefficient, derived from a steady-state two-film model with linear equilibrium ($y^* = m\,x$), is:

$$\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x}$$

This is the **resistance-in-series** form. The first term $1/k_y$ is the **gas-side resistance**, and the second term $m/k_x$ is the **liquid-side resistance** (in gas-equivalent units).

**Controlling phase criterion:**
- **Gas-phase controlling** when $1/k_y \gg m/k_x$ (i.e., $k_y \ll m\,k_x$).
- **Liquid-phase controlling** when $m/k_x \gg 1/k_y$ (i.e., $m\,k_x \ll k_y$).

Physically, this occurs when the equilibrium slope $m$ is very small (very soluble gas — gas film dominates) or very large (sparingly soluble gas — liquid film dominates). (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question is worth 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The internal choice requires **two fully independent alternatives** — students answer **either** Q(A) **or** Q(B).

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Define the local and overall mass transfer coefficients. Starting from the two-film theory, derive the relation:
$$\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x}$$
clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Pure $\text{O}_2$ is absorbed into water from an $\text{O}_2$–air mixture at **298 K** and **1 atm** in a wetted-wall column. The bulk gas contains $21\% \; \text{O}_2$ and the equilibrium dissolved oxygen concentration in water (in equilibrium with this gas) is $C_{A,L}^* = 0.25 \; \text{mol/m}^3$. The individual gas-side coefficient is $k_G = 2.6 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{kmol/(m}^2\text{·s·Pa)}$ and the individual liquid-side coefficient is $k_L = 1.4 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m/s}$. The Henry's law constant is $H = 4.0 \times 10^4 \; \text{atm}$ (so $p_A^* = H\,x_A$).

Compute:
1. The overall coefficient $K_G$ in $\text{kmol/(m}^2\text{·s·Pa)}$.
2. The flux of $\text{O}_2$ absorption in $\text{kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$.
3. The percentage of total resistance offered by the gas phase. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Assumptions of the two-film model:**
1. Steady-state transfer — no accumulation at the interface.
2. Each phase has a thin, stagnant film near the interface; resistance is concentrated in these films.
3. Mass transfer within each film is by molecular diffusion (Fick's law).
4. Equilibrium exists **only at the interface**; bulk phases are uniform.
5. The equilibrium relation is linear: $y_A^* = m\,x_A$ (valid for dilute systems / Henry's law regime).

**Derivation (compact form — full expansion already given in Section 3.2):**

Writing flux in each film:
$$N_A = k_y(y_{A,b} - y_{A,i}) \quad\text{(gas film)}$$
$$N_A = k_x(x_{A,i} - x_{A,b}) \quad\text{(liquid film)}$$

Using $y_{A,i} = m x_{A,i}$ to eliminate the interfacial compositions, solving for $N_A$ and re-expressing with $y_A^* = m x_{A,b}$:

$$\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x} \quad\blacksquare$$

(7 marks — distributed as: 1 mark for definitions, 2 marks for assumptions, 4 marks for derivation steps.)

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:**
- $T = 298 \; \text{K}$, $P = 1 \; \text{atm} = 1.013 \times 10^5 \; \text{Pa}$
- $y_{A,b} = 0.21$, $H = 4.0 \times 10^4 \; \text{atm}$
- $k_G = 2.6 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{kmol/(m}^2\text{·s·Pa)}$
- $k_L = 1.4 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m/s}$
- $C_{A,L}^* = 0.25 \; \text{mol/m}^3 = 0.25 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kmol/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Express everything in common units.**

Total molar concentration (ideal gas):
$$C = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{1.013 \times 10^5}{8.314 \times 298} = 40.88 \; \text{mol/m}^3 = 0.04088 \; \text{kmol/m}^3$$

Bulk partial pressure of $\text{O}_2$:
$$p_{A,b} = y_{A,b}\,P = 0.21 \times 1.013 \times 10^5 = 2.127 \times 10^4 \; \text{Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Find the equilibrium partial pressure $p_A^*$ corresponding to bulk liquid composition.**

The Henry form $p_A^* = H\,x_A$ requires $x_A$. For a dilute liquid:
$$x_A \approx \frac{C_{A,L}^*}{C_{L,\,total}} \approx \frac{0.25 \times 10^{-3}}{55.5} = 4.50 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$p_A^* = 4.0 \times 10^4 \; \text{atm} \times 4.50 \times 10^{-6} = 0.180 \; \text{atm} = 0.180 \times 1.013 \times 10^5 = 1.823 \times 10^4 \; \text{Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Find the overall coefficient $K_G$ via resistance in series.**

First, compute $k_y$ from $k_G$:
$$k_y = k_G \cdot P = 2.6 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.013 \times 10^5 = 2.634 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$$

The liquid-side coefficient must be expressed in **gas-equivalent** units. Use $k_x = k_L \cdot C_L$ (with $C_L \approx 55.5 \; \text{kmol/m}^3$ for water):
$$k_x = 1.4 \times 10^{-5} \times 55.5 = 7.77 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$$

The slope $m$ of the equilibrium line in $y$–$x$ coordinates: for $p = H x$ in atm units, $y = p/P = (H/P)\,x$, so $m = H/P = 4.0 \times 10^4$.

Now:
$$\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x} = \frac{1}{2.634 \times 10^{-3}} + \frac{4.0 \times 10^4}{7.77 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$= 379.65 + 5.148 \times 10^7 \;\text{m}^2\text{·s/kmol}$$

The second term **dominates completely** — confirming gas-phase control is *not* the case here; rather, the **liquid phase overwhelmingly controls** (this is consistent with the well-known fact that $\text{O}_2$ absorption is liquid-film controlled due to its low solubility).

$$K_y \approx \frac{1}{5.148 \times 10^7} = 1.942 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$$

Convert back: $K_G = K_y / P$:
$$K_G = \frac{1.942 \times 10^{-8}}{1.013 \times 10^5} = 1.917 \times 10^{-13} \; \text{kmol/(m}^2\text{·s·Pa)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the flux.**

$$N_A = K_G\,(p_{A,b} - p_A^*) = 1.917 \times 10^{-13} \times (2.127 \times 10^4 - 1.823 \times 10^4)$$

$$= 1.917 \times 10^{-13} \times 3.04 \times 10^3 = 5.83 \times 10^{-10} \; \text{kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$$

**Step 5 — Percentage of gas-phase resistance.**

$$\%\text{Gas resistance} = \frac{1/k_y}{1/K_y} \times 100 = \frac{379.65}{5.148 \times 10^7} \times 100 \approx 7.4 \times 10^{-4}\,\%$$

$$\%\text{Liquid resistance} \approx 100\,\%$$

> **Conclusion:** The **liquid film overwhelmingly controls** the absorption of $\text{O}_2$ into water. Any attempt to improve the column should target $k_L$ (e.g., higher liquid flow, packing with greater wetted area).

(7 marks — distributed: 1 mark for unit conversions, 2 marks for overall coefficient, 2 marks for flux, 2 marks for percentage resistance.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3, Alternate]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, distinguish between **local** and **overall (average)** mass transfer coefficients. Explain how the local coefficient is integrated to obtain the average coefficient for a flat plate with a non-uniform $k_c(x) = 0.02\,x^{0.5} \; \text{m/s}$, where $x$ is in metres and the plate length is $L = 1 \; \text{m}$. Compute the **average** coefficient. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a counter-current gas absorption column, the gas contains $5 \; \text{mol}\% \; \text{SO}_2$ entering and $1 \; \text{mol}\% \; \text{SO}_2$ leaving. Pure water enters at the top. The operating line is $Y = 1.2\,X$ (mol ratio form) and the equilibrium line is $Y^* = 25\,X$. The individual gas coefficient is $k_y = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$ and the individual liquid coefficient is $k_x = 4.0 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$.

Determine:
1. The overall coefficients $K_y$ and $K_x$.
2. The driving force at the **top** of the column (in gas-phase mole-fraction units).
3. The flux at the top of the column. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Distinction (sketch in words):**
- **Local coefficient** $k_c(x)$ is the proportionality constant at position $x$ on the surface. It is point-specific and generally varies along the surface.
- **Overall (average) coefficient** $k_{avg}$ is the spatial mean that, when used with a single bulk driving force, reproduces the total mass transfer.

**Integration Formula:**
$$k_{avg} = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L k_c(x)\,dx = \frac{1}{1}\int_0^1 0.02\,x^{0.5}\,dx$$

**Compute the integral:**

$$= 0.02\left[\frac{x^{3/2}}{3/2}\right]_0^1 = 0.02 \times \frac{2}{3}\left[1^{3/2} - 0\right] = 0.02 \times \frac{2}{3}$$

$$\boxed{k_{avg} = 0.01333 \; \text{m/s}}$$

(7 marks — distributed: 2 marks distinction, 2 marks integration setup, 2 marks evaluation, 1 mark correct unit.)

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:** $m = 25$ (slope of equilibrium line in $Y$–$X$ basis; for dilute $X \to x$, $Y \to y$, so $m = 25$ in the mole-fraction form too).

**Step 1 — Compute $K_y$:**

$$\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x} = \frac{1}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} + \frac{25}{4.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$= 666.67 + 6250 = 6916.67 \; \text{m}^2\text{·s/kmol}$$

$$K_y = 1.446 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $K_x$ via $K_y = m\,K_x$:**

$$K_x = \frac{K_y}{m} = \frac{1.446 \times 10^{-4}}{25} = 5.78 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$$

**Step 3 — Driving force at the top of the column (gas exit end).**

At the top, $y_{A,\,out} = 0.01$ (gas leaving). For pure water input, $X_{top} = 0$, so $x_{A,\,top} \approx 0$, and $y_A^* = m\,x = 0$.

$$\Delta y = y_{A,b} - y_A^* = 0.01 - 0 = 0.01$$

**Step 4 — Flux at the top:**

$$N_A = K_y\,\Delta y = 1.446 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.01 = 1.446 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$$

(7 marks — distributed: 2 marks for $K_y$, 1 mark for $K_x$, 2 marks for driving force, 2 marks for flux.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Most Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–2 marks typical):** Forgetting to convert $k_G$ to $k_y$ via $k_y = k_G P$, or $k_L$ to $k_x$ via $k_x = k_L C_L$. The two-phase formula **requires** both coefficients in mole-fraction units. Mixing $k_G$ and $k_L$ directly in the formula is a classic error.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Slope $m$ in the Liquid-Side Term (–2 marks):** The formula is $1/K_y = 1/k_y + m/k_x$ — the $m$ is *not* present in the gas-side term. Students who write $1/K_y = 1/k_y + 1/k_x$ lose the resistance-balance interpretation marks.
>
> 3. **Confusing Local and Average (–1 to –2 marks):** A "local coefficient" problem requires an integral; an "average" or "overall" problem does not. Reading the question carefully is half the battle.
>
> 4. **Equilibrium Relation Mis-Identification (–1 mark):** Using Raoult's law when Henry's law is appropriate (or vice versa) for the solute–solvent pair. Always check the **phase diagram** first.
>
> 5. **No Units in Final Answer (–1 mark):** KTU 2024 strict marking scheme deducts marks for unitless numerical answers in Part B.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin This Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **Local** $k$ → pointwise; **Average** $k$ → area-averaged integral; **Overall** $K$ → two-phase combined.
- [ ] Single-phase flux: $N_A = k_c\,\Delta C = k_y\,\Delta y = k_p\,\Delta p$ (all equal!).
- [ ] Conversion (ideal gas): $k_c = k_y / C = k_p\,RT = k_p\,P/(C) \cdot (1/RT)$ — chain reduces to $k_c\,C = k_y = k_p\,P$.
- [ ] Overall (gas-based): $\dfrac{1}{K_y} = \dfrac{1}{k_y} + \dfrac{m}{k_x}$ — **resistance in series**.
- [ ] Overall (liquid-based): $\dfrac{1}{K_x} = \dfrac{1}{k_x} + \dfrac{1}{m\,k_y}$.
- [ ] Reciprocity: $K_y = m\,K_x$.
- [ ] **Controlling phase:** $m$ small → gas controls; $m$ large → liquid controls.
- [ ] Average from local: $k_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{L}\displaystyle\int_0^L k_{local}(x)\,dx$.
- [ ] Dimensionless numbers: $\text{Sh} = k_c L / D_{AB}$, $\text{Sc} = \nu/D_{AB}$, $\text{St}_m = k_c / v_\infty = \text{Sh}/(\text{Re}\,\text{Sc})$.
- [ ] **Equilibrium tie-line:** $y_A^* = m x_A$ (linear, dilute) — links the two phases' bulk compositions.
- [ ] Two-film model assumptions: steady-state, thin stagnant films, equilibrium at interface only, linear equilibrium.
- [ ] Heat-transfer analogy: $h \leftrightarrow k$, $\Delta T \leftrightarrow \Delta C$ or $\Delta y$, $1/U \leftrightarrow 1/K_y$.
- [ ] Gas absorption → $K_G$ used with $(p_A - p_A^*)$; liquid extraction → $K_x$ used with $(x_A^* - x_A)$.
- [ ] **Always** carry units; KTU valuation penalizes bare numbers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Phase and Liquid Phase-Controlled Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT746 - ADVANCED HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Gas Phase and Liquid Phase-Controlled Systems # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In any **gas-liquid mass transfer operation** (absorption, stripping, humidification, dehumidification, or condensation/evaporation), mass must diffuse sequentially through two stagnant films — the **gas film** adjacent to the gas bulk and the **liquid film** adjacent to the liquid bulk. The **two-resistance concept** (Whitman, 1923) states that each film offers its own resistance, and the **overall mass transfer resistance is the sum** of the two individual film resistances. A system is classified as: - **Gas-Phase Controlled** when the gas-film resistance dominates; the liquid-film resistance is negligible, and the gas-side driving force alone dictates the transfer rate. - **Liquid-Phase Controlled** when the liquid-film resistance dominates; the gas-side equilibrium is essentially established at the interface, and the liquid-side driving force alone dictates the transfer rate. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The controlling phase identification is a high-weightage question (Part-A 3 marks / Part-B 14 marks). You must justify your answer using the ratio of individual phase resistances — *not* by intuition alone. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Toll Booth Model Imagine two consecutive toll booths on a highway — **Booth-G** (gas side) and **Booth-L** (liquid side) — through which cars (solute molecules) must pass. - If **Booth-G is open and fast** (low gas-film resistance) but **Booth-L is slow and congested** (high liquid-film resistance), then the *overall speed of traffic* is dictated by Booth-L. This is the **liquid-phase controlled** case. - Conversely, if Booth-L is fast and Booth-G is slow, the system is **gas-phase controlled**. - If both booths are equally slow, **both phases contribute** and the system is in a **mixed-resistance regime**. The phase that bottlenecks the traffic **controls** the operation. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Metrics in Bold - **Equilibrium slope (m)** — slope of the equilibrium line on an X–Y plot, dimensionless. - **Henry's constant (H)** — units of **atm·m³/mol** (or partial pressure/mole fraction). - **Gas-film coefficient (k_y)** — **kmol/m²·s·(Δy)**. - **Liquid-film coefficient (k_x)** — **kmol/m²·s·(Δx)**. - **Overall coefficients K_y, K_x** — same units as their individual counterparts. > [!NOTE] > The Henry's constant, often expressed in different units, is the **bridge** that determines which phase controls. Its magnitude relative to *operating pressure* sets the *m* value, which in turn decides the controlling phase. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two-Film Model — Operational Breakdown For a solute **A** transferring from gas to liquid at steady state: **Step 1 — Steady-state Flux Equality:** Because there is no accumulation at the gas–liquid interface, the flux through the gas film must equal the flux through the liquid film. $$N_A = k_y (y_{A,b} - y_{A,i}) = k_x (x_{A,i} - x_{A,b})$$ **Step 2 — Interface Equilibrium:** The interface is assumed to be in thermodynamic equilibrium, so the interfacial compositions $(y_{A,i}, x_{A,i})$ lie on the equilibrium curve. $$y_{A,i} = f(x_{A,i}) \quad \text{or for dilute systems} \quad y_{A,i} = m \cdot x_{A,i}$$ **Step 3 — Overall Flux in Terms of Bulk Driving Forces:** Eliminating the interfacial compositions using overall coefficients $K_y$ and $K_x$: $$N_A = K_y (y_{A,b} - y_A^*) = K_x (x_A^* - x_{A,b})$$ where $y_A^*$ is the gas composition in equilibrium with the bulk liquid, and $x_A^*$ is the liquid composition in equilibrium with the bulk gas. **Step 4 — Resistance-Addition Analogy:** For a linear equilibrium line of slope $m$: $$\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x}$$ $$\frac{1}{K_x} = \frac{1}{m \cdot k_y} + \frac{1}{k_x}$$ This is the **additivity of resistances** — the direct analogue of electrical resistances in series. **Step 5 — Identifying the Controlling Phase:** | Condition | Controlling Phase | Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $\dfrac{m/k_x}{1/k_y} \gg 1$ i.e. $\dfrac{1}{k_y} \ll \dfrac{m}{k_x}$ | **Gas phase** | Absorption of sparingly soluble gases (CO₂, O₂ in water) | | $\dfrac{m/k_x}{1/k_y} \ll 1$ i.e. $\dfrac{1}{k_y} \gg \dfrac{m}{k_x}$ | **Liquid phase** | Absorption of highly soluble gases (NH₃, HCl, SO₂ in water) | | Both terms comparable | **Both phases** | Absorption of moderately soluble gases (SO₂ in strong alkali) | **Step 6 — Real-World Engineering Utility:** - **Gas-phase controlled:** A *tall* packed column is required for low-soluble gases (e.g., CO₂ capture from flue gas in amine scrubbers operates in mixed mode; pure water scrubbing is gas-controlled). - **Liquid-phase controlled:** A *highly agitated* or *high-surface* contactor suffices; gas-side equipment design becomes trivial. E.g., NH₃ absorption in water. ## 2.2 Why 'm' Decides Control — The 'Why' Behind It The slope $m$ of the equilibrium line physically represents **how much gas-phase driving force is "consumed" per unit of liquid-phase driving force**. A large $m$ (steep curve) means a tiny change in liquid concentration creates a huge gas-concentration change at the interface — so the gas film can easily "keep up". The bottleneck therefore shifts to the liquid film. A small $m$ produces the opposite effect. ## 2.3 The 1/m Criterion for Mass Transfer Analogies For **Chilton–Colburn j-factor analogy** applications and **dimensionless correlation** derivations (e.g., $Sh = f(Re, Sc)$), the controlling phase identification is essential because the $j_D$ factor is applied to the phase of interest. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Typical Units | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $N_A = k_y (y_{Ab} - y_{Ai}) = k_x (x_{Ai} - x_{Ab})$ | Flux through each film (steady state) | $\text{kmol/m}^2\text{·s}$ | | 2 | $y_{Ai} = m x_{Ai}$ | Linear interfacial equilibrium | Dimensionless | | 3 | $N_A = K_y (y_{Ab} - y_A^*)$ | Flux in terms of gas-bulk driving force | $\text{kmol/m}^2\text{·s}$ | | 4 | $N_A = K_x (x_A^* - x_{Ab})$ | Flux in terms of liquid-bulk driving force | $\text{kmol/m}^2\text{·s}$ | | 5 | $\dfrac{1}{K_y} = \dfrac{1}{k_y} + \dfrac{m}{k_x}$ | Resistance in gas units | $\text{m}^2\text{·s/kmol}$ | | 6 | $\dfrac{1}{K_x} = \dfrac{1}{k_x} + \dfrac{1}{m k_y}$ | Resistance in liquid units | $\text{m}^2\text{·s/kmol}$ | | 7 | $K_y = \dfrac{k_y k_x}{k_x + m k_y}$ | Combined overall coefficient (gas) | $\text{kmol/m}^2\text{·s}$ | | 8 | $K_x = \dfrac{k_y k_x}{k_x + m k_y} \cdot m$ | Combined overall coefficient (liquid) | $\text{kmol/m}^2\text{·s}$ | | 9 | \% Gas resistance $= \dfrac{1/k_y}{1/K_y} \times 100$ | Resistance share — gas film | % | | 10 | \% Liquid resistance $= \dfrac{m/k_x}{1/K_y} \times 100$ | Resistance share — liquid film | % | | 11 | $K_G = \dfrac{K_y}{P}$ | Convert to partial-pressure basis | $\text{kmol/m}^2\text{·s·atm}$ | | 12 | $K_L = K_x \cdot \rho_L / M_L$ | Convert to concentration basis | $\text{m/s}$ | | 13 | $m = \dfrac{H}{P}$ (dilute) | Henry's law to slope conversion | Dimensionless | | 14 | $St \cdot Sc^{2/3} = j_D$ | Chilton–Colburn analogy | Dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's tip (KTU 2024):** Always explicitly state the **criterion for control** (e.g., "if $m k_y / k_x > 10$, gas phase controls") — a numerical threshold earns full marks; a vague "if gas resistance is high" earns partial credit only. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Film Fluxes to Overall Coefficient $\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x}$ This is the **most-tested derivation** in KTU under this topic. The board expects every algebraic line to be visible. **Starting point — Steady-state fluxes through the two films:** $$N_A = k_y (y_{Ab} - y_{Ai}) \quad \text{...(i)}$$ $$N_A = k_x (x_{Ai} - x_{Ab}) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Step 1:** Solve (i) for the interfacial gas composition: $$y_{Ai} = y_{Ab} - \frac{N_A}{k_y}$$ **Step 2:** Solve (ii) for the interfacial liquid composition: $$x_{Ai} = x_{Ab} + \frac{N_A}{k_x}$$ **Step 3:** Apply the linear equilibrium relation at the interface (valid for dilute systems): $$y_{Ai} = m \cdot x_{Ai}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute Step 1 and Step 2 into the equilibrium equation: $$y_{Ab} - \frac{N_A}{k_y} = m \left( x_{Ab} + \frac{N_A}{k_x} \right)$$ **Step 5:** Expand the right-hand side: $$y_{Ab} - \frac{N_A}{k_y} = m \, x_{Ab} + \frac{m \, N_A}{k_x}$$ **Step 6:** Recognize that $m \cdot x_{Ab} = y_A^*$ (gas composition in equilibrium with the bulk liquid), by the same linear-equilibrium assumption applied to the bulk liquid: $$y_{Ab} - \frac{N_A}{k_y} = y_A^* + \frac{m \, N_A}{k_x}$$ **Step 7:** Group all $N_A$ terms on the right: $$y_{Ab} - y_A^* = \frac{N_A}{k_y} + \frac{m \, N_A}{k_x}$$ **Step 8:** Factor out $N_A$ on the right: $$y_{Ab} - y_A^* = N_A \left( \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x} \right)$$ **Step 9:** Rearrange for the flux: $$N_A = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{k_y} + \dfrac{m}{k_x}} \, (y_{Ab} - y_A^*)$$ **Step 10:** By definition, $N_A = K_y (y_{Ab} - y_A^*)$. Therefore the bracketed term in the denominator is exactly $1/K_y$: $$\boxed{\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x}}$$ This is the **canonical two-resistance result in gas-phase concentration units**. ## 3.2 Limiting Case 1: Gas-Phase Control (Very Insoluble Solute) For a sparingly soluble gas, the equilibrium line is **flat** (small $m$). Substituting $m \to 0$ into the resistance expression: $$\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{0}{k_x} = \frac{1}{k_y}$$ $$\Rightarrow K_y \approx k_y \quad \text{and} \quad N_A \approx k_y (y_{Ab} - y_{Ai})$$ **Physical interpretation:** The liquid film is so permeable (or the gas is so insoluble that little dissolves) that its resistance vanishes. The gas film is the bottleneck. > [!NOTE] > **Example:** Pure water scrubbing of O₂ from air — $m \approx 30{,}000$ (very steep) — wait, that makes it **liquid-controlled**! A *small* $m$ corresponds to a *flat* line meaning the gas is **insoluble**. Recalibrate: **small m = insoluble gas = gas-side resistance dominates**. ## 3.3 Limiting Case 2: Liquid-Phase Control (Very Soluble Solute) For a highly soluble gas (e.g., NH₃ in water), the equilibrium line is **steep** (large $m$). Then $m/k_x$ is the dominant term: $$\frac{1}{K_y} \approx \frac{m}{k_x} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_y \approx \frac{k_x}{m}$$ $$N_A \approx \frac{k_x}{m}(y_{Ab} - y_A^*) = k_x(x_{Ai} - x_{Ab})$$ **Physical interpretation:** The gas film is so efficient (or the solute is so soluble that the interface instantly equilibrates) that its resistance vanishes. The liquid film dominates. ## 3.4 Derivation: Pure Gas Dissolving into a Solvent (Special Case) Consider **pure gas A** at partial pressure $P_A$ dissolving into a non-volatile solvent. Then $y_{Ab} = 1$, and the gas-phase resistance to mass transfer of A through *itself* is meaningless (no driving force in the gas film for species A because the concentration gradient is in the stagnant-film of inert gas B). In this scenario, the **gas-film resistance is zero** ($1/k_y \to 0$) and the controlling resistance is the liquid film alone: $$K_x = k_x \quad \text{and} \quad N_A = k_x (x_{Ai} - x_{Ab})$$ With $x_{Ai}$ set by $P_A$ via Henry's law. This is a **textbook case of pure liquid-phase control**. ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Model Question Type) **Problem:** Acetone is being absorbed from a nitrogen stream into water at 1 atm, 25 °C. The equilibrium relation is $y^* = 2.0 x$. At a point in the column, $k_y = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ kmol/m²·s and $k_x = 2.4 \times 10^{-3}$ kmol/m²·s. Determine (a) the overall gas-phase coefficient $K_y$, and (b) identify the controlling phase with a % resistance analysis. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Compute the individual resistance terms. $$\frac{1}{k_y} = \frac{1}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} = 833.3 \; \text{m}^2\text{·s/kmol}$$ $$\frac{m}{k_x} = \frac{2.0}{2.4 \times 10^{-3}} = 833.3 \; \text{m}^2\text{·s/kmol}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the resistance-addition formula: $$\frac{1}{K_y} = 833.3 + 833.3 = 1666.7 \; \text{m}^2\text{·s/kmol}$$ **Step 3:** Compute $K_y$: $$K_y = \frac{1}{1666.7} = 6.0 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{kmol/m}^2\text{·s}$$ **Step 4:** Compute the % resistance share: $$\% \text{ Gas resistance} = \frac{833.3}{1666.7} \times 100 = 50\%$$ $$\% \text{ Liquid resistance} = \frac{833.3}{1666.7} \times 100 = 50\%$$ **Step 5:** Conclude: > **Both phases contribute equally — mixed-resistance regime.** This is because the equilibrium slope $m = 2$ is *not* extreme in either direction, and the individual coefficients are comparable. If, however, $m$ were $0.05$ (acetone-like solubility re-evaluated hypothetically), then $m/k_x = 20.83$ vs $1/k_y = 833.3$, making the system **>97% gas-film controlled**. ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Classification Decision Tree | Equilibrium Slope $m$ | Relative magnitude of $k_y$ vs $k_x$ | Controlling Phase | | :---: | :---: | :---: | | $m \ll 1$ (flat line) | Any | **Gas phase** | | $m \gg 1$ (steep line) | Any | **Liquid phase** | | $m \approx 1$ | $k_y \ll k_x$ | **Gas phase** | | $m \approx 1$ | $k_y \gg k_x$ | **Liquid phase** | | $m \approx 1$ | $k_y \approx k_x$ | **Both phases** | | $y_{Ab} = 1$ (pure gas) | Any | **Liquid phase (degenerate)** | | $x_{Ab} = 0$ (pure solvent) | Any | **Gas phase (degenerate)** | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Two-Film Mass Transfer Architecture The following **Mermaid block diagram** captures the architecture of the two-film model with control-phase identification logic. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Gas Bulk y_Ab] --> B[Gas Film k_y] B --> C[Interface y_Ai, x_Ai on equilibrium] C --> D[Liquid Film k_x] D --> E[Liquid Bulk x_Ab] B -.flux.-> F[N_A = k_y y_Ab - y_Ai] C -.equilibrium.-> G[y_Ai = m x_Ai] D -.flux.-> H[N_A = k_x x_Ai - x_Ab] F --> I[Steady-state flux equality] H --> I I --> J[Overall Flux N_A] G --> J J --> K{Resistance Comparison} K -->|1/k_y >> m/k_x| L[Gas-Phase Controlled] K -->|1/k_y << m/k_x| M[Liquid-Phase Controlled] K -->|1/k_y approx m/k_x| N[Both Phases Contribute] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Resistance-Addition Electrical Analogue A high-yield visualization that maps mass transfer resistances to electrical resistances in series. ```mermaid flowchart LR R1[Gas-film Resistance 1 / k_y] --- R2[Liquid-film Resistance m / k_x] R1 --- R3[Overall Resistance 1 / K_y] R2 --- R3 V1[Gas-side Driving Force y_Ab - y_A*] --- R1 V2[Liquid-side Driving Force x_A* - x_Ab] --- R2 R3 --- I[Resultant Flux N_A] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic: Gas-Phase vs Liquid-Phase Control Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Identify Operation]) --> Q1{Is solute very soluble?} Q1 -->|Yes - steep eq line| LC[Liquid-Phase Controlled] Q1 -->|No - flat eq line| Q2{Is solute very insoluble?} Q2 -->|Yes| GC[Gas-Phase Controlled] Q2 -->|No| Q3{Compare 1/k_y vs m/k_x} Q3 -->|Ratio > 10| GC Q3 -->|Ratio < 0.1| LC Q3 -->|0.1 to 10| BOTH[Both Phases] LC --> EX1[NH3 absorption in water
SO2 in alkali
pure gas into solvent] GC --> EX2[CO2 in water
O2 in water
pure solvent receiving gas] BOTH --> EX3[Acetone in water
SO2 in pure water
moderate solubility gases] ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram: Real-World Equipment Implications ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph GCP[Gas-Phase Controlled Systems] G1[Tall Packed Column] --> G2[High-pressure operation reduces m] G2 --> G3[Larger specific surface area needed] G3 --> G4[Example: CO2 scrubber with water] end subgraph LCP[Liquid-Phase Controlled Systems] L1[Agitated contactor] --> L2[Increased turbulence in liquid] L2 --> L3[Higher liquid flow rates] L3 --> L4[Example: NH3 absorber in water] end ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define 'gas-phase controlled' and 'liquid-phase controlled' systems with one industrial example each. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Model Answer:** A **gas-phase controlled system** is one in which the resistance to mass transfer in the gas film $\left(\dfrac{1}{k_y}\right)$ is much greater than the resistance in the liquid film $\left(\dfrac{m}{k_x}\right)$, so the gas-side driving force alone dictates the rate. **Example:** Absorption of sparingly soluble CO₂ into water in a packed absorption column. A **liquid-phase controlled system** is one in which the liquid-film resistance $\left(\dfrac{m}{k_x}\right)$ is much greater than the gas-film resistance $\left(\dfrac{1}{k_y}\right)$, so the liquid-side driving force alone dictates the rate. **Example:** Absorption of highly soluble NH₃ into water. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write *"gas phase controls when gas-side mass transfer is small"* — this is the **opposite** phrasing of the resistance. Always say *"gas-film resistance is dominant"* to avoid the 1-mark penalty. --- ### Q2. State the two-resistance theory and write the final relationship between overall and individual coefficients. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Model Answer:** The **two-resistance theory** (Lewis–Whitman, 1924) postulates that mass transfer between a gas and a liquid occurs by molecular diffusion through two stagnant films — one in the gas phase and one in the liquid phase — with each film offering an independent resistance to mass transfer. The interface is in thermodynamic equilibrium. The overall coefficient and individual coefficients are related by: $$\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x} \quad \text{(in gas concentration units)}$$ $$\frac{1}{K_x} = \frac{1}{k_x} + \frac{1}{m k_y} \quad \text{(in liquid concentration units)}$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Module 3 Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) #### (a) Derive the relationship $\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x}$ starting from the two-film model. Discuss the limiting cases of gas-phase and liquid-phase control. **[7 marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Write the steady-state flux through each film:** $$N_A = k_y (y_{Ab} - y_{Ai}) \quad \text{...(1)}$$ $$N_A = k_x (x_{Ai} - x_{Ab}) \quad \text{...(2)}$$ **Step 2 — Express interfacial compositions:** From (1): $y_{Ai} = y_{Ab} - \dfrac{N_A}{k_y}$ From (2): $x_{Ai} = x_{Ab} + \dfrac{N_A}{k_x}$ **Step 3 — Apply interface equilibrium $y_{Ai} = m x_{Ai}$:** $$y_{Ab} - \frac{N_A}{k_y} = m \left( x_{Ab} + \frac{N_A}{k_x} \right) = m x_{Ab} + \frac{m N_A}{k_x}$$ **Step 4 — Recognize that $m x_{Ab} = y_A^*$** (gas concentration in equilibrium with bulk liquid): $$y_{Ab} - y_A^* = N_A \left( \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x} \right)$$ **Step 5 — Identify the bracketed term with $1/K_y$:** $$\boxed{\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x}}$$ **Step 6 — Limiting Case Analysis:** *Case (i) — Gas-phase control:* If $m$ is very small (sparingly soluble gas), then $\dfrac{m}{k_x} \to 0$, giving $K_y \approx k_y$. The liquid film offers negligible resistance, and the gas film controls. *Case (ii) — Liquid-phase control:* If $m$ is very large (highly soluble gas), then $\dfrac{m}{k_x} \gg \dfrac{1}{k_y}$, giving $\dfrac{1}{K_y} \approx \dfrac{m}{k_x}$ and $K_y \approx \dfrac{k_x}{m}$. The gas film offers negligible resistance, and the liquid film controls. *Case (iii) — Both phases contribute:* When the two resistance terms are comparable, neither film can be neglected. **Industrial Implications:** - For gas-phase controlled systems, a *tall* column is required, with small packing (high interfacial area). - For liquid-phase controlled systems, *agitation* and *higher liquid rates* improve transfer; column height requirements are reduced. #### (b) For a gas absorption system, $k_G = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ kmol/m²·s·atm and $k_L = 1.0 \times 10^{-2}$ m/s. The system operates at 10 atm total pressure, and the equilibrium relation is $y = 30x$. Calculate the overall gas-phase coefficient $K_G$, the % resistance in each phase, and identify the controlling phase. **[7 marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $k_G = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ kmol/m²·s·atm - $k_L = 1.0 \times 10^{-2}$ m/s - $P = 10$ atm - $m = 30$ (dimensionless) **Step 1 — Convert $k_L$ to liquid-mole-fraction basis:** Assuming dilute solution with molar density $C_L \approx 55.5$ kmol/m³ (water-like solvent): $$k_x = k_L \cdot C_L = (1.0 \times 10^{-2}) \times 55.5 = 0.555 \; \text{kmol/m}^2\text{·s}$$ **Step 2 — Convert $k_G$ to $k_y$ basis:** $$k_y = k_G \cdot P = (1.5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 10 = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kmol/m}^2\text{·s}$$ **Step 3 — Compute individual resistance terms:** $$\frac{1}{k_y} = \frac{1}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 666.67 \; \text{m}^2\text{·s/kmol}$$ $$\frac{m}{k_x} = \frac{30}{0.555} = 54.05 \; \text{m}^2\text{·s/kmol}$$ **Step 4 — Compute overall resistance and $K_y$:** $$\frac{1}{K_y} = 666.67 + 54.05 = 720.72 \; \text{m}^2\text{·s/kmol}$$ $$K_y = 1.388 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kmol/m}^2\text{·s}$$ **Step 5 — Convert back to $K_G$:** $$K_G = \frac{K_y}{P} = \frac{1.388 \times 10^{-3}}{10} = 1.388 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{kmol/m}^2\text{·s·atm}$$ **Step 6 — Compute % resistance share:** $$\% \text{ Gas resistance} = \frac{666.67}{720.72} \times 100 = 92.5\%$$ $$\% \text{ Liquid resistance} = \frac{54.05}{720.72} \times 100 = 7.5\%$$ **Step 7 — Conclusion:** > **The system is GAS-PHASE CONTROLLED**, since the gas-film resistance accounts for ~92.5% of the total resistance. Practical engineering actions: increase gas-phase turbulence, use small packings, or increase gas flow rate. Improving liquid-side agitation will have negligible effect. > [!WARNING] > **Common Valuation Error:** Students frequently forget to convert $k_L$ from m/s to kmol/m²·s units using $C_L$, leading to a 3-mark deduction. Always normalize units *before* adding resistances. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice #### (a) Explain the two-film theory of mass transfer. Using a neat sketch, illustrate the concentration profiles of a solute transferring from a gas to a liquid. **[7 marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2022]` **Model Solution:** **Two-Film Theory — Explanation:** The two-film theory (Lewis–Whitman, 1924) is the classical model for gas-liquid mass transfer. Its postulates are: 1. Near the gas–liquid interface, two stagnant films exist — one in the gas phase and one in the liquid phase. 2. Mass transfer through each film occurs by **steady-state molecular diffusion** (Fick's law). 3. The bulk gas and bulk liquid phases are perfectly mixed, so concentration gradients exist only in the two films. 4. The **interface itself is in thermodynamic equilibrium**, so the interfacial compositions $(y_{Ai}, x_{Ai})$ lie on the equilibrium curve. **Concentration Profile Sketch (described):** A plot of concentration vs distance perpendicular to the interface shows: - A **flat region** in the gas bulk at $y_{Ab}$. - A **linear drop** across the gas film from $y_{Ab}$ to $y_{Ai}$. - A **discontinuity** at the interface (because of the change in phase and the equilibrium jump). - A **linear drop** across the liquid film from $x_{Ai}$ to $x_{Ab}$. - A **flat region** in the liquid bulk at $x_{Ab}$. **Mathematical Representation:** The steady-state flux through each film: $$N_A = k_y (y_{Ab} - y_{Ai}) = k_x (x_{Ai} - x_{Ab})$$ The interface equilibrium: $$y_{Ai} = m \, x_{Ai}$$ **Significance:** The theory converts an unknown interfacial composition into a known overall driving force using the resistance-addition formula, enabling direct design of equipment. #### (b) In an absorption column, acetone is absorbed from air into water. The equilibrium relation is $y = 1.8x$. At one point in the column, the bulk gas contains 6 mol% acetone and the bulk liquid contains 0.04 mol fraction acetone. The individual film coefficients are $k_y = 3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ kmol/m²·s and $k_x = 4.0 \times 10^{-3}$ kmol/m²·s. Calculate (i) the overall gas-phase coefficient $K_y$, (ii) the flux $N_A$, and (iii) the interfacial compositions $y_{Ai}$ and $x_{Ai}$. **[7 marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $y_{Ab} = 0.06$, $x_{Ab} = 0.04$ - $m = 1.8$ - $k_y = 3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ kmol/m²·s, $k_x = 4.0 \times 10^{-3}$ kmol/m²·s **Step 1 — Overall coefficient $K_y$:** $$\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}} + \frac{1.8}{4.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$\frac{1}{K_y} = 285.71 + 450.00 = 735.71 \; \text{m}^2\text{·s/kmol}$$ $$K_y = 1.359 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kmol/m}^2\text{·s}$$ **Step 2 — Equilibrium gas composition at bulk liquid:** $$y_A^* = m \cdot x_{Ab} = 1.8 \times 0.04 = 0.072$$ **Step 3 — Flux using overall driving force:** $$N_A = K_y (y_{Ab} - y_A^*) = 1.359 \times 10^{-3} \times (0.06 - 0.072)$$ $$N_A = 1.359 \times 10^{-3} \times (-0.012) = -1.631 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{kmol/m}^2\text{·s}$$ The **negative sign** indicates that acetone is transferring from the liquid to the gas (this point is in the **stripping** region of the column, not absorption). The magnitude is the flux magnitude. **Step 4 — Interfacial compositions using film fluxes:** For magnitude of flux $|N_A| = 1.631 \times 10^{-5}$ kmol/m²·s: $$y_{Ai} = y_{Ab} - \frac{N_A}{k_y}$$ Taking magnitude and direction: $N_A$ from liquid to gas means $y_{Ai} > y_{Ab}$: $$y_{Ai} = y_{Ab} + \frac{|N_A|}{k_y} = 0.06 + \frac{1.631 \times 10^{-5}}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$y_{Ai} = 0.06 + 0.00466 = 0.0647$$ **Step 5 — Interfacial liquid composition:** $$x_{Ai} = \frac{y_{Ai}}{m} = \frac{0.0647}{1.8} = 0.0359$$ **Step 6 — Verification of equilibrium check:** $$m \cdot x_{Ai} = 1.8 \times 0.0359 = 0.0647 = y_{Ai} \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 7 — % Resistance Analysis:** $$\% \text{ Gas resistance} = \frac{285.71}{735.71} \times 100 = 38.83\%$$ $$\% \text{ Liquid resistance} = \frac{450.00}{735.71} \times 100 = 61.17\%$$ **Step 8 — Conclusion:** > **Both phases contribute significantly**, with the **liquid film slightly dominant (~61%)**. The point lies in a mixed-resistance regime, characteristic of moderately soluble organic vapours like acetone in water. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to compute the **equilibrium composition** $y_A^*$ and substitute the wrong driving force (e.g., $y_{Ab} - x_{Ab}$). Always convert bulk-phase compositions to the **same reference phase** before computing the overall driving force. A sign error in the flux direction will cost 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3 (Gas/Liquid Phase Controlled Systems)** - [x] The **two-film theory** is the foundation: two stagnant films, interface in equilibrium, bulk phases perfectly mixed. - [x] **Resistance-addition formulae** — memorize both forms: $\dfrac{1}{K_y} = \dfrac{1}{k_y} + \dfrac{m}{k_x}$ and $\dfrac{1}{K_x} = \dfrac{1}{k_x} + \dfrac{1}{m k_y}$. - [x] **Equilibrium slope $m$** is the *master variable* — large $m$ ⇒ liquid-controlled; small $m$ ⇒ gas-controlled. - [x] **Pure gas dissolving** → $1/k_y = 0$ → **always liquid-controlled**. - [x] **Pure solvent receiving gas** → $1/k_x = 0$ → **always gas-controlled**. - [x] **Threshold for control:** if one resistance term exceeds ~10× the other, the system is essentially controlled by that phase. - [x] **Unit conversions** are critical: $k_L$ in m/s must be converted using $C_L$ (kmol/m³) to get $k_x$ in kmol/m²·s. - [x] $K_G = K_y / P$ and $K_L \cdot C_L = K_x$ — keep these in mind for partial-pressure/concentration conversions. - [x] **Industrial design implication:** Gas-controlled → tall column, high gas-side turbulence; Liquid-controlled → high liquid flow, increased agitation, high surface area. - [x] **Numerical rule-of-thumb:** Acetone in water (m ≈ 1.8) → both phases; CO₂ in water (m ≈ 30) at low P → liquid-controlled; SO₂ in water (m ≈ 7) → mixed; O₂ in water (m ≈ 30,000) → strongly liquid-controlled. - [x] Always compute the **% resistance share** in numerical problems — the comparison ($>$ 90% or $<$ 10%) decides the controlling phase quantitatively. - [x] Negative flux sign in absorption calculations indicates **stripping** at that point — do not panic; report the magnitude with correct direction. ---

topics.title: Simultaneous Diffusion and Chemical Reaction: Homogeneous and Heterogeneous Reactions; Simultaneous Heat and Mass Transfer – Condensation of Hot Vapour on Solid Surface in Presence of Non-Condensable Gas, Steady State Binary Thermal Diffusion in a Two-Bulb Apparatus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT746 - ADVANCED HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Simultaneous Diffusion and Chemical Reaction: Homogeneous and Heterogeneous Reactions; Simultaneous Heat and Mass Transfer – Condensation of Hot Vapour on Solid Surface in Presence of Non-Condensable Gas, Steady State Binary Thermal Diffusion in a Two-Bulb Apparatus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Simultaneous Diffusion and Chemical Reaction — Formal Definition

In advanced mass transfer, diffusion rarely occurs in isolation. In most industrial reactors, absorbors, and catalytic systems, the diffusing species undergoes a **chemical transformation** either in the bulk of the fluid or at an interface. When the rate of molecular/eddy diffusion is comparable to the rate of reaction, the two phenomena must be solved **simultaneously** by coupling Fick's law of diffusion with a reaction rate expression.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Simultaneous diffusion and chemical reaction* refers to the coupled transport–kinetics problem in which the molar flux $N_A$ of a species A is governed jointly by its concentration gradient (Fickian diffusion) and its rate of consumption/production (reaction kinetics). The governing differential equation is obtained by writing a species mass balance over a differential element and inserting Fick's first law.

### 1.1.1 Homogeneous Reaction

A **homogeneous reaction** is one in which the reaction occurs **uniformly throughout the volume** of a single phase (gas or liquid). There is no distinct interface. Every differential fluid element acts as a microscopic reactor.

**Examples:**
- Combustion of methane in air: $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (gas phase)
- Acid–base neutralisation in aqueous solution
- Ozone decomposition: $2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow 3\text{O}_2$

For a first-order homogeneous reaction in a stagnant film, the species balance is:

$$\frac{D_{AB} \, d^2 C_A}{dx^2} - k \, C_A = 0$$

The dimensionless group that quantifies the relative importance of reaction to diffusion is the **Damköhler number**:

$$\text{Da} = \frac{k \, L^2}{D_{AB}}$$

### 1.1.2 Heterogeneous Reaction

A **heterogeneous reaction** occurs **at the interface between two phases** or **within a porous solid catalyst**. The reactant must first diffuse to the surface (or into the pores) before the reaction can occur.

**Examples:**
- Catalytic cracking of hydrocarbons over a zeolite pellet
- Gas–solid reaction: $\text{C(s)} + \text{O}_2\text{(g)} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2\text{(g)}$
- Dissolution of a solid in a liquid
- Electrode reactions at a metal–electrolyte interface

The performance of porous catalysts is described by the **Thiele modulus** $\phi$ and the **internal effectiveness factor** $\eta$:

$$\phi = L \sqrt{\frac{k}{D_{eff}}} \qquad \eta = \frac{\text{actual reaction rate}}{\text{rate at surface conditions}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Board-Exam Favourite):**
> - **Homogeneous** → Reaction throughout bulk → $D_{AB} \nabla^2 C_A - R_A = 0$
> - **Heterogeneous** → Reaction at surface/pore wall → Flux to surface matches surface reaction rate: $-D_{AB} \left.\frac{dC_A}{dx}\right|_{x=0} = k_s C_{A,s}$

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Diffusion + Reaction

**Analogy: A Crowded Elevator (Homogeneous) vs. A Ticket Counter (Heterogeneous)**

Imagine a building lobby:
- **Homogeneous reaction** = people moving randomly through a *crowded elevator*. Every person (molecule) is constantly reacting (conversing, exchanging items) with neighbours *wherever they are*. There is no single "reaction point."
- **Heterogeneous reaction** = people forming a *queue at a single ticket counter*. The rate at which tickets are issued depends on both **how fast people walk up** (diffusion) and **how fast the clerk processes each ticket** (surface reaction kinetics).

If people arrive at the counter faster than the clerk can serve them, a queue builds up — the system is **diffusion-limited**. If the clerk is slow, the counter is idle most of the time — the system is **reaction-limited**.

## 1.3 Simultaneous Heat and Mass Transfer — Condensation with Non-Condensable Gas

When a hot vapour (e.g., steam) condenses on a cold surface that is surrounded by a non-condensable gas (e.g., air), heat and mass transfer occur **simultaneously**. The vapour must diffuse through the stagnant (or slowly moving) non-condensable gas film to reach the cold surface, while the latent heat released is conducted back through the same film.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Condensation of a vapour on a cold surface in the presence of a non-condensable gas* is the coupled heat-and-mass-transfer process in which the mass flux of vapour to the condensate film is governed by **isothermal Fickian diffusion** through the gas film, and the heat flux carried by this vapour equals the latent heat of condensation. The presence of the non-condensable gas **drastically reduces** the condensation rate compared to pure-vapour condensation (Nusselt's analysis).

**Industrial examples:** steam condensers with air ingress, reflux condensers, dehumidifiers, nuclear reactor containment condensers, air-cooled condensers in power plants.

## 1.4 Steady-State Binary Thermal Diffusion in a Two-Bulb Apparatus

**Thermal diffusion** (the *Soret effect*) is the phenomenon in which a temperature gradient in a binary gas mixture induces a **concentration gradient**. A *two-bulb apparatus* (sometimes called a *two-chamber or two-vessel diffusion cell*) is the simplest experimental device used to measure the thermal diffusion factor $\alpha_T$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A *two-bulb thermal diffusion apparatus* consists of two bulbs (or chambers) maintained at temperatures $T_H$ (hot) and $T_C$ (cold), connected by a thin tube. At steady state, the diffusive flux due to the concentration gradient exactly balances the flux due to the temperature gradient, and the equilibrium composition difference between the two bulbs is used to back-calculate the thermal diffusion factor $\alpha_T$.

This is the **fundamental building block** of the Clusius–Dickel thermogravitational column, which achieves enormous separation factors by coupling thermal diffusion with natural convection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Reference Constants (Bold for Board Emphasis):**
> - Universal gas constant: **R = 8314 J/(kmol·K)**
> - Standard conditions: **T₀ = 298.15 K, P₀ = 101.325 kPa**
> - Soret effect was first observed by **Charles Soret in 1879**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Homogeneous Reaction in a Stagnant Film — First-Order Kinetics

Consider species A diffusing through a stagnant gas film of thickness $\delta$ and undergoing an irreversible first-order homogeneous reaction $A \rightarrow B$ throughout the film. The species balance on a differential element is:

$$\frac{d^2 C_A}{dx^2} - \frac{k}{D_{AB}} C_A = 0$$

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Mass balance on element:** (Flux in − Flux out) − Consumption = 0
2. **Insert Fick's law** for the flux: $N_A = -D_{AB} \, dC_A/dx$
3. **Add homogeneous reaction term** $-\,k C_A$ (consumption per unit volume per unit time)
4. **Boundary Conditions (BCs):**
   - At $x = 0$ (film–bulk gas interface): $C_A = C_{A,0}$
   - At $x = \delta$ (film–other boundary): impermeable wall, $N_A = 0 \Rightarrow dC_A/dx = 0$
5. **Solution:** $C_A(x) = C_{A,0} \dfrac{\cosh\!\left[\phi\left(1 - \tfrac{x}{\delta}\right)\right]}{\cosh(\phi)}$ where $\phi = \delta\sqrt{k/D_{AB}}$
6. **Molar flux at $x = 0$:** $N_{A,0} = \dfrac{D_{AB} C_{A,0}}{\delta} \, \phi \tanh(\phi)$

**Limiting cases:**
- $\phi \to 0$ (slow reaction): $N_{A,0} \to 0$ — diffusion is fast enough to keep $C_A$ uniform, so little net flux is needed.
- $\phi \to \infty$ (fast reaction): $N_{A,0} \to \dfrac{D_{AB} C_{A,0}}{\delta} \phi = k C_{A,0} \delta$ — reaction consumes A as fast as it can diffuse in.

## 2.2 Heterogeneous Reaction at a Surface

For a first-order heterogeneous reaction at a surface immersed in a fluid:

$$-D_{AB} \left.\frac{dC_A}{dx}\right|_{x=0} = k_s C_{A,s}$$

The **enhancement factor** $E$ compares the rate with reaction to pure physical mass transfer:

$$E = \frac{\text{Rate with reaction}}{\text{Rate without reaction}} = \frac{k_s C_{A,s}}{k_c (C_{A,0} - C_{A,s})} = \frac{\text{Da}_s}{1 + \text{Da}_s}$$

where $\text{Da}_s = k_s / k_c$ is the surface Damköhler number.

## 2.3 Diffusion and Reaction in Porous Catalyst Pellets

For a spherical porous catalyst pellet of radius $R$ with a first-order reaction:

$$\frac{1}{r^2} \frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r^2 D_{eff} \frac{dC_A}{dr}\right) - k C_A = 0$$

With BCs: $C_A = C_{A,s}$ at $r = R$ and $dC_A/dr = 0$ at $r = 0$.

Solution gives the internal effectiveness factor:

$$\eta = \frac{3}{\phi^2}\left[\phi \coth(\phi) - 1\right]$$

where $\phi = R \sqrt{k/D_{eff}}$ is the **Thiele modulus**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limiting Behaviour of $\eta$:**
> - $\phi < 0.5$ → $\eta \approx 1$ (reaction-limited; diffusion is fast).
> - $\phi > 3$ → $\eta \approx 3/\phi$ (diffusion-limited; reaction is fast).
> - Industrial catalysts are designed with $\phi \approx 1$–$3$ to balance activity and utilisation.

## 2.4 Condensation of Vapour in Presence of Non-Condensable Gas

### Physical Setup
- A cold vertical plate at temperature $T_w$ is exposed to a mixture of vapour A and non-condensable gas B.
- The bulk gas (far from the plate) is at $T_\infty$ and partial pressure $p_{A,\infty}$.
- Vapour A must diffuse through the boundary layer (thickness $\delta$) where B is essentially stagnant.
- The condensate film on the plate is thin; the dominant resistance is in the **gas-phase diffusion boundary layer**.

### Governing Equations

**(1) Mass transfer of A through stagnant B (Stefan diffusion):**

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T \, \delta} \ln\!\left(\frac{P - p_{A,w}}{P - p_{A,\infty}}\right) = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T \, \delta} \ln\!\left(\frac{p_{B,w}}{p_{B,\infty}}\right)$$

**(2) Energy balance at the condensate interface (latent heat of condensation = conduction through the gas film):**

$$N_A \, \lambda = h \, (T_\infty - T_w)$$

where $\lambda$ is the latent heat of condensation (J/kmol) and $h$ is the gas-film heat transfer coefficient.

**(3) Coupled solution — the interface temperature $T_w$ is unknown and must be solved iteratively or graphically.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The coupling arises because $N_A$ depends on $p_{A,w}$ (the partial pressure at the wall), and $p_{A,w}$ is fixed by the saturation condition at $T_w$: $p_{A,w} = p_{A,sat}(T_w)$. So $T_w$ and $N_A$ are **inseparable**.

### Linearised Approximation (Minkowycz–Sparrow / Labuntsov)

For small temperature differences, use the log-mean temperature driving force:

$$N_A = \frac{h}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{(T_\infty - T_w)}{\left(1 - \tfrac{p_{A,\infty}}{P}\right)_{\text{modified}}}$$

A common engineering shortcut is to define the **Ackermann correction factor**:

$$\zeta = \frac{h / (c_{p,m} N_A)}{e^{h / (c_{p,m} N_A)} - 1}$$

which multiplies the pure-vapour Nusselt result: $h_{\text{with NCG}} = \zeta \, h_{\text{pure vapour}}$.

## 2.5 Two-Bulb Thermal Diffusion Apparatus — Theory

### Setup
Two bulbs of volumes $V_1$ and $V_2$ (at $T_H$ and $T_C$ respectively) connected by a tube of length $L$ and cross-section $A$. The total moles $N$ of each species are conserved.

### Governing Flux Equations (1-D, no convection in tube)

The total flux of species 1 in a binary mixture with thermal and concentration gradients is:

$$J_1 = -\rho D_{12} \left[ \frac{d\omega_1}{dx} + \frac{\alpha_T \, \omega_1 \omega_2}{T} \frac{dT}{dx} \right]$$

where $\alpha_T$ is the **thermal diffusion factor** (dimensionless) and $\omega_i$ are mass fractions.

### Steady-State Condition

At steady state, $J_1 = 0$ throughout the connecting tube. Integrating over the tube length:

$$\int_{T_C}^{T_H} \frac{\alpha_T}{T} \, dT = -\int_{\omega_{1,C}}^{\omega_{1,H}} \frac{d\omega_1}{\omega_1 \omega_2}$$

If $\alpha_T$ is constant and using $\omega_2 = 1 - \omega_1$:

$$\alpha_T \ln\!\left(\frac{T_H}{T_C}\right) = \ln\!\left[\frac{\omega_{1,H} / (1 - \omega_{1,H})}{\omega_{1,C} / (1 - \omega_{1,C})}\right]$$

Equivalently, in terms of mole fractions $y$:

$$\alpha_T \ln\!\left(\frac{T_H}{T_C}\right) = \ln\!\left[\frac{y_{1,H}(1 - y_{1,C})}{y_{1,C}(1 - y_{1,H})}\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of the Two-Bulb Cell:**
> - Direct experimental measurement of $\alpha_T$ for gas pairs.
> - Used for isotope separation (e.g., $^{235}\text{U}/^{238}\text{U}$ enrichment research).
> - Provides input data for designing Clusius–Dickel thermogravitational columns.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — Print This for Revision!**

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Symbols / Units |
|---|---------|--------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Homogeneous 1st-order in film | $D_{AB} \dfrac{d^2 C_A}{dx^2} - k C_A = 0$ | $D_{AB}$ [m²/s], $k$ [1/s] |
| 2 | Damköhler number (homog.) | $\text{Da} = k L^2 / D_{AB}$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Film modulus | $\phi = \delta\sqrt{k/D_{AB}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Molar flux with homog. rxn | $N_{A,0} = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{\delta} C_{A,0}\, \phi \tanh\phi$ | [kmol/m²·s] |
| 5 | Surface rxn. condition | $-D_{AB}\left.\dfrac{dC_A}{dx}\right|_0 = k_s C_{A,s}$ | — |
| 6 | Enhancement factor | $E = \text{Da}_s / (1 + \text{Da}_s)$ | — |
| 7 | Thiele modulus (sphere) | $\phi = R \sqrt{k / D_{eff}}$ | — |
| 8 | Effectiveness factor | $\eta = \dfrac{3}{\phi^2}[\phi\coth\phi - 1]$ | — |
| 9 | Stefan (stagnant B) flux | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} P}{R T \delta}\ln\!\dfrac{p_{B,w}}{p_{B,\infty}}$ | [kmol/m²·s] |
| 10 | Latent-heat coupling | $N_A \, \lambda = h (T_\infty - T_w)$ | $\lambda$ [J/kmol] |
| 11 | Ackermann factor | $\zeta = \dfrac{x}{e^x - 1},\; x = h/(c_{p,m} N_A)$ | — |
| 12 | Two-bulb $\alpha_T$ | $\alpha_T \ln(T_H/T_C) = \ln\!\left[\dfrac{y_{1,H}(1 - y_{1,C})}{y_{1,C}(1 - y_{1,H})}\right]$ | — |
| 13 | Mass-flux (combined) | $J_1 = -\rho D_{12}\!\left[\nabla\omega_1 + \dfrac{\alpha_T \omega_1\omega_2}{T}\nabla T\right]$ | [kg/m²·s] |
| 14 | Soret coefficient | $s_T = \alpha_T / T$ | [1/K] |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units reminder:** Use **SI throughout KTU papers.** Mass fluxes in kmol/m²·s, $D_{AB}$ in m²/s, $R$ = 8314 J/(kmol·K), pressures in Pa (or kPa with consistent $R$).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Homogeneous First-Order Reaction in a Stagnant Gas Film

**Problem statement:** Species A diffuses through a stagnant gas film of thickness $\delta$. At $x = 0$, $C_A = C_{A,0}$. At $x = \delta$, the wall is impermeable. A first-order irreversible reaction $A \to B$ with rate constant $k$ occurs homogeneously in the film.

**Step 1 — Species mass balance on element $\Delta x$ at steady state:**

$$N_A\big|_{x} - N_A\big|_{x + \Delta x} - R_A \, \Delta x = 0$$

where $R_A = k C_A$ is the rate of consumption per unit volume.

**Step 2 — Divide by $\Delta x$ and take limit $\Delta x \to 0$:**

$$-\frac{dN_A}{dx} - k C_A = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{dN_A}{dx} = -k C_A$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Fick's law (1-D, stagnant film, no bulk flow for A):**

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

$$\therefore \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(-D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}\right) = -k C_A$$

Assuming $D_{AB} = $ constant:

$$D_{AB} \frac{d^2 C_A}{dx^2} = k C_A \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{d^2 C_A}{dx^2} - \frac{k}{D_{AB}} C_A = 0$$

**Step 4 — Define film modulus** $m^2 = k/D_{AB}$, so:

$$\frac{d^2 C_A}{dx^2} - m^2 C_A = 0$$

**Step 5 — General solution:**

$$C_A(x) = A_1 \cosh(mx) + A_2 \sinh(mx)$$

**Step 6 — Apply BCs:**

- (i) At $x = 0$: $C_A = C_{A,0}$ → $A_1 = C_{A,0}$
- (ii) At $x = \delta$: impermeable wall, $N_A = 0$ → $dC_A/dx = 0$

From BC (ii):

$$A_1 m \sinh(m\delta) + A_2 m \cosh(m\delta) = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad A_2 = -A_1 \tanh(m\delta)$$

**Step 7 — Final concentration profile:**

$$\boxed{\,C_A(x) = C_{A,0}\,\dfrac{\cosh\!\left[m(\delta - x)\right]}{\cosh(m\delta)}\,}$$

**Step 8 — Molar flux at $x = 0$:**

$$N_{A,0} = -D_{AB}\left.\frac{dC_A}{dx}\right|_{x=0} = -D_{AB}\, C_{A,0}\, m \cdot \frac{-\sinh(m\delta)}{\cosh(m\delta)}$$

$$\boxed{\,N_{A,0} = \frac{D_{AB} C_{A,0}}{\delta} \, (m\delta) \tanh(m\delta) = \frac{D_{AB} C_{A,0}}{\delta} \, \phi \tanh(\phi)\,}$$

where $\phi \equiv m\delta = \delta\sqrt{k/D_{AB}}$.

**Step 9 — Limiting cases (verify physical intuition):**

- $\phi \to 0$ (slow reaction, $k \to 0$): $\tanh\phi \to \phi$ and $N_{A,0} \to 0$. ✓
- $\phi \to \infty$ (fast reaction, $k \to \infty$): $\tanh\phi \to 1$ and $N_{A,0} \to D_{AB} C_{A,0}/\delta \cdot \phi = k C_{A,0} \delta$. ✓
- Pure diffusion (no reaction, $k = 0$): $N_{A,0} = 0$ because there is no sink to drive the flux through the impermeable wall. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation: Effectiveness Factor for a Spherical Porous Catalyst Pellet

**Problem statement:** First-order reaction $A \to B$ inside a porous sphere of radius $R$, surface concentration $C_{A,s}$ held constant.

**Step 1 — Shell balance** on a spherical shell of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$:

$$-\left. 4\pi r^2 N_A \right|_r + \left. 4\pi r^2 N_A \right|_{r+dr} - 4\pi r^2 \, dr \, k C_A = 0$$

**Step 2 — Steady state, Fick's law** $N_A = -D_{eff} \, dC_A/dr$:

$$\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r^2 \frac{dC_A}{dr}\right) - \frac{k}{D_{eff}} r^2 C_A = 0$$

**Step 3 — Define** $\phi^2 = k R^2 / D_{eff}$ and dimensionless radius $\xi = r/R$:

$$\frac{1}{\xi^2}\frac{d}{d\xi}\!\left(\xi^2 \frac{dC_A}{d\xi}\right) - \phi^2 C_A = 0$$

**Step 4 — Substitute** $C_A = u/\xi$:

$$\frac{d^2 u}{d\xi^2} - \phi^2 u = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad u = A_1 \cosh(\phi \xi) + A_2 \sinh(\phi \xi)$$

**Step 5 — BCs:**

- Centre symmetry: finite $C_A$ at $\xi = 0$ → $A_2 = 0$ (using $C_A \propto u/\xi$).
- Surface: $C_A(\xi = 1) = C_{A,s}$ → $A_1 = C_{A,s}/\cosh\phi$.

**Step 6 — Profile:**

$$C_A(\xi) = C_{A,s}\,\frac{\sinh(\phi \xi)}{\xi \,\sinh\phi}$$

**Step 7 — Flux at the surface:**

$$N_{A,s} = -D_{eff}\left.\frac{dC_A}{dr}\right|_{r=R} = \frac{D_{eff} C_{A,s}}{R}\left(\phi\coth\phi - 1\right)$$

**Step 8 — Effectiveness factor** (ratio of actual to surface-rate):

$$\eta = \frac{4\pi R^2 N_{A,s}}{(4/3)\pi R^3 \cdot k C_{A,s}} = \frac{3}{\phi^2}\left(\phi\coth\phi - 1\right)$$

$$\boxed{\,\eta = \frac{3}{\phi^2}\left(\phi\coth\phi - 1\right)\,}$$

**Verification:**

- $\phi \to 0$: $\coth\phi \approx 1/\phi + \phi/3 - \phi^3/45 \Rightarrow \eta \to 3/\phi^2 \cdot (\phi \cdot 1/\phi - 1 + \phi^2/3) \to 1$. ✓
- $\phi \to \infty$: $\coth\phi \to 1 \Rightarrow \eta \to 3/\phi^2(\phi - 1) \to 3/\phi$. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation: Thermal Diffusion Factor in a Two-Bulb Apparatus

**Step 1 — One-dimensional mass flux** of species 1 in a binary gas mixture under combined gradients:

$$J_1 = -\rho D_{12}\left[\frac{d\omega_1}{dx} + \frac{\alpha_T \omega_1 \omega_2}{T}\frac{dT}{dx}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Steady-state condition** in the connecting tube (no net mass flux at equilibrium):

$$J_1 = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{d\omega_1}{dx} = -\frac{\alpha_T \omega_1 \omega_2}{T}\frac{dT}{dx}$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate** from the cold bulb (C) to the hot bulb (H):

$$\int_{\omega_{1,C}}^{\omega_{1,H}} \frac{d\omega_1}{\omega_1(1 - \omega_1)} = -\alpha_T \int_{T_C}^{T_H} \frac{dT}{T}$$

Note: $\omega_2 = 1 - \omega_1$.

**Step 4 — Evaluate the LHS using partial fractions:**

$$\frac{1}{\omega_1(1 - \omega_1)} = \frac{1}{\omega_1} + \frac{1}{1 - \omega_1}$$

$$\int_{\omega_{1,C}}^{\omega_{1,H}} \!\left(\frac{1}{\omega_1} + \frac{1}{1 - \omega_1}\right) d\omega_1 = \ln\!\left[\frac{\omega_{1,H}(1 - \omega_{1,C})}{\omega_{1,C}(1 - \omega_{1,H})}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the RHS:**

$$-\alpha_T \int_{T_C}^{T_H} \frac{dT}{T} = -\alpha_T \ln\!\left(\frac{T_H}{T_C}\right) = \alpha_T \ln\!\left(\frac{T_C}{T_H}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Combine** (drop the negative sign by reversing the log argument):

$$\boxed{\,\alpha_T = \frac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{\omega_{1,H}(1 - \omega_{1,C})}{\omega_{1,C}(1 - \omega_{1,H})}\right]}{\ln(T_H / T_C)}\,}$$

In mole-fraction form (using $y_i = \omega_i / M_i \cdot \bar{M}$, valid for ideal gases where $\bar{M} = y_1 M_1 + y_2 M_2$), the same expression holds with $y$ replacing $\omega$ **only for the special case of equal molar masses**; otherwise the full derivation with $x_i$ (mole fractions) requires a different factor. For board purposes, the **mole-fraction form** is:

$$\boxed{\,\alpha_T = \frac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{y_{1,H}(1 - y_{1,C})}{y_{1,C}(1 - y_{1,H})}\right]}{\ln(T_H/T_C)}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention (frequently asked!):**
> - $\alpha_T > 0$ → heavier/larger species migrates to the **cold** bulb.
> - $\alpha_T < 0$ → heavier species migrates to the **hot** bulb.
> - The sign depends on the intermolecular potential and is generally a function of composition.

## 3.4 Derivation: Mass Flux for Condensation with Non-Condensable Gas (Stefan Diffusion + Energy Coupling)

**Step 1 — Stefan flow for A through stagnant B** (Fick's law with bulk-flow correction):

$$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB} P}{R T} \frac{dx_A}{dx} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - x_A}$$

where $x_A$ is the mole fraction of A and $P$ is the total pressure.

**Step 2 — Note that** $x_B = 1 - x_A$ and $dx_B = -dx_A$, so:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T} \frac{dx_B}{(1 - x_B) \, x_B} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad N_A \, dx = \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T} \frac{dx_B}{x_B}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate across the gas film** of thickness $\delta$ (assumed isothermal at $T_f$):

$$N_A \, \delta = \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T_f} \ln\!\left(\frac{x_{B,w}}{x_{B,\infty}}\right) = \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T_f} \ln\!\left(\frac{P - p_{A,w}}{P - p_{A,\infty}}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\,N_A = \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T_f \delta} \ln\!\left(\frac{P - p_{A,w}}{P - p_{A,\infty}}\right)\,}$$

**Step 4 — Couple with energy balance at the interface.** All the heat released by condensation is conducted back through the same gas film:

$$q = N_A \, \lambda = h_{gas} \, (T_\infty - T_w)$$

**Step 5 — Combine to eliminate $N_A$:**

$$\frac{D_{AB} P \lambda}{R T_f \delta} \ln\!\left(\frac{P - p_{A,w}}{P - p_{A,\infty}}\right) = h_{gas} (T_\infty - T_w)$$

This transcendental equation must be solved numerically for $T_w$ (and hence $N_A$) since $p_{A,w} = p_{A,sat}(T_w)$ is a strong non-linear function of $T_w$ (Antoine or similar equation).

**Step 6 — Linearised shortcut for small $\Delta T$.** Use the log-mean partial pressure driving force and approximate $T_f \approx (T_\infty + T_w)/2$. The final design equation used in KTU board problems is:

$$N_A = \frac{h_{gas}}{\lambda \cdot \left(\dfrac{P}{P - p_{A,m}}\right)_{\text{film mean}}} (T_\infty - T_w)$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Coupled Diffusion–Reaction Problem

The following Mermaid diagram shows the logical flow of how a homogeneous reaction–diffusion problem is structured, the BCs imposed, and the final outputs typically sought in a KTU exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Setup: Stagnant Film of thickness delta] --> B[Choose Differential Element of thickness dx]
    B --> C[Write Species Mass Balance at Steady State]
    C --> D[Insert Fick Law: N_A = -D_AB dC_A slash dx]
    D --> E[Add Homogeneous Reaction Sink: -k times C_A]
    E --> F[Obtain ODE: D_AB d2C_A by dx2 = k C_A]
    F --> G[Define Film Modulus phi = delta sqrt k by D_AB]
    G --> H[Apply BC 1: C_A at x=0 equals C_A0]
    H --> I[Apply BC 2: dC_A by dx at x=delta equals 0]
    I --> J[Solve ODE to get C_A of x]
    J --> K[Compute N_A0 from -D_AB times dC_A by dx at x=0]
    K --> L[Final Result: N_A0 equals D_AB C_A0 phi tanh phi divided by delta]
    L --> M{Check Limits}
    M --> N[phi to 0: No reaction, N_A0 to 0]
    M --> O[phi to infinity: Fast reaction, N_A0 to k C_A0 delta]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Two-Bulb Thermal Diffusion Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hot Bulb Volume V1 Temperature TH] -- Connecting Tube length L cross-section A --> B[Cold Bulb Volume V2 Temperature TC]
    A --> C[Initial uniform composition y1 initial]
    B --> C
    C --> D[Steady State: J1 equals 0 in tube]
    D --> E[Integrate over L: d omega1 by dx plus alpha_T omega1 omega2 by T times dT by dx equals 0]
    E --> F[Use omega2 equals 1 minus omega1 and partial fractions]
    F --> G[Final Equation: alpha_T ln TH by TC equals ln of ratio of mole fractions]
    G --> H[Solve for alpha_T from measured y1H and y1C]
    H --> I[Engineering Application: Design Clusius Dickel Column]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Condensation with Non-Condensable Gas — Coupled Heat–Mass Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hot Gas Mixture: Vapour A plus Non-condensable Gas B at T_infty] --> B[Gas Film Boundary Layer thickness delta]
    B --> C[Stefan Diffusion of A through stagnant B]
    C --> D[Molar flux N_A in terms of pA_w and pA_infty]
    B --> E[Conduction through same gas film]
    E --> F[Heat flux q equals h times T_infty minus T_w]
    D --> G[Latent Heat Released: q equals N_A times lambda]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Coupled Transcendental Equation in T_w]
    H --> I{pA_w equals pA_sat of T_w}
    I --> J[Solve iteratively or graphically for T_w]
    J --> K[Back-substitute to obtain N_A and h_with_NCG]
    K --> L[Result: Condensation rate is much lower than pure vapour Nusselt case]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix: Homogeneous vs Heterogeneous Reactions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reaction Type] --> B[Homogeneous]
    A --> C[Heterogeneous]
    B --> D[Location: Throughout bulk volume]
    B --> E[Governing ODE: D_AB Laplacian C_A minus k C_A equals 0]
    B --> F[Key Parameter: Damköhler number Da equals k L2 by D_AB]
    B --> G[Example: Combustion in gas phase]
    C --> H[Location: At surface or inside porous solid]
    C --> I[Governing Condition: Flux to surface equals surface rate -D_AB dC_A by dx at 0 equals k_s C_A_s]
    C --> J[Key Parameter: Thiele modulus phi equals R sqrt k by D_eff]
    C --> K[Example: Catalytic pellet in reactor]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
Differentiate between **homogeneous** and **heterogeneous** reactions with two examples each. Write the governing differential equation for species mass balance in each case.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Homogeneous | Heterogeneous |
|---------|-------------|---------------|
| Reaction site | Throughout the bulk of one phase | At the interface of two phases or within pores of a solid |
| Phase count | Single phase | Two (or more) phases |
| Governing equation | $D_{AB}\nabla^2 C_A - R_A(C_A) = 0$ | $-D_{AB}\!\left.\dfrac{dC_A}{dx}\right|_{x=0} = k_s C_{A,s}$ |
| Examples | Methane combustion in air; ozone decomposition | Catalytic cracking on zeolite; rusting of iron |
| Key dimensionless group | Damköhler number $\text{Da} = kL^2/D_{AB}$ | Thiele modulus $\phi = R\sqrt{k/D_{eff}}$ |

**Governing ODE (homogeneous, 1st order):**

$$D_{AB} \frac{d^2 C_A}{dx^2} - k C_A = 0$$

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
Define the **thermal diffusion factor** $\alpha_T$. With a neat sketch, explain the working of a two-bulb apparatus for its measurement.

**Model Answer:**

The **thermal diffusion factor** $\alpha_T$ is a dimensionless measure of the tendency of a temperature gradient to induce a concentration gradient in a binary gas mixture (the *Soret effect*).

$$\alpha_T = \frac{D_T}{D_{12}\,T} \cdot T = \frac{D_T}{D_{12}} \quad \text{(equivalent form)}$$

where $D_T$ is the thermal diffusion coefficient [m²/s] and $D_{12}$ is the mutual diffusion coefficient.

**Working of the two-bulb apparatus:**
1. Two glass or metal bulbs of volumes $V_1$ and $V_2$ are connected by a narrow tube.
2. Bulb 1 is maintained at high temperature $T_H$ (using an electrical heater); Bulb 2 is maintained at low temperature $T_C$ (using a cooling bath).
3. Initially both bulbs are filled with a uniform binary gas mixture of known composition $y_1^0$.
4. After sufficient time, steady state is reached: a concentration difference $\Delta y_1 = y_{1,H} - y_{1,C}$ is established.
5. The composition in each bulb is sampled and analysed (e.g., by gas chromatography or thermal conductivity).
6. $\alpha_T$ is then computed from:

$$\alpha_T = \frac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{y_{1,H}(1 - y_{1,C})}{y_{1,C}(1 - y_{1,H})}\right]}{\ln(T_H / T_C)}$$

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Module-internal choice. Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Derive the concentration profile of species A undergoing a first-order homogeneous reaction in a stagnant gas film of thickness $\delta$, with BCs $C_A(0) = C_{A,0}$ and $\left.\dfrac{dC_A}{dx}\right|_{x=\delta} = 0$. From this, obtain an expression for the molar flux at $x = 0$. Discuss the limiting cases $\phi \to 0$ and $\phi \to \infty$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gas mixture containing A and B flows over a catalytic surface where A undergoes a first-order heterogeneous reaction with $k_s = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s. The mass transfer coefficient in the gas phase is $k_c = 1.5 \times 10^{-2}$ m/s and the bulk concentration is $C_{A,0} = 0.05$ kmol/m³. Determine the surface concentration $C_{A,s}$, the rate of reaction per unit area, and the enhancement factor $E$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

The derivation is given in **Section 3.1** above. For the model answer, write the key steps:

1. Mass balance: $\dfrac{dN_A}{dx} = -k C_A$
2. Fick's law substitution → $D_{AB}\dfrac{d^2 C_A}{dx^2} = k C_A$
3. Define $m^2 = k/D_{AB}$, general solution $C_A = A_1\cosh(mx) + A_2\sinh(mx)$
4. Apply BCs: $A_1 = C_{A,0}$ and $A_2 = -C_{A,0}\tanh(m\delta)$
5. **Concentration profile:**

$$C_A(x) = C_{A,0}\dfrac{\cosh\!\left[m(\delta - x)\right]}{\cosh(m\delta)}$$

6. **Molar flux at $x = 0$:**

$$N_{A,0} = \frac{D_{AB} C_{A,0}}{\delta}\,\phi \tanh\phi,\quad \phi = \delta\sqrt{k/D_{AB}}$$

7. **Limits:** $\phi \to 0$: $N_{A,0} \to 0$ (no driving force since no reaction); $\phi \to \infty$: $N_{A,0} \to k C_{A,0}\delta$ (reaction-limited, all A consumed in the film).

**Model Solution to (b):**

Step 1 — At the surface, equate diffusive flux to surface reaction rate (1st order):

$$k_c(C_{A,0} - C_{A,s}) = k_s C_{A,s}$$

Step 2 — Solve for $C_{A,s}$:

$$k_c C_{A,0} = (k_c + k_s) C_{A,s} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad C_{A,s} = \frac{k_c}{k_c + k_s} C_{A,0}$$

$$C_{A,s} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-2}}{1.5 \times 10^{-2} + 5 \times 10^{-3}} \times 0.05 = \frac{1.5}{2.0} \times 0.05 = 0.75 \times 0.05$$

$$\boxed{C_{A,s} = 0.0375~\text{kmol/m}^3}$$

Step 3 — Rate of reaction per unit area:

$$R'' = k_s C_{A,s} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.0375 = 1.875 \times 10^{-4}~\text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$

Step 4 — Enhancement factor:

$$E = \frac{\text{Rate with reaction}}{\text{Rate without reaction}} = \frac{k_s C_{A,s}}{k_c C_{A,0}} = \frac{1.875 \times 10^{-4}}{1.5 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.05} = \frac{1.875 \times 10^{-4}}{7.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.25$$

Equivalently, $E = \text{Da}_s / (1 + \text{Da}_s)$ where $\text{Da}_s = k_s/k_c = 1/3$:
$$E = \frac{1/3}{1 + 1/3} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 \;\checkmark$$

Note: $E < 1$ here because the surface reaction is **slower** than mass transfer — the system is **reaction-limited**, not diffusion-limited. (Enhancement factors $>1$ arise only when reaction is faster than diffusion.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** A two-bulb thermal diffusion apparatus has a hot bulb at $T_H = 600$ K and a cold bulb at $T_C = 300$ K, connected by a tube. At steady state, the mole fraction of species 1 (heavier) in the hot bulb is $y_{1,H} = 0.45$ and in the cold bulb is $y_{1,C} = 0.55$. Calculate the thermal diffusion factor $\alpha_T$ and comment on the direction of migration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Steam at atmospheric pressure and $T_\infty = 373$ K containing 5 mol% of air (non-condensable) condenses on a cold vertical plate at $T_w = 333$ K. The gas film is 2 mm thick, with $D_{AB} = 2.6 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $\lambda = 2.26 \times 10^7$ J/kmol, and the mean film temperature $T_f = 353$ K. Estimate the condensation mass flux. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

Step 1 — Use the two-bulb formula:

$$\alpha_T = \frac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{y_{1,H}(1 - y_{1,C})}{y_{1,C}(1 - y_{1,H})}\right]}{\ln(T_H/T_C)}$$

Step 2 — Compute the concentration ratio:

$$\frac{y_{1,H}(1 - y_{1,C})}{y_{1,C}(1 - y_{1,H})} = \frac{0.45 \times 0.45}{0.55 \times 0.55} = \frac{0.2025}{0.3025} = 0.6694$$

Step 3 — Compute the temperature ratio:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{T_H}{T_C}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{600}{300}\right) = \ln(2) = 0.6931$$

Step 4 — Final $\alpha_T$:

$$\alpha_T = \frac{\ln(0.6694)}{0.6931} = \frac{-0.4014}{0.6931} = -0.579$$

$$\boxed{\alpha_T \approx -0.58}$$

Step 5 — **Comment:** Since $\alpha_T < 0$, the **heavier species (species 1) migrates toward the hot bulb**. This is consistent with the observation that $y_{1,H} = 0.45 < y_{1,C} = 0.55$ — there is *less* of the heavier species in the cold bulb because it has moved to the hot one. (The negative sign in the formula arises from the sign convention: positive $\alpha_T$ → heavy species goes cold.)

**Model Solution to (b):**

Step 1 — Identify partial pressures (ideal gas, $P = 101.325$ kPa):

- $p_{A,\infty}$ (vapour) = $0.95 \times 101.325 = 96.26$ kPa
- $p_{A,w}$ (vapour at wall) = $p_{sat}(T_w = 333~\text{K}) \approx 19.92$ kPa (from steam tables)
- $p_{B,\infty}$ (air) = $0.05 \times 101.325 = 5.066$ kPa
- $p_{B,w}$ (air at wall) = $P - p_{A,w} = 101.325 - 19.92 = 81.40$ kPa

Step 2 — Stefan diffusion flux:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T_f \delta}\ln\!\left(\frac{p_{B,w}}{p_{B,\infty}}\right)$$

Step 3 — Compute the coefficient:

$$\frac{D_{AB} P}{R T_f \delta} = \frac{2.6 \times 10^{-5} \times 101325}{8314 \times 353 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}$$

Numerator = $2.6 \times 10^{-5} \times 101325 = 2.6345$ (units: m²/s × Pa)

Denominator = $8314 \times 353 \times 0.002 = 5869.7$

Coefficient = $2.6345 / 5869.7 = 4.489 \times 10^{-4}$ kmol/(m²·s) [per unit log]

Step 4 — Log ratio:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{81.40}{5.066}\right) = \ln(16.07) = 2.776$$

Step 5 — Final flux:

$$\boxed{N_A = 4.489 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.776 = 1.246 \times 10^{-3}~\text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}}$$

This is significantly **less** than the pure-vapour Nusselt flux would be — illustrating the strong retarding effect of the non-condensable gas.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\ln$** in Stefan diffusion (using linear difference instead) — costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $p_A$ and $p_B$** in the log argument. The correct form is $\ln(p_{B,w}/p_{B,\infty})$, *not* $\ln(p_{A,w}/p_{A,\infty})$.
> 3. **Using bulk temperature** $T_\infty$ instead of **film-mean temperature** $T_f = (T_\infty + T_w)/2$ in the $RT$ term.
> 4. **Omitting the sign of $\alpha_T$** discussion in two-bulb problems — always state the direction of migration.
> 5. **Forgetting BCs in derivation questions** — write them explicitly before solving the ODE; partial marks are given for BCs even if the algebra is incomplete.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 4 portion):**

- [ ] **Homogeneous 1st order in film:** ODE is $D_{AB} C_A'' - k C_A = 0$; solution involves $\cosh$, $\sinh$; flux $= (D_{AB} C_{A,0}/\delta)\phi\tanh\phi$.
- [ ] **Damköhler number** $\text{Da} = kL^2/D_{AB}$ — large Da = reaction-controlled; small Da = diffusion-controlled.
- [ ] **Heterogeneous at surface:** BC: $-D_{AB} dC_A/dx \vert_0 = k_s C_{A,s}$; enhancement $E = \text{Da}_s/(1+\text{Da}_s)$.
- [ ] **Thiele modulus** $\phi = R\sqrt{k/D_{eff}}$ (sphere); $\eta = (3/\phi^2)(\phi\coth\phi - 1)$.
- [ ] **Limiting $\eta$:** $\phi \to 0 \Rightarrow \eta \to 1$; $\phi \to \infty \Rightarrow \eta \to 3/\phi$.
- [ ] **Stefan diffusion with non-condensable gas:** $N_A = (D_{AB}P/RT\delta)\ln[(P-p_{A,w})/(P-p_{A,\infty})]$.
- [ ] **Coupling with energy:** $N_A \lambda = h(T_\infty - T_w)$; $p_{A,w} = p_{A,sat}(T_w)$.
- [ ] **Ackermann factor** $\zeta = x/(e^x-1)$ reduces condensation rate with NCG.
- [ ] **Two-bulb $\alpha_T$ formula** in **mole-fraction form** (the version you should quote in the exam):

$$\alpha_T = \frac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{y_{1,H}(1 - y_{1,C})}{y_{1,C}(1 - y_{1,H})}\right]}{\ln(T_H/T_C)}$$

- [ ] **Sign of $\alpha_T$:** positive → heavy species goes to **cold**; negative → heavy species goes to **hot**.
- [ ] **Clusius–Dickel column** amplifies two-bulb separation by orders of magnitude using thermal convection — sometimes asked as a short note.
- [ ] **Units checklist:** $D_{AB}$ [m²/s], $k$ [1/s], $k_s$ [m/s], $C_A$ [kmol/m³], $N_A$ [kmol/(m²·s)], $R = 8314$ J/(kmol·K).
- [ ] **Always show BCs** in derivation questions; mark allocations in KTU explicitly reward this.
